places.name: Istanbul

visits.text: Jour 1: Enter Hagia Sophia, the 6th-century architectural wonder that defined Byzantine grandeur for a millennium. Its massive dome-55 meters high and 30 meters wide-remained the world's largest until St. Peter's in Rome, while Byzantine mosaics share space with Islamic calligraphy.

Jour 1: Explore Topkapi Palace, the Ottoman sultans' residence for 400 years overlooking the Bosphorus and Golden Horn. This UNESCO-listed complex spans 70 hectares with four courtyards, the imperial harem, and treasury displaying the Spoonmaker's Diamond.

Jour 1: Wander through the Topkapi Harem, where up to 300 women lived in a labyrinth of 400 rooms. The apartments of the Sultan's mother (Valide Sultan) and the crown prince showcase the finest Ottoman tilework and carved fountains.

Jour 1: Descend into the Basilica Cistern, an underground cathedral of 336 marble columns built by Justinian to supply the Byzantine palace. Two columns rest on carved Medusa heads-one sideways, one inverted-their origins still debated.

Jour 1: Marvel at the Blue Mosque (Sultan Ahmed Mosque), the only imperial mosque with six minarets. Over 20,000 blue Iznik tiles line the interior, while 260 windows fill the vast prayer hall with light.
Jour 1: Study the Blue Mosque's architecture rivaling Hagia Sophia across the plaza. Sultan Ahmed I, who commissioned it at age 19, is buried in a mausoleum by the mosque's northwest corner.

Jour 2: Lose yourself in the Grand Bazaar, the world's largest covered market with over 4,000 shops along 60 covered streets. Since the 15th century, merchants have traded carpets, spices, jewelry, and ceramics under its painted vaults-the final marketplace of the Silk Road.

Jour 2: Visit the Süleymaniye Mosque, architect Sinan's 16th-century masterpiece built for Suleiman the Magnificent. Four minarets with ten balconies encode the sultan's place in Ottoman history-fourth sultan of Istanbul, tenth of the dynasty.

Jour 2: Explore the Süleymaniye complex including the tombs of Suleiman and his powerful wife Roxelana, plus madrasas, a hospital, and a caravanserai. The terrace offers sweeping views over the Golden Horn.

Jour 2: Discover the Church of the Holy Saviour in Chora (Kariye Mosque), a Byzantine gem whose 14th-century mosaics and frescoes depicting the life of Christ rank among the finest surviving examples of late Byzantine art.

Jour 2: Tour Dolmabahçe Palace on the Bosphorus, where Ottoman opulence meets European grandeur. Crystal chandeliers from Bohemia, a 4.5-ton Baccarat masterpiece in the throne room, and 14 tons of gold leaf mark the empire's final royal residence.
Jour 2: Visit the Dolmabahçe Harem, separate from the main palace, where the sultan's family lived amid European furniture, Hereke carpets, and views over the Bosphorus. Atatürk died here in 1938.

Jour 3: Explore the Spice Bazaar (Egyptian Bazaar), Istanbul's second-largest covered market specializing in spices, dried fruits, Turkish delight, and teas. The L-shaped 17th-century structure near Galata Bridge remains a sensory feast.

Jour 3: Visit the Rüstem Pasha Mosque near the Spice Bazaar, a small Sinan-designed gem featuring the finest Iznik tiles in Istanbul. Crimson tulips, carnations, and geometric patterns cover every surface.

Jour 3: Discover the Santral Istanbul Energy Museum in the converted Silahtarağa power plant-the Ottoman Empire's first thermal power station. Interactive exhibits trace Istanbul's industrial heritage in this unexpected cultural venue.

Jour 3: Cruise the Bosphorus, the strait dividing Europe and Asia that has shaped Istanbul's destiny for millennia. Ottoman wooden mansions (yalıs), Byzantine fortresses, and the graceful profiles of mosques line both shores.

Hagia Sophia is the logical starting point for any Istanbul visit. Built in the 6th century under Emperor Justinian, it served as the world's largest cathedral for nine centuries, became a mosque after 1453, a museum in 1935, and returned to mosque status in 2020. Inside, the 31-meter central dome appears to float above a forest of marble columns, while Byzantine mosaics share space with Ottoman calligraphy. Arrive at opening time to beat the security lines, which can exceed an hour during peak season. Women must cover their heads - complimentary scarves are available at the entrance.

The Blue Mosque (Sultan Ahmed, 1616) rises across from Hagia Sophia, recognizable by its six minarets - a record that scandalized the sultan's contemporaries. The interior is lined with over 20,000 İznik tiles in blue-and-white floral patterns that bathe the prayer hall in cool, calming light. Entry is free; respect prayer times (the mosque closes to visitors five times daily). Plan to cover shoulders and knees, and remove your shoes.

Descend beneath the streets of Sultanahmet: the Basilica Cistern (Yerebatan Sarnıcı) is a 6th-century underground chamber supported by 336 marble columns bathed in amber light. At the far end, two columns rest on carved Medusa head bases - one positioned sideways, the other upside down. According to legend, this orientation neutralized the creature's petrifying gaze. Buy tickets online to skip the queue.

The Hippodrome of Constantinople was the civic and sporting heart of Byzantium, capable of holding 100,000 spectators. Only a few monuments remain: the Obelisk of Theodosius (imported from Egypt in 390 AD, in perfect condition), the Serpent Column (brought from Delphi), and the German Fountain gifted by Wilhelm II in 1901. The square makes a pleasant break between visits.

Topkapı Palace served as the administrative and residential heart of the Ottoman Empire for nearly 400 years. This isn't a palace in the European sense - it's a sprawling complex of pavilions, courtyards, and gardens extending over 70 hectares overlooking the Bosphorus. Don't miss the Treasury (86-carat diamond, Suleiman the Magnificent's sword) and the Harem if you're curious about Ottoman court intrigue. The palace is closed on Tuesdays. Allow at least 3 hours.

The Süleymaniye Mosque dominates Istanbul from its hilltop perch, a masterwork by architect Mimar Sinan commissioned by Suleiman the Magnificent in 1550. Less crowded than the Blue Mosque, experts consider it a masterpiece of proportion. The adjacent cemetery holds the tombs of Suleiman and his wife Roxelana. The terrace offers one of the finest views over the Golden Horn.

The Spice Bazaar (Mısır Çarşısı), built in 1663 near the New Mosque, is an L-shaped covered gallery where bags of sumac, Turkish tea packets, Turkish delight, and dried figs jostle for space. It's considerably more compact than the Grand Bazaar - allow 30 to 45 minutes to explore. Shops near the Eminönü entrance offer the most reasonable prices.

Galata Tower (Galata Kulesi) was erected in 1348 by the Genoese at the highest point of their merchant quarter. From the summit (paid access), the panorama encompasses the Golden Horn, Sultanahmet with its minarets, the Bosphorus, and the Asian hills. It's currently partially closed for renovation - check status before visiting. If closed, the nearby Anemon restaurant has a terrace with identical views open to the public.

Istiklal Avenue is Beyoğlu's pedestrian artery, stretching 1.5 km between Galata Tower and Taksim Square. From independent bookshops to art-house cinemas, 19th-century covered passages to contemporary galleries - this is secular, cosmopolitan Istanbul revealing itself. The historic red tram (nostaljik tramvay) runs the length several times daily, often crowded but photogenic.

Return to Istanbul by the Safranbolu → Istanbul route (420 km, about 4.5 hours via highway). This final leg crosses the wooded hills of northern Anatolia before descending toward the Sea of Marmara and the first silhouettes of Istanbul's minarets. Arrival in the afternoon. The 25-day loop is complete.

activities.text: Jour 1: Relax at Cağaloğlu Hammam, an 18th-century Ottoman bathhouse where marble halls beneath star-pierced domes have hosted royalty and celebrities for three centuries. The men's and women's sections operate separately.

Jour 2: Cross to the Asian shore to see Haydarpaşa Station, a German neo-Renaissance terminus from 1908. This grand railway station-currently under restoration-marked the start of the Baghdad Railway, the modern heir to the ancient Silk Road.

Jour 3: Watch sunset from Galata Bridge, where fishermen cast lines and restaurants serve fresh catches from the Golden Horn. The silhouettes of minarets against the evening sky complete the Silk Road journey at the gateway between East and West.

End your day in Sultanahmet with a çay (Turkish black tea) on one of the terraces facing Hagia Sophia. The sight of illuminated minarets at sunset, with the call to prayer echoing simultaneously from multiple mosques, creates an experience that's hard to forget.

Wander through the Ottoman alleyways between the Hippodrome and the Sea of Marmara - the Arasta Bazaar neighborhood, just behind the Blue Mosque, shelters artisan shops in a covered 17th-century gallery. Less crowded than the Grand Bazaar, it offers a glimpse of what Ottoman markets must have been like.

Get lost in the 4,000 shops of the Grand Bazaar (Kapalıçarşı), one of the world's oldest covered markets, operating since the 15th century. Each street specializes: gold jewelry, carpets, ceramics, leather. The golden rule: if a price interests you, negotiate - it's expected. The bazaar opens at 8:30 AM and closes at 7 PM, closed Sundays.

Eat a grilled fish sandwich (balık ekmek) on the Galata Bridge, steps from the Spice Bazaar. Street vendors cook fresh mackerel on their boats moored along the Golden Horn. It's Istanbul's most popular lunch and costs just a handful of lira.

The public Bosphorus ferry cruise (Şehir Hatları) is the best way to see Istanbul from the water. The short ferry (2 hours round trip) departs Eminönü in early afternoon and passes Dolmabahçe Palace, Rumeli Fortress, Ottoman wooden mansions (yalı), and Ortaköy Mosque. Sit on the left side (European shore) for the best views on the outbound journey.

Explore Karaköy and the Galata neighborhood on foot in late afternoon: third-wave coffee shops, art galleries, vinyl and used bookstores pack the alleys around the tower. This is where Istanbul's alternative cultural scene concentrates, far removed from Sultanahmet's tourist atmosphere.

Closing evening on the Bosphorus: take a public ferry from Eminönü to see Istanbul one last time from the water. The 20-minute journey to Üsküdar and back costs a few lira and offers the same view as the Day 3 cruise - but with the feeling of having traveled all across Turkey between the two.

visits.text: Deep blue against forests of pines, beeches, and oaks—Lake Abant at 1,328 meters altitude is one of northern Turkey's most celebrated lake landscapes. Formed by ancient tectonic processes, it's fed by springs. In winter, it's snow-covered and the road from Bolu is sometimes closed.

activities.text: Rent a pedal boat and tour the lake from the main pier—or walk the peripheral trail (about 8 km, 2 hours of easy walking). Lakeside hotels offer breakfast with your feet in the grass. Leave early morning before weekend groups arrive.

places.name: Adiyaman

visits.text: Mount Nemrut peaks at 2,134 m in the eastern Taurus range. On its summit, King Antiochus I of Commagene (69-34 BC) built his tomb-sanctuary surrounded by colossal statues 8 to 10 meters tall representing syncretic Greco-Perso-Armenian deities: Zeus-Oromasdes, Apollo-Mithra-Helios-Hermes, Heracles-Artagnes-Ares, and himself. The heads, toppled by earthquakes, measure roughly 2 m and have gazed at the horizon for two thousand years. The tumulus of crushed stone covering the summit's center (50 m high) is believed to house Antiochus's tomb - no one has found it yet. UNESCO site since 1987.

The east terrace is the meeting point for sunrise: five seated statues face the rising sun, with lions and eagles guarding the flanks. The west terrace, slightly different due to earthquakes, offers the most photographed views at sunset. On the west terrace you'll also find a relief showing Antiochus shaking hands with a god - the act of dexiosis, symbol of alliance - and an astronomical relief depicting a lion with 19 stars and three planets, dated by some scholars to July 7, 62 BC, possibly the day of Antiochus's coronation.

activities.text: Sunrise at Nemrut is an experience unto itself. Tour buses arrive in the middle of the night from Kahta or Malatya to reach the summit before dawn. In June-July, it's already between 5 and 10°C at the top even in midsummer: bring a jacket. If you come in April or October, expect sweaters, hat, and gloves. The walk from the parking lot to the terraces is about 500 m (300 steps).

On the return to Kahta, stop at the Cendere Bridge, a single-arch Roman bridge built in the 2nd century AD in honor of Emperor Septimius Severus. It spans the Kahta Çayı at 6 m height. Originally four columns marked its entrances - three still stand, the fourth having been toppled for political reasons in antiquity.

visits.text: Afyon's black rock pierces the city's rooftops at 226 meters high: Karahisar Kalesi (literally 'black fortress') is this volcanic rock that Hittites, Phrygians, and Byzantines successively fortified. The ascent counts roughly 650 steps carved into the rock. At the top, four large cisterns and traces of Cybele worship—and a view that justifies the effort.

Forty pine columns support Afyon's Ulu Camii, built in 1272 by the Seljuks and now UNESCO-listed (2023) alongside those of Sivrihisar and Beyşehir. The marble mihrab and capitals sculpted without nails or mortar impress carpenters as much as historians. Right across from the foot of the rock, easy to combine.

days_plan.itin_title: Central Anatolia: Phrygia, Konya, and the Caravanserais

visits.text: Its Latin name means 'village between two streams'—and that's exactly what Ağva is: wedged between the Göksu and Yeşilçay, both emptying into the Black Sea. Until the 1950s, the village shipped charcoal from Istanbul's forests. Today, tourist boats have replaced charcoal barges.

The ochre cliffs of Kilimli drop straight into the Black Sea at the end of a short walk from the village. The panorama over both rivers and the coast is excellent—plan for sunset here. It's also a diving spot for the daring who jump from the rocks.

activities.text: Rent a boat on the Göksu before noon—local captains offer hour-long tours among the reeds and willows. It's the most local way to see Ağva. In the evening, dine on fresh fish on the Yeşilçay side—that's where the most reputed seafood restaurants are.

Until the 1950s, Ağva shipped charcoal to Istanbul from its forests. Canoes and pedal boats are available on both rivers to explore the reed-lined waterways at your own pace. Excursions to Hacıllı Waterfall (12 km into the forest) are done by local minibus.

days_plan.itin_title: Black Sea & Safranbolu: 9 days village to village

visits.text: Listed on the UNESCO tentative list in 2013, Akçakoca's Genoese Castle (Ceneviz Kalesi) dominates the sea from its promontory. Of Roman and Byzantine origin, it was remodeled by the Genoese in the 13th century, then taken by the Ottomans in 1323. Behind it, coastal caves known as 'seal reefs' open at low tide.

The blue of Genoese Bay contrasts with the brown of the castle perched above. Kale Beach, at the foot of the fortress itself, has earned Blue Flag status. On either side, caves along the coast lend themselves to coastal caving. Avoid July-August if you're seeking tranquility.

activities.text: Head down to the fishing harbor at sunset—boats are coming in, fish restaurants come alive. Local cuisine relies on hazelnuts and chestnut honey from the Düzce region. Akçakoca is a transition stop before the wild coast toward Amasra—take advantage to eat well and simply.

days_plan.itin_title: Black Sea & Safranbolu: 9 days village to village

visits.text: Built between 915 and 921, the Church of the Holy Cross at Akdamar is the work of architect Manuel for Armenian King Gagik I Artsruni. Hripsimé-type cross plan, 15 meters by 12. The exterior friezes in red tuff tell in bas-relief Adam and Eve, Jonah and the whale, David and Goliath, Daniel among the lions.

The red tuff of the façade glows in the midday sun: quarried from distant sites, the stone seems to radiate. Inside, faded but legible frescoes, biblical scenes on the four absidioles. The church is open for Armenian worship once a year since 2010; the rest of the time it's a museum.

activities.text: Buy your ticket at the Gevaş pier, 35 km southwest of Van. The crossing takes 20 minutes; the boat waits for you to return (often only 1 hour on the island—negotiate 2 hours if possible). Round-trip fare is around 200 TL in 2025. Sunglasses and water mandatory.

days_plan.itin_title: Eastern Anatolia in 13 days: from the Caucasus to Ararat

visits.text: The Eğri Minare has leaned for 800 years without warning: built around 1236 in red brick, this Seljuk leaning minaret is nicknamed Aksaray's Tower of Pisa. Whether the tilt is deliberate or due to uneven foundations—specialists don't settle it. Accessible in 5 minutes on foot from the center, no entry fee, easy photos from any angle.

The Karamanoğlu Ulu Camii (1408) closes the architectural loop of the journey: Seljuk rectangular plans, carved stone portal, and minaret added in 1925. Less spectacular than those of Konya or Afyon, but its tree-lined courtyard invites a mid-afternoon pause. The neighboring Aksaray museum displays local Neolithic pieces if you have 30 minutes.

days_plan.itin_title: Central Anatolia: Phrygia, Konya, and the Caravanserais

visits.text: Nasreddin Hoca's türbe sums up the character well: four sides open, only one side locked with a huge padlock. The death date on the sarcophagus is engraved upside down—a deliberate touch of humor according to tradition. The sage-philosopher from Sivrihisar, who died in Akşehir in 1284, is one of the most widely disseminated comic and moral figures of the Turkic-speaking world. UNESCO designated 1996 as 'Nasreddin Hoca Year.'

days_plan.itin_title: Central Anatolia: Phrygia, Konya, and the Caravanserais

visits.text: The Hittites didn't demolish Alacahöyük when they settled there around 1850 BC—they built over a royal Hattian necropolis. The site covers about 7 hectares, excavated since 1907. Less spectacular than Hattusa but far denser in relief sculpture visible in situ. Allow 1½ hours on-site.

Before the Sphinx Gate of Alacahöyük, two stone protomes flank the southern entrance. The relief orthostats (ritual procession, sacrifices, jugglers) are replicas; the originals have joined the Museum of Anatolian Civilizations in Ankara. Current dating: 13th century BC.

activities.text: After the visit, head to the small museum of Alacahöyük, right next to the site. Bronze sun discs from the royal tombs, Chalcolithic ceramics, gold jewelry from the Bronze Age. An hour is enough. If you're heading back toward Amasya, the road passes through Çorum, where the regional museum intelligently completes the picture.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Central Turkey: 7 Days Ankara to Kayseri

visits.text: Sea on both sides—that's your first impression arriving in Amasra by the winding road. The town stretches on an isthmus with a small harbor to the west and a large sandy harbor to the east. Founded in the 12th century BC under the name Sesamos, then renamed after the Persian princess Amastris. The castle connects two sections—Sormagir on Boztepe island and Zindan on the peninsula—via the Kemere Bridge.

The ramparts reach 8 meters thick in places, reinforced by Roman, Byzantine, Genoese, and Ottoman bastions. The Kemere Bridge linking the two parts of the castle dates from Roman times and was restored in the 9th century. Climb the ramparts late in the day for the light over both harbors.

Amasra Museum, opened in 1982, presents collections spanning Hellenistic, Roman, Byzantine, Genoese, and Ottoman periods. Small and honest. It's the logical complement to the ramparts—objects found on site are properly displayed. Allow 30 to 40 minutes.

activities.text: Stroll the old harbor on the west side late afternoon—fishing boats return, terraces come alive, the citadel rises above the houses. Amasra has preserved its charm precisely because it's remained off the major routes. Less concrete than elsewhere on the coast.

If you arrive late in the day, the sand beach of the large harbor (east side) is the ideal place to end the stop. The water is calmer here than on the open coast. The beach remains accessible in the evening when the crowds disperse.

places.name: Amasya

visits.text: The road between Rize and Amasya is this itinerary's transition leg: about 290 km and 6 hours of driving, leaving the coast to cross into interior Anatolia. Leave early. Midway, the city of Samsun on the Black Sea offers a lunch break before descending toward the Yeşilırmak valley. Amasya appears late afternoon: Ottoman wooden houses line both riverbanks, with cliffs and rock-cut tombs looming above. First view from the central bridge.

The Yalıboyu houses - the riverside houses - are Amasya's most photographed buildings. These Ottoman residences from the 18th and 19th centuries, built on stone foundations with wooden overhangs above the Yeşilırmak, have mostly been restored as hotels and cafés. The promenade along the south bank takes 20 minutes and allows views of all the colorful facades. In the evening, the rock tombs are illuminated and reflect in the water.

The rock tombs of the Pontic kings are carved into the limestone of Mount Harşena, at a height of 8 to 15 meters above ground. The five principal tombs belonged to kings who ruled Amaseia between 281 and 155 BC: Mithridates I, Ariobarzanes, Mithridates II, Mithridates III, and Pharnaces I. Impossible to enter the burial chambers - the openings are too high - but the access path offers a plunging view over the city and the Yeşilırmak. Allow 20 minutes' climb from the ticket office.

The Amasya Archaeological Museum is one of the region's most interesting - and not just for museum regulars. The centerpiece is a hall devoted to 14th-century Ilkhanid mummies: bodies naturally preserved without organ extraction, discovered beneath the Burmalı Minare mosque. The museum also houses a bronze statuette of the Hittite storm god Teshub, found locally. Information panels in English.

The Sultan Beyazıt II Mosque (1486) is Amasya's largest religious complex: mosque, medrese, fountain, and imaret (charitable kitchen) form a cohesive ensemble on the south bank. The architecture - sober, well-proportioned, without the decorative excesses of certain imperial mosques - illustrates the transition between Seljuk and classical Ottoman styles.

Amasya's citadel perches atop Mount Harşena, 272 meters above the city. The first fortifications date to the Bronze Age, and the citadel was modified under the Pontic Kingdom, Byzantines, and Ottomans. A 186-meter tunnel carved through rock connects different parts of the enceinte. The climb on foot from the tombs takes an additional 30 minutes but the view from the summit surpasses everything you've seen from the lower viewpoints.

activities.text: Late arrival obliges, settle this evening for a riverside stroll and dinner in one of the restaurants set in old Ottoman houses. The nighttime view of the illuminated tombs from the bank is worth the journey's fatigue.

Amasya has produced apples since antiquity and made them its symbol. The orchards surrounding the city in autumn (September-October) give rise to very lively farmers' markets in the center. If you're there outside apple season, local groceries sell jams, apple vinegar, and artisanal juice.
Return to see the tombs at nightfall: the spotlights that illuminate them create a mirror effect on the river below. This is when Amasya is most beautiful - and least crowded.

visits.text: Ani strikes you with its silence: an entire Armenian capital, abandoned on a plateau above the Arpaçay River. At its peak around the year 1000, it may have held 100,000 inhabitants. Mongols, earthquakes, and shifting trade routes emptied it from 1236 onward. Inscribed as a UNESCO site in 2016.

The Church of St. Gregory of Tigran Honents, dated 1215 by the Armenian inscription on its south façade, preserves the site's best-kept frescoes. Lives of Christ and St. Gregory the Illuminator in still-vivid colors. You're now 50 meters from the closed border with Armenia.

Ani Cathedral, completed around 1001 by architect Trdat (the same one who worked on Hagia Sophia after the 989 earthquake), measured 38 m high. The dome has collapsed, but the elevation of the three naves remains impressive. Converted to Fethiye Mosque under the Seljuks after 1064.

activities.text: Plan 3 to 4 hours on site: the grounds span about 5 to 6 km on foot between major points. No refreshment stand inside—water and a hat mandatory in summer. Entry around 90 TL in 2025. Parking is at the entrance to the Seljuk ramparts on the west side.

days_plan.itin_title: Eastern Anatolia in 13 days: from the Caucasus to Ararat

visits.text: Founded in 1921 at Atatürk's initiative, the Museum of Anatolian Civilizations occupies a former Ottoman bedesten and the Kurşunlu Han at the foot of the castle. Voted European Museum of the Year in 1997. Plan 2½ hours to walk through the Neolithic, Hittite, Phrygian, and Urartian periods in chronological order. Closed Mondays in winter.

On Rasattepe Hill, Anıtkabir stretches across several hectares of pale stone. Built between 1944 and 1953 by architects Onat and Arda, it serves as the mausoleum of Atatürk and, incidentally, İsmet İnönü. Free admission, strict security screening. Avoid November 10th, the day of national crowds.

Ankara Castle, a Byzantine citadel later rebuilt, crowns the Atpazarı quarter. The black stone ramparts tower over the entire old town and offer panoramic views to the Anatolian horizon. Walk up through artisan alleys, about 30 minutes from the museum. Free access.

activities.text: Head up to the citadel at sunset, then descend through the alleys of Hamamönü, the restored Ottoman quarter. Half-timbered and plaster houses, artisan workshops, tea salons set up in old courtyards. In the evening, several traditional restaurants serve Ankara köfte, spicier than elsewhere.

The Hamamönü quarter is worth a detour for quiet evenings and cafés in inner courtyards. Skip the souvenir shops lined up on the main street, head into the side alleys where craftsmen still work with copper and wood. Lively until late in summer, more subdued in winter.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Central Turkey: 7 Days Ankara to Kayseri
Central Anatolia: Phrygia, Konya, and the Caravanserais

visits.text: Hadrian's Gate is the only ancient passage still standing from Antalya's ramparts. It was erected in 130 AD on the occasion of Emperor Hadrian's visit by the local governor Quintus Veranius Nepos. Its three white marble arches, flanked by two towers from different periods (the Roman tower to the south, the Seljuk tower to the north rebuilt in the 13th century), constitute a two-thousand-year layering of history in a single monument. Entering Kaleiçi through this gate is the best possible start for exploring the quarter.

Kaleiçi is a tangle of cobblestone streets mixing overhanging Ottoman houses, boutique hotels, Roman ruins, and mosques. Hıdırlık Tower (2nd century AD, 14 m), at the southwestern corner of the ramparts, overlooked the old harbor. Recent excavations have uncovered 100 meters of a columned Roman road linking the tower to Hadrian's Gate - an underground street visible from glass walkways being installed. The old Roman harbor, now a pleasure marina, is 5 minutes' walk.

The Yivli Minare ('fluted minaret' in Turkish) was built around 1226-1227 under Seljuk Sultan Alaeddin Keykubad I. Its silhouette - eight engaged brick columns spiraling up 38 meters - is Antalya's symbol. It's on Turkey's UNESCO World Heritage preliminary list. The adjoining mosque is open to visitors outside prayer times.

Antalya Museum is regularly cited as one of the world's ten best archaeological museums. Its collection of Roman statues - largely from the Perge excavations - is stunning. The gods hall displays deity statues (Hadrian, Artemis, Apollo, Tyche of Perge) of extraordinary technical refinement and preservation. The sarcophagus hall exhibits 2nd-century funerary chests whose bas-reliefs narrate the labors of Heracles. Allow at least two hours.

The Lower Düden Falls are one of the few places in the world where a river plunges from a cliff directly into the sea. The Düden River descends from the Taurus Mountains and dives from a 40-meter precipice into the Mediterranean. The view from the cliff is vertiginous. From the sea, excursion boats pass within meters of the waterfall - the spray and roar are striking. You can also descend by stairway to the base of the cliff.

Perge's stadium is the best-preserved Roman stadium in Turkey. Built in the 2nd century AD, it could accommodate about 12,000 spectators. The arched tiers are still largely standing, with their interior vaults that served as commercial galleries - one of history's first 'under the stands.' Climb to the top of the tribune to see the track axis along with the ramparts on the horizon.

The two Hellenistic defensive towers (3rd century BC) form the heart of Perge's ramparts. In Roman times, their niches were filled with commemorative statues, several of which are now displayed in Antalya Museum. The colonnaded avenue traversing the city from north to south (500 m) was lined with shops, monumental fountains, and a central canal. Ruts worn by carts into the stone are still visible. Perge was one of Pamphylia's great cities: Saint Paul preached here in 46 AD during his first missionary journey.

Perge's southern baths (1st-2nd centuries AD) are among Turkey's most extensive. Under the still-standing arches, you can distinguish the structure of different rooms (frigidarium, tepidarium, caldarium). Fragments of mosaics and marble cladding survive. Statues of exceptional quality - including the Venus of Perge and Artemis of Perge - were found here and are displayed in Antalya Museum.

activities.text: Antalya's old Roman harbor is now a marina surrounded by cafés and restaurants. Terraces look directly onto the boats and ramparts. It's the ideal place for the penultimate evening of the trip, over fresh fish meze and a glass of rakı.

This cylindrical Roman tower from the 2nd century, 14 meters tall, stands at the southwestern tip of the ramparts in Karaalioğlu Park. It probably served as a lighthouse and watchtower. Sunset from this promontory, with the Gulf of Antalya and Taurus Mountains in the background, is one of the city's best.

Small boats depart from the base of the cliff to approach as close as possible to the falls. You'll pass under the spray, very often beneath a rainbow. It's a brief but memorable experience, different from all the other sites on the journey.
