visits.text: Ayazini is Cappadocia without the buses. This village in Ihsaniye harbors a Byzantine church carved straight into the tuff, with a vault on six columns and a deeply carved baptistery—one of 25 rock-cut churches recorded in the area. The village houses themselves are partly carved into the rock. Arrive early; the few inhabitants know every chamber.

Carved into the tuff, Avdalaz Kalesi stacks entire floors of interconnected chambers through tunnels, with a large cistern at the summit and Romano-Byzantine tombs at the base. The sculpted Medusa head at the entrance lintel alone is worth the climb. Unmarked site, prefer visiting before 10am for the best light.

days_plan.itin_title: Central Anatolia: Phrygia, Konya, and the Caravanserais

visits.text: Ayder Plateau smells of damp fog and wet wood. Timber chalets, green meadows, thermal springs. The village has turned touristy, sometimes excessively, but remains the best base for exploring Kaçkar Mountains National Park. Gelintülü Waterfall is a 10-minute walk.

Fırtına Valley ranks among the WWF's 200 ecological regions to preserve. The eponymous river tumbles between dense forests and Ottoman arched bridges, notably the one at Şenyuva. Zilkale Castle, perched at 1,300 meters, is worth the 25-km detour from Ayder. Narrow but paved road.

activities.text: Ayder's thermal baths are functional, not glamorous. Sulfurous water at 56°C, communal pool or private cabins. Modest fee, family atmosphere. More interesting than beautiful; you go for the heat after a day in the rain. Avoid weekends.

days_plan.itin_title: Turkish Black Sea Road Trip: 8 Days from Istanbul to Hopa

places.name: Ayval K

visits.text: Built in 1844, the Taksiyarhis is the symbol of Greek presence in Ayvalık. Unlike most surrounding churches converted into mosques, it functions as a secular museum. After the Ottoman victory in 1922, the Greek population left the town overnight as part of the population exchange under the Treaty of Lausanne. The church served as an olive oil factory in the 1940s before being abandoned—its restoration and reopening in 2018 uncovered part of the original mosaic flooring.

More than 1,000 historic buildings have been counted in the old town. Neoclassical houses built by Greek olive oil merchants in the late 19th century feature local stone walls, carved window frames, and coloured shutters. Unlike Alaçatı, many lanes remain residential—you'll encounter more locals than shops. Look for the Macaron quarter for the best-preserved alleys.

Cunda is linked to Ayvalık by a bridge built in 1964—Turkey's first island-mainland bridge—and by ferries from the harbour. Its real name is Moschonisi, 'the perfumed island', but everyone calls it Cunda. Cobbled streets, Greek stone houses, and fish restaurant terraces. The Taksiyarhis Rahmi M. Koç Museum, in a restored Orthodox church, displays a collection of vintage cars, motorcycles, and toys—one of the finest collections of its kind in Turkey.

The Devil's Table is a rocky promontory overlooking the Ayvalık archipelago of 22 islands and coastal olive groves. Legend says the devil, cast out of paradise, left the imprint of his foot in the rock. Clear views of Lesbos, Cunda, and the olive-covered hills. Arrive 30 minutes before sunset to secure a spot—it's the town's most sought-after photo location.

activities.text: Ayvalık is surrounded by 2.5 million olive trees, some several centuries old. The region is considered by many professionals as one of the world's finest extra-virgin oil production zones. Tour operators offer mill visits with guided tastings. Plan to bring back a few bottles: the best local oils are found in the mills, not supermarkets.

Local operators offer island-hopping tours among the 22 islands of the archipelago. Most are uninhabited. Hidden coves between islets offer turquoise waters for swimming. The morning half-day departure allows you to combine the boat tour with an afternoon visit to Cunda.

The weekly market gathers local producers and artisans. Marinated olives, fig jams (the local eşme), artisanal olive oil soaps, and regional cheeses. Allow an hour for the full circuit and taste the products offered for sampling.

days_plan.itin_title: Turkish Aegean Coast: Gallipoli, Troy, Ephesus & Bodrum in 12 Days

map_keywords: Ayvalik old town Greek houses Turkey

visits.text: The village of Kapıkırı is built amid the ruins of a Hellenistic city founded in the 3rd century BC. Columns and foundations emerge between current residents' houses: column shafts serve as garden tables. The 6.5-kilometre ramparts—still partly standing at 6 metres high and reinforced by 65 towers—are an exceptional example of Hellenistic fortification. Look for the Sanctuary of Endymion, carved into rock beside the lake: legend says the moon goddess Selene fell in love with this sleeping shepherd and asked Zeus to grant him eternal youth.

This 60-square-kilometre lake was 2,000 years ago a gulf of the Aegean Sea. Alluvium deposited by the Maeander river progressively filled the maritime entrance, transforming the gulf into an inland lake. This natural phenomenon explains why ancient port cities like Miletus and Heraclea now find themselves tens of kilometres from the sea. The lake water is slightly salty, a legacy of its marine past.

In the 7th century, monks from Sinai settled in caves and on Lake Bafa's islets, transforming Mount Latmos into a holy mountain comparable to Athos. By the end of the Byzantine era, thirteen monasteries and churches had been built in the labyrinth of rocks. The most famous is the Monastery of the Seven Brothers (Yediler), accessible on foot from the village with a local guide. Local boats offer crossings to the islets where monastery ruins stand—with swimming possible in the slightly salty waters.

activities.text: Local fishermen offer crossings to the lake's monastic islets. On the Twin Islands (Ikizce Adaları), the Byzantine walls of a former cloister still stand. The lake water is clear and slightly salty—ideal for swimming. Negotiate duration with the boatman on arrival.

The Carian Trail traverses Heraclea over 800 kilometres along the Carian coast. The section around Lake Bafa winds between rock formations, prehistoric rock paintings, and monastery ruins. Take a local guide from the village—the landscapes are magnificent but signage is insufficient to navigate alone.

days_plan.itin_title: Turkish Aegean Coast: Gallipoli, Troy, Ephesus & Bodrum in 12 Days

visits.text: The inhabitants of Ballıhisar literally live on the ruins of Pessinus, the ancient sacred city of the goddess Cybele according to ancient sources. In 204 BC, the Romans exported the site's sacred black stone to Rome to protect the Empire against Carthage—only the foundations of the tiered theater remain, the colonnaded street, and inscriptions. A strangely quiet site, with no ticket booth or excessive signage.

days_plan.itin_title: Central Anatolia: Phrygia, Konya, and the Caravanserais

visits.text: Not on the tourist circuits, but these Art Nouveau and Baroque façades grafted onto two-story timber structures are unique to Bartın. Local houses (with their 'daraba' fences) reflect regional woodcraft on their pediments. Ancient city mentioned in the Iliad under the name Kromna.

The Kemer Bridge, built in 1872, measures 42 meters and spans the Bartın River in the city center. It marks the boundary between the old town and modern quarters. Go down to the banks to see both sides from below—the city reads better from there.

activities.text: Bartın's old market is worth an hour wandering among stalls of local products. The region is known for its honey and hazelnuts. More authentic than coastal tourist bazaars—locals do their shopping here. Look for chestnut honey vendors in the alleys behind the main mosque.

days_plan.itin_title: Black Sea & Safranbolu: 9 days village to village

visits.text: Batman surprises: an oil town, flat, with no obvious charm, it's mainly a practical stop before Diyarbakır. Born in the 1940s with the discovery of oil, it only urbanized in the 20th century. Its archaeology museum deserves an hour for the pieces rescued from the Tigris valley before the Hasankeyf flood.

Dating from 1146-1147, the Malabadi Bridge spans the Batman Su with a single stone arch nearly 40 meters wide, one of the widest in the medieval world. Built by the Artuqids, it still serves as an arch for pedestrians. It's 30 km east of the city, on the road to Silvan, and can be visited for free.

activities.text: Eat a yoğurtlu leben, Batman-style yogurt soup thickened with fine bulgur and flavored with dried mint. Several lokantas in the center serve it at lunch, about 70 TL per bowl. It's the regional dish less known than Antep's beyran, but just as characteristic. Served with fresh flatbread.

days_plan.itin_title: Turkish Mesopotamia: 11 Days from Gaziantep to the Tigris

places.name: Behramkale

visits.text: Climb all the way up—235 metres of elevation from the village, allow 20 minutes on foot. At the summit, reconstructed andesite column shafts give an idea of the temple built around 530-520 BC. It's the only known archaic Doric temple in Asia Minor. Aristotle came here to teach in 348 BC, invited by King Hermias, married his niece, and left for Lesbos three years later. The island you see 15 kilometres to the southwest is precisely Lesbos. The original friezes—Heracles against the centaurs—are scattered between the Louvre, Istanbul, and Boston.

The fortified wall measures 3.1 kilometres long and reaches 12 metres high in places—one of the best-preserved defensive complexes from Greek antiquity in Turkey. The walls were raised in several phases starting in the 4th century BC and reinforced by 8 towers. The west gate, flanked by two large Hellenistic towers, still stands. The site has been on the UNESCO tentative list since 2017.

Between the acropolis and the harbour, successive terraces reveal the agora with its two porticoes, the bouleuterion (council chamber), and the theatre carved into the southern slope—orientation chosen to offer spectators the view across to Lesbos. The Roman baths are partially excavated. Excavations continue each summer under the direction of Çanakkale University.

Walk down to the harbour: it was already active in Greek times and functioned continuously until the 18th century, exporting valonia oak bark from local trees to the European leather industry. Today a protected zone, all new construction is banned. The Apostle Paul embarked here during his third missionary journey, mentioned in the Acts of the Apostles.

activities.text: The Ottoman village of Behramkale, built after the Turkish conquest in the 14th century, developed on the slope opposite the ancient city. Grey andesite houses, cobbled lanes, carved lintels. Olive oil soap shops cluster around the central square. Arrive early or late afternoon—between 11 a.m. and 4 p.m. in summer, tour buses arrive.

Return to the Temple of Athena late in the day. The sun sets behind Lesbos and bathes the andesite columns in orange light. It's one of the most striking panoramas on the entire Aegean coast—and often quieter than the midday visit. Bring an extra layer: the wind picks up in the evening on the acropolis.

Three kilometres from the village, Kadırga beach flies the Blue Flag. The water is clear and shallow, in a cove protected from northern winds. Ideal for a midday swim between the morning acropolis visit and the harbour in late afternoon.

days_plan.itin_title: Turkish Aegean Coast: Gallipoli, Troy, Ephesus & Bodrum in 12 Days

map_keywords: Temple of Athena Assos Behramkale Turkey

visits.text: Founded in the 3rd century BC as capital of the Attalid dynasty, Pergamon is a UNESCO site since 2014. The site dominates the Bakırçay plain from a 335-metre-high hill. A cable car connects the lower town to the acropolis—take it up and walk down. At the top, the Temple of Trajan (2nd century AD) is the only Roman building on the acropolis. Below, the Altar of Zeus, whose foundations measure 36 × 33 metres, was one of the masterpieces of the Hellenistic era: its 113-metre-long Gigantomachy frieze is now in Berlin's Pergamon Museum (closed for renovation until 2027).

Built in the Hellenistic period under Eumenes II, this 10,000-seat theatre is considered the steepest in antiquity—its 70° angle gives it a dizzying profile. From the top of the 78 rows of seats, the view plunges to the plain and mountains. The stage was wooden, dismantled after each performance so as not to obstruct the permanent valley view.

Founded in the 4th century BC around a sacred spring, the Asclepieion was antiquity's second medical centre after Epidaurus. Treatments included mud baths, dream-analysis psychotherapy, medicinal herbs, and theatre—yes, there was a 3,500-seat theatre for convalescing patients. Galen, born in Pergamon in 129 AD, studied here at sixteen and became court physician to the emperor. Recent analyses showed the sacred spring was slightly radioactive. Walk through the 70-metre underground tunnel that linked the dormitories to the healing temple.

In the heart of modern Bergama, this massive red-brick basilica was built in the 2nd century AD for the cult of the Egyptian gods Serapis and Isis. It's one of the largest Roman religious structures in Asia Minor. One of its two lateral towers now houses a mosque—a mosque inside a former Roman temple tower, itself built on a Greek sanctuary. Pergamon's layers are everywhere.

activities.text: The cable car links the lower town to the acropolis in a few minutes, with panoramic views of the tumuli (royal tombs) scattered across the plain. Ride up by cable car, descend on foot via the Antik Yol (ancient path) to enjoy the landscape and encounter less-visited ruins.

The town museum holds more than 8,700 archaeological objects and 7,300 coins from local excavations. A good introduction to Pergamon's chronology before or after the site. The most important pieces were taken to Berlin in the 19th century, but recent discoveries remain here.

days_plan.itin_title: Turkish Aegean Coast: Gallipoli, Troy, Ephesus & Bodrum in 12 Days
Turkey End to End: The Complete 25-Day Grand Tour

visits.text: The 42 cedar columns of Eşrefoğlu Camii were immersed 6 months in Lake Beyşehir before being installed—to harden them, tradition says. Built in 1299, this Seljuk mosque was just UNESCO-listed in 2023: it's the largest example of a wooden-column mosque from the Anatolian principalities period. The walnut minbar in kündekari is a rare mastery. Cover your shoulders at entry.

Lake Beyşehir stretches at your feet, Turkey's third-largest lake, with surprisingly transparent water. The northwest shore welcomes anglers in early morning and heron nests in the reeds. For a break, the city's terraces look directly onto the water.

days_plan.itin_title: Central Anatolia: Phrygia, Konya, and the Caravanserais

visits.text: The black stone of Ahlat colors all of Bitlis. This city at 1,545 m altitude is wedged in a gorge; two rivers cross it. The castle overlooks the town from a spur. According to local legend, it was built by an Alexander general named Badlis in 312 BC—information unverifiable but persistent.

Şerefiye Mosque, dated 1529 by inscription, is a rare complex: mosque, madrasa, soup kitchen, and founder's tomb for Emir Şeref. The muqarnas portal evokes Seljuk style even though the Ottomans already dominated the region. Madrasa roof accessible for a panoramic view.

activities.text: Eat büryan kebabı, lamb cooked in a pit for several hours then served on flatbread. It's Bitlis's specialty, to taste at lunchtime only (pits are lit in the morning and emptied around 2 p.m.). Expect 250 to 350 TL for a portion, served with raw onions and fresh tomatoes.

days_plan.itin_title: Eastern Anatolia in 13 days: from the Caucasus to Ararat

visits.text: The Knights of Saint John began building this castle in 1402, reusing stones from the Mausoleum of Halicarnassus—one of the Seven Wonders of the ancient world—for the foundations. Look closely at the walls: you'll see ancient marble and reliefs embedded in the medieval masonry. The castle was ceded to the Ottomans in 1523, converted into a mosque, then into a prison in 1895. Since 1964, it has housed the world's largest underwater archaeology museum. The Uluburun shipwreck hall (14th century BC) is fascinating: the wreck carried copper from Cyprus, tin from Central Asia, Egyptian glass, Anatolian cereals, and a gold scarab bearing Nefertiti's name. Bronze Age globalisation, reconstructed from the seabed.

Today only an archaeological site with excavated foundations and a few scattered marble blocks remains—but the place's history justifies the visit. The Mausoleum of Halicarnassus (around 350 BC) was the monumental tomb of Mausolus, satrap of Caria, and his sister-wife Artemisia II. Fifty metres high, decorated with 400 sculptures and topped with a quadriga, it gave its name to all the world's monumental tombs (mausoleum). Most of the friezes were taken to the British Museum by Charles Newton in 1856.

Gümüşlük is the most authentic village on the Bodrum peninsula. It's built on the ruins of ancient Myndos, a 4th-century BC Carian city. Part of the ancient city is submerged in the bay—the underwater walls are still visible at shallow depth. The place is protected as an archaeological zone: no new construction, no large-capacity hotels. Result: an almost intact fishing-village atmosphere, waterside fish restaurants, and some of the best seafood dining on the entire Turkish coast.

A submerged stone causeway—the ancient walls of Myndos—links Gümüşlük beach to Rabbit Island. At low tide, the water doesn't exceed waist height. On the islet, wild rabbits (which gave it its name) coexist with the ruins of a Hellenistic tower. Archaeological excavations are occasionally active on the site, which may be partially closed some seasons. Bring water shoes: the submerged stones are slippery.

Ten kilometres from Gümüşlük, Turgutreis is the westernmost point of the Bodrum peninsula. Its sunsets over the Greek islands—Kos, Kalymnos, Leros on the horizon—are renowned along the entire coast. The town is newer and less picturesque than Gümüşlük, but the Saturday market is one of the peninsula's liveliest: local producers, fabrics, spices, and handicrafts.

Bodrum's Tuesday market is one of the region's liveliest: vegetables, spices, marinated olives, textiles, pottery. Ideal for buying last souvenirs before the airport. Arrive early (before 10 a.m.) to avoid crowds and find the best products.

activities.text: Bodrum's waterfront stretches between the two bays framing the castle. The marina gathers gulets (typical Turkish wooden boats) and luxury yachts. In the evening, the kordon terraces come alive. This is also where you'll find the town's best fresh fish restaurants—fishermen unload their catch directly at the harbour market in the morning.

Tour operators offer traditional hammams in Bodrum's old town. Scrub, massage, and steam bath: a good way to recover after several days walking archaeological sites. Book in advance—evening slots fill quickly in high season.

Gümüşlük's fish restaurants line their tables directly on the water, some on floating pontoons. Octopus drying on lines, the day's catch displayed on chalkboards, your gaze resting on Rabbit Island—it's the image of a simple, delicious coastal Turkey. Arrive around 7:30 p.m. to catch the sunset from your table.

The Bodrum peninsula can be covered in a day via the coastal road: Bodrum → Gümüşlük (west) → Turgutreis (tip) → return via Yalikavak (north) or through the villages of Gündoğan and Türkbükü. The road runs along coves, bays, and olive groves. Avoid the main road crossing the interior in summer—coastal roads are slower but far more beautiful.

The beach in central Bodrum, between the castle and the marina, has a protected boat-free zone. Quieter beaches can be found at Bitez (10 min) or Ortakent (15 min). Enjoy this last morning before heading to Milas-Bodrum airport (BJV), 35 kilometres from the centre.

Milas-Bodrum airport is 35 kilometres from Bodrum centre, about 40 to 50 minutes by car depending on traffic. Regular shuttles operate from the central bus station. Allow 2.5 hours before departure in high season—security checks can be lengthy.

days_plan.itin_title: Turkish Aegean Coast: Gallipoli, Troy, Ephesus & Bodrum in 12 Days
Turkish Aegean Coast: Gallipoli, Troy, Ephesus & Bodrum in 12 Days
Turkish Aegean Coast: Gallipoli, Troy, Ephesus & Bodrum in 12 Days

visits.text: Hattusa was the capital of the Hittite Empire for over four centuries, from approximately 1650 to 1178 BC. Inscribed on UNESCO's World Heritage list since 1986, the site covers several square kilometers and should be visited by car - the ruins are too spread out to do everything on foot. Begin with the lower city: the Great Temple dedicated to the Storm God and the Sun Goddess of Arinna, with its storerooms for cuneiform tablets and cyclopean blocks. More than 30,000 cuneiform tablets were discovered here, including a copy of the peace treaty of Kadesh between Hattusili III and Ramesses II.

The Lion Gate closes the southwestern entrance to the upper city. Two limestone lions - protruding heads, open muzzles - have guarded the passage since the 13th century BC. Right next door, the Yerkapı (Earth Gate) is pierced by a 70-meter tunnel under an artificial rampart. This corbelled tunnel - a rare technique in Anatolia at that time - probably served during religious rituals or defensive sorties. Climb onto the rampart from outside to see the two sphinxes that flanked the entrance on the steppe side (copies - the originals are in Berlin and Istanbul).

The King's Gate (Kral Kapı), to the southeast, is decorated with a relief depicting a divine warrior - probably the Storm God himself. The original is in Ankara's Museum of Anatolian Civilizations; here, a faithful copy stands in place. The upper city rampart runs for 6 km and hints at the city's extent at its peak.

Yazılıkaya, 2 km northeast of Hattusa, is an open-air rock sanctuary carved into two natural limestone chambers. The walls are covered with 63 reliefs of Hittite deities in procession - the largest ensemble of this type known. In chamber A, the Storm God Teshub faces the Sun Goddess Hepat, both standing on deified mountains. In chamber B, narrower, King Tudhaliya IV is depicted in the arms of the god Sharruma. The quality of the reliefs, carved around 1250 BC, rivals contemporary Egyptian bas-reliefs.

activities.text: Hire a local guide in Boğazkale before visiting the site - the on-site panels are useful but insufficient for understanding Hittite cosmology and the function of the different gates. Guides speaking French are rare but English speakers are good. Allow 3 to 4 hours for Hattusa + Yazılıkaya.

After the visit, the small village of Boğazkale offers a few simple guesthouses and a handful of restaurants serving central Anatolian dishes. Enjoy the evening calm - most visitors leave the same day for Ankara or Çorum.

days_plan.itin_title: Trabzon to Istanbul: Black Sea, Hittites, and Safranbolu in 10 Days
Turkey End to End: The Complete 25-Day Grand Tour
Secret Central Turkey: 7 Days Ankara to Kayseri

places.name: Bolu

visits.text: Before leaving Safranbolu, a morning visit to the saffron fields is possible in October - the only month when the Crocus sativus crocuses are in bloom. Stigma harvesting is done by hand, at dawn, before the heat weakens the filaments. About 30 farmers still cultivate Safranbolu saffron (European geographical indication saffron). Outside October, drying rooms and artisan cooperatives remain visitable on request.

Lake Abant, 34 km southwest of Bolu, is a natural lake at 1,310 meters altitude formed by a landslide in a cradle of pine and fir forests. A 7 km trail circles the entire lake - walkable in two hours, without significant elevation. The lake harbors an endemic trout subspecies, the Abant Alası (Salmo trutta abanticus), listed on the red list of vulnerable species.

activities.text: The Safranbolu → Bolu road descends westward through the forests of the Karabük region before joining the D-100 / TEM highway. Lake Abant is a 20 km detour from Bolu - don't miss it if the weather's fine. In autumn, the beech forests around the lake take on orange and red tones worth the trip alone.

Bolu is known in Turkey for producing many great cooks - the Mengen region has been renowned for its chefs since Ottoman times. In the evening, look for a restaurant serving Mengen Pilavı or a local kebap: Bolu cuisine is simple, generous, and little known to foreign tourists.

days_plan.itin_title: Trabzon to Istanbul: Black Sea, Hittites, and Safranbolu in 10 Days
Black Sea & Safranbolu: 9 days village to village

map_keywords: Lake Abant Bolu nature park forest
Abant lake walking trail pine forest

visits.text: Surprising for such a modest town: Burdur Museum houses the colossal head of Hadrian recovered at Sagalassos by Marc Waelkens, one of the finest known representations of the emperor. The dancer statues from the Sagalassos Heroon are also exhibited here, better preserved than the on-site copies. Allow 1.5 hours; closed Mondays.

İnsuyu Cave, 13 km east of Burdur, was the first cave opened to tourism in Turkey. Stalactites, stalagmites, a small underground lake. Guided tour around 30 minutes; stairs are damp and slippery. Water level varies with rainfall, sometimes closed during droughts. Check before you go.

activities.text: Go birdwatching at Lake Burdur, a Ramsar site since 1994. The lake has lost over a third of its water in 50 years; current surface area roughly 150 km². The white-headed duck (Oxyura leucocephala), globally threatened, nests here. Bring binoculars; observation best in autumn-winter.

days_plan.itin_title: 7-Day Taurus: Turquoise Lakes & Lycian Cities

places.name: Canakkale

visits.text: Bring a guide or at minimum a solid reference book—without context, Troy's stones remain mute. The site is organised around nine layers of civilisation stacked over 4,000 years, from 3000 BC to late Roman times. Layer Troy VIIa, destroyed by fire around 1180 BC, is the one archaeologists link to Homer's Trojan War. In 2024-2025, excavations uncovered thousands of sling stones and arrowheads concentrated in front of the palace: evidence of a violent assault, not gradual decline. Enter from the right and follow the marked circuit—layers Troy III-V offer the best view of the strait in the distance.

Opened in 2018, this museum 500 metres from the site is one of the best introductions to Troy's excavation. More than 2,000 objects cover all periods of occupation. The originals of 'Priam's Treasure' discovered by Schliemann are in Moscow's Pushkin Museum and Berlin's Neues Museum—the replicas displayed here help you understand what the archaeologist actually found. Visit the museum before the site: the stratigraphy is better explained here.

Cross the Dardanelles by ferry from Çanakkale—the crossing takes 20 minutes. On the other side, the fortress built by Mehmed II in 1452 just before the fall of Constantinople literally locks the passage: Kilit-ül-Bahr means 'lock of the sea'. Suleiman the Magnificent added a seven-storey tower in 1541. Climb to the top for a full view of the strait—and spot Çimenlik Castle on the opposite shore, which closed the passage on the Asian side.

The promenade displays cannons, anchors, and models of warships—an open-air museum of the Dardanelles battle. Look for the Ottoman Clock Tower, built in 1897 with funds from an Italian consul. Nearby, a replica of the Trojan horse made for the 2004 film dominates the quay. It's kitsch, it's the obligatory photo stop, and it reminds you that the Troy myth still feeds popular imagination.

activities.text: The crossing between Çanakkale (Asian coast) and Kilitbahir (European coast) takes 20 minutes, but allow 45 minutes including the wait. Position yourself on the outer deck: the parade of tankers through one of the world's busiest straits reminds you that these waters have been decisive since antiquity.

The Mirror Bazaar dates from the 1890s. Its name comes from the mirrors that once adorned the upper galleries, used during wedding preparations. Lively covered alleys: Ottoman ceramics, olive oil soaps, evil-eye jewellery. A good place to buy a few souvenirs without tourist traps.

Tour operators offer guided visits in French or English departing from Çanakkale or directly on-site. A guide radically transforms the experience: without one, the numbered strata Troy I to Troy IX remain abstract. Book in advance during high season (June-August).

days_plan.itin_title: Turkish Aegean Coast: Gallipoli, Troy, Ephesus & Bodrum in 12 Days

map_keywords: Archaeological Site of Troy Canakkale Turkey

visits.text: Çatalhöyük rests beneath two mounds, the largest reaching 20 meters high: 18 occupation levels between 7400 and 6200 BC, meaning 1,200 years of continuous sedentary life. This Neolithic site, UNESCO-listed in 2012, is among the world's most important: no streets, houses stacked back-to-back with entry through the roof. One in ten visitors here realizes they've revised their idea of prehistory.

Then cross into the reconstructed experimental house: roof-terrace accessible by ladder, adobe walls, central hearth, burials beneath the floors. The new visitor center opened in 2023 clearly explains the excavations with interactive displays. Free entry, closes at 5pm.

days_plan.itin_title: Central Anatolia: Phrygia, Konya, and the Caravanserais

visits.text: Aizanoi: the best-preserved Temple of Zeus in Anatolia, and that's an understatement. Built around the 2nd century AD with Hadrian's support, it measures 35 by 53 meters and its Ionic columns stand upright without recent consolidation. The site is still little visited—its inscription on the UNESCO indicative list since 2012 is starting to attract attention. No hotel in Çavdarhisar, come as a day trip from Kütahya (50 km).

Aizanoi's macellum bears a copy of Diocletian's Price Edict (301 AD): all official prices for goods in the Roman Empire carved in stone—slaves, horses, spices, labor. Next door, the theater-stadium complex is unique: a single enclosure served for both performances and games. Allow an extra 30 minutes.

days_plan.itin_title: Central Anatolia: Phrygia, Konya, and the Caravanserais

visits.text: Mehmet Kuşman, guardian of Çavuştepe, can read and write the Urartian language—one of 25 people in the world able to do so, according to multiple sources. The site, built by King Sarduri II between 764 and 734 BC, includes an open-air temple to Haldi. Cuneiform stelae still in place on the enclosure.

Hoşap Castle, perched on its rock in the village of Güzelsu, dates from 1643 and was built by a Kurdish governor of the Ottoman Empire. Massive round towers, well-preserved curtain walls. The site likely occupies an earlier Urartian fortress. Allow 45 minutes for your visit.

activities.text: Buy a stele carved in Urartian on site—Mehmet Kuşman sculpts and sells them for a few euros. It's a chance to chat with one of the last speakers of this isolated language. The site has no refreshment stand; bring water and cash.

days_plan.itin_title: Eastern Anatolia in 13 days: from the Caucasus to Ararat

visits.text: Right in the heart of Izmir, this 2nd-century AD agora—rebuilt by Marcus Aurelius after an earthquake—is one of the few Roman civic centres accessible in an urban setting in Turkey. The vaulted basements (cryptoporticus) housed storerooms and shops. Don't miss the frescoes still visible on the northern portico walls. The entrance is discreet: look for the sign between the Konak district buildings.

The Ottoman Clock Tower of Konak, built in 1901 for the 25th anniversary of Abdülhamid II's reign, is Izmir's visual emblem. The square around it overlooks the waterfront—the kordon—lined with café terraces. This is where you'll see everyday Izmir, far from tourist circuits: strolling couples, grilled corn vendors, students reading on benches.

Built by the Ottomans in the late 15th century and reinforced under Suleiman the Magnificent, this well-preserved castle now houses a museum. It guards the harbour entrance, and facing it eight kilometres away you can see the Greek island of Chios. In 1770, the Ottoman fleet was destroyed here by the Russians at the Battle of Çeşme—commemorative plaque visible on the ramparts.

Eight kilometres from Çeşme, Alaçatı is a restored Greek-architecture village with grey stone houses with coloured shutters, palm-lined lanes, and craft shops. Much more touristy than Ayvalık, but the old centre houses, built in the 19th century by Greek merchants, are genuinely well-maintained. Alaçatı is also known for its windsurfing and kitesurfing spots—the Çeşme channel winds are among the most consistent in the Mediterranean.

activities.text: The Çeşme peninsula is renowned for its natural thermal springs, whose waters reach 35 to 45°C. Thermal establishments have been set up around these springs, some directly on the beach. A 1.5-hour session late in the day, after morning visits, is ideal for recovery.

Çeşme's waterfront lines up cafés and fish restaurants facing the castle and the ferry port to Chios. In the evening, the vibe is lively without being aggressive. It's a good place to dine while watching sunsets over the Greek islands on the horizon.

days_plan.itin_title: Turkish Aegean Coast: Gallipoli, Troy, Ephesus & Bodrum in 12 Days

visits.text: These tombs were carved into the limestone cliff around 400 BC for the kings and nobles of the city of Caunos. According to Lycian belief, the higher the burial, the closer the deceased to the gods. The best way to admire them is from a kayık: the river offers the ideal angle, with reeds in the foreground and mausoleums in full cliff face. Several tombs display columned facades imitating contemporary temple architecture.

Caunos was founded in the 9th century BC. On site, you'll find a Greco-Roman theater with a 5,000-seat capacity, whose climb to the upper tiers offers an exceptional panorama over the Dalyan delta. The Roman baths, acropolis (152 m elevation), and remains of a Byzantine basilica complete the visit. Mosquitoes made the city sadly famous in Antiquity - Strabo himself noted it - so bring repellent in summer.

İztuzu stretches about 4 km between the sea and an interior lagoon. It's one of the main nesting sites for loggerhead sea turtles (Caretta caretta) in the Mediterranean: from May to September, females come to lay eggs at night. The beach closes every evening at dusk. After a long morning in the ruins, it's the ideal place to swim in blue water with no buildings on the horizon.

activities.text: The kayık is Dalyan's traditional flat-bottomed boat. A full day by boat allows you to see the tombs, disembark at Caunos, reach İztuzu Beach, and stop at Köyceğiz's thermal mud baths - whose hot sulfurous waters (38°C) are said to have therapeutic properties for skin. Several guides offer this combined circuit departing from Dalyan's central pier.

Dalyan's mud baths are natural pools where visitors coat themselves with warm sulfurous mineral mud, then rinse in thermal springs. The experience is both absurd and joyful. The tradition dates back to Antiquity - Strabo already mentioned this region's springs.

days_plan.itin_title: From Caria to Pamphylia: 12 Days on the Turquoise Coast, Marmaris to Antalya

visits.text: Push on to Eski Datça, the old village 3 km from the harbor: about a hundred houses built from pale stone in the 18th century, lanes shaded by almond and fig trees, a few small cafés. Knidos first settled here, in the middle of the peninsula, before relocating to its western tip in the 4th century BC. The prize for tranquility goes to this village, largely spared by mass tourism.

This circular temple (tholos) is one of the site's most remarkable monuments. It housed the statue of Aphrodite of Knidos sculpted by Praxiteles in 365 BC - the first female nude in Greek art, displayed from all sides thanks to doors opening around the perimeter. The original statue has disappeared; late copies are preserved in the Vatican. The pedestal is still in place: you can pause here for a few minutes wondering why kings traveled from across the Mediterranean just to see this one sculpture.

Knidos's grand Hellenistic theater could accommodate up to 20,000 spectators according to some estimates. From its tiers, you have a direct view of the two natural harbors - the small harbor to the north and the large harbor to the south - that made the city wealthy: ships waited here for winds to shift before rounding Cape Krio. The Lion of Knidos, symbol of the city's naval victory, was carried off to the British Museum in 1858.

A 20 to 30-minute walk from the archaeological site leads to the lighthouse at the western tip of the peninsula. This is the exact meeting point between the Aegean Sea and the Mediterranean. On clear days, you can make out the Greek island of Kos less than 30 km away. Come preferably in late afternoon: the sunset over the open sea is one of the most beautiful along the entire coast.

activities.text: Datça harbor is less crowded than Marmaris: a few fishermen's restaurants, sailing boats, and a relaxed atmosphere. Datça almonds - hand-harvested on the peninsula - are sold everywhere and worth trying. Sample the local rakı too if you're out in the evening.

Knidos's two coves are stunningly beautiful swimming spots, framed by ancient ruins. The north harbor is calmer; the south harbor, more exposed to wind, is often frequented by anchored gulets. The water clarity is remarkable.

days_plan.itin_title: From Caria to Pamphylia: 12 Days on the Turquoise Coast, Marmaris to Antalya

visits.text: The ancient city of Dolichiste was partially submerged in the 2nd century after an earthquake. From the boat deck, you'll see under 1 to 6 meters of water the walls of houses, staircases descending toward the sea, quays, and Roman cisterns. The area has been classified as a special protected zone since 1990: diving and swimming are prohibited. The ideal time is morning before 9am, when the water is perfectly still and the ruins stand out with surprising clarity. Polarized sunglasses make all the difference.

Kaleköy ('castle village') is a village with no road access, reachable only by boat or trail. The Byzantine castle atop the hill dominates the entire Kekova bay. Inside the walls: the smallest known theater in Lycia. Lycian sarcophagi emerge everywhere among the houses and in the harbor water. Take time to climb to the castle - the 360° view of the site is exceptional, and the descent into the village offers a chance to appreciate the few small fish restaurants.

Myra's tombs are among the best preserved in all of Lycia. Carved directly into the limestone cliff in the 4th and 3rd centuries BC, they imitate Lycian wooden house facades - some still bear traces of polychrome paintings. Several dozen tombs are layered on the wall, on four to five levels. The Lycian inscriptions and funerary reliefs make this site one of the most precious documents on Lycian civilization.

This is Lycia's largest theater. It could accommodate around 11,000 spectators according to estimates. Built in Roman times and later enlarged, it's remarkably well preserved. From the upper tiers, the rock-cut tombs are visible just above.

activities.text: The entire day is spent by boat. Departure from Üçağız or Çayağzı (Demre) harbor. The circuit includes passing over the sunken city, a stop at Kaleköy, and several swimming bays (including Aquarium Bay and Karemlik Bay where sea turtles are often observed). Glass-bottom boats offer maximum visibility of the underwater ruins. Gulets offer lunch on board.

days_plan.itin_title: From Caria to Pamphylia: 12 Days on the Turquoise Coast, Marmaris to Antalya

visits.text: Under a plain street, eight levels carved in tuff descend to 85 m depth at Derinkuyu, Cappadocia's deepest known underground city. It could shelter up to 20,000 people with livestock and provisions for months. Narrow tunnels, ceiling height often under 1.70 m. Claustrophobes stay away.

Probably dating to the 8th-7th century BC in the Phrygian era, Derinkuyu was mainly used from the 2nd century by Christians fleeing Roman persecutions, then in Byzantine and Seljuk periods. Rediscovered by accident in 1963 during a cellar renovation. Less than 10% of the complex has been explored.

activities.text: Visit Kaymaklı as a complement, 10 km north of Derinkuyu: it's the second accessible underground city, wider and shallower, with more airy galleries than its cousin. Four levels open to the public. Both can be done in the same half-day, count on 6 hours with the road. Combined ticket sometimes available.

days_plan.itin_title: Extended Cappadocia in 8 Days: Kayseri, Göreme, Konya

visits.text: Priene is a lesson in urban planning: a city founded in the 4th century BC on a Hippodamian grid (regular perpendicular streets), it's one of the best illustrations of the Greek city ideal. Alexander the Great financed the Temple of Athena here—a commemorative plaque confirms it, preserved in the British Museum. The theatre, carved into the slope, offers views over the Maeander plain. The city was never rebuilt nor invaded by a modern town: it's this isolation that makes it an exceptional site.

Miletus' Great Theatre could seat 15,000 spectators. Miletus was one of the most influential Ionian cities in antiquity, birthplace of Thales, Anaximander, and Anaximenes—the first philosophers to seek natural (not mythological) explanations for the world. The city was gradually strangled by the alluvium of the Maeander river, which silted up its harbour over centuries. Also visit the Baths of Faustina (2nd century AD) and the bouleuterion portico.

The Temple of Apollo at Didyma is the fourth-largest temple in Greek antiquity, measuring 109 × 51 metres. Its construction began around 300 BC and was never completed—even in the 4th century AD, columns and capitals remained unfinished. The Didyma oracle was second in importance after Delphi: Alexander the Great consulted it after liberating Miletus from the Persians in 334 BC, and the oracle declared him son of Zeus. An 18-kilometre Sacred Way linked the sanctuary to Miletus—processions followed it during major festivals. Three monumental columns still standing give an idea of the project's scale. The Medusa head adorning the temple threshold has become the symbol of the town of Didim.

activities.text: These three sites are 30 minutes' drive from each other. Tour operators offer organised circuits from Selçuk or Kuşadası. By private car, allow a full day starting early (8 a.m.): 2 hours at Priene, lunch in Söke or on-site, 1.5 hours at Miletus, 2 hours at Didyma. The three sites together don't have Ephesus's crowds—that's precisely their charm.

Two kilometres from the Temple of Apollo, Altınkum (Golden Sand) is a fine-sand beachfront with shallow water ideal for swimming. Late in the day, after visits, an hour at the beach allows you to decompress before heading to Bodrum.

days_plan.itin_title: Turkish Aegean Coast: Gallipoli, Troy, Ephesus & Bodrum in 12 Days

visits.text: Built in 1228 by the Mengujekid emir Ahmed Shah and his wife Melike Turan, the complex of the Great Mosque and Hospital of Divriği is one of the first three Turkish sites inscribed on UNESCO in 1985, along with Cappadocia and Istanbul. Architects came from Ahlat and Tiflis. Nicknamed the Alhambra of Anatolia for its portal sculpture.

The portals of Divriği's Great Mosque accumulate tens of thousands of hand-carved motifs; none repeats. Four major portals, geometries in relief so deep they evoke Gothic work. The carved wooden minbar is the work of a Georgian master from Tbilisi.

activities.text: If you're coming after May 2024, Divriği's Great Mosque has reopened for worship and visits after nine years of restoration. A small influx of local visitors, atmosphere still sleepy on weekdays. Isolated town at the end of a valley, long but uncrowded road from Sivas. One night on-site is worthwhile.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Central Turkey: 7 Days Ankara to Kayseri

visits.text: Diyarbakır's ramparts are the world's longest after the Great Wall of China - 5.8 km of black basalt walls, pierced by four main gates and flanked by 82 towers. Built in the Roman era (4th century AD) and UNESCO-listed in 2015, they still bear 63 inscriptions from different epochs. The exterior promenade along the base reveals the fortress's entire silhouette from the Tigris plain. Allow 2 hours for the complete circuit.

Diyarbakır's Ulu Cami (Grand Mosque) is Anatolia's oldest mosque - its construction dates to the Arab conquest of 639, on the site of a former Byzantine church. The current building, remodeled in the Seljuk period (11th century), is a fine example of early Islamic architecture with its arcaded courtyard and numerous reused columns. The prayer hall differs from classic Ottoman mosques with its seven naves running north-south.

The Hevsel Gardens stretch across 700 hectares between the ramparts and the Tigris banks. UNESCO-listed alongside the fortress, they've fed the city for 8,000 years and today constitute the region's main avifauna habitat, with over 180 bird species recorded. Otters, foxes, and hedgehogs also live here. Access isn't universal, but viewing platforms built into the ramparts offer a striking overview of this green carpet against the black walls.

activities.text: The Sur district, within the ramparts, is Diyarbakır's historic heart. Its cobbled lanes lead from mosque to Armenian church, from spice market to carved basalt houses. The city bears traces of every civilization that passed through: Roman, Byzantine, Arab, Turkish, Kurdish. Take time to explore on foot; some sections were damaged during 2015-2016 conflicts and are being restored.

days_plan.itin_title: Gaziantep to Trabzon: Mesopotamia, Nemrut & Historic Armenia - 14 Days

places.name: Diyarbakir

visits.text: UNESCO-listed in 2015, the black ramparts of Diyarbakır form a 5.8 km ring of volcanic basalt around the Sur district. It's one of the longest medieval enclosures still standing in the world. The ramparts date largely from the Byzantine era, 4th-6th centuries, with Roman and Islamic additions.

The basalt heats under your fingers, dark and pitted. The local volcanic rock is so present that the city was nicknamed Kara Amid (black Amida) until the 16th century. The ramparts count 82 bastions and four main gates (Harput, Mardin, Urfa, Yeni), from the Romans to the Ottomans. The Mardinkapı and Urfakapı gates are the best preserved.

Locals say Kara Amid, black Amida. The Sur district, within the walls, suffered from the 2015-2016 clashes; some blocks remain inaccessible or under reconstruction. Check before venturing into certain alleys. The tour along the top of the ramparts remains safe and marked, in good condition on the southern half.

The Ulu Camii beats the Umayyad Mosque of Damascus for age in Anatolia: it's considered the oldest great mosque still standing in Turkey. Its northern façade, with reused Corinthian columns and reliefs of a bull attacked by a lion, looks like no other. Women: scarf and covered legs mandatory.

Rebuilt in 1091 by the Seljuks, the Ulu Camii adopts the plan of the Umayyad mosque of Damascus, transposed into black basalt and white limestone. The adjoining Mesudiye Medresesi, completed in 1198 by the Artuqids, is one of the region's first theological schools. Black-and-white chevron decoration, very distinctive.

In the Sur bazaar, the smell of tütün and hot tobacco mingles with stalls of dried fruit and copper. The market stretches from the Ulu Camii to the Mardin gate, along Gazi Street. Less touristy than those of Urfa or Antep, more authentic. Look for Kurdish passementerie and black filigree jewelry, a city specialty.

activities.text: Walk 4 km on the rampart walkway on the Mardinkapı side: it's the best-preserved section for views over the Tigris and the Hevsel gardens below. Three hours on foot with photo stops. Access is from the Mardin gate, paid ticket at the entrance. Avoid the hot summer hours; no shade on the wall.

If you come in spring, descend to the Hevsel gardens: 700 hectares of vegetable gardens irrigated by the Tigris, UNESCO-listed with the ramparts. Many bird species nest there, including herons and egrets. Marked trail from the Mardin gate, allow 1.5 hours round trip on foot.

Follow Gazi Street to the Cahit Sıtkı Tarancı house, a Kurdish-Turkish poet born in Diyarbakır in 1910. His family home, a magnificent example of Sur's vernacular architecture in basalt and wood, now serves as a museum. Modest entry; allow 45 minutes. The black-and-white checkerboard inner courtyard is typical of the region.

The Dengbêj are Kurdish storytellers: their house in Diyarbakır, opened in 2007 in Sur, hosts their traditional improvised songs, accompanied by tea. Sessions take place in the afternoon, free. You won't understand the lyrics, but the deep voice grain and oral rhythm are enough to tell a story.

days_plan.itin_title: Eastern Anatolia in 13 days: from the Caucasus to Ararat
Turkish Mesopotamia: 11 Days from Gaziantep to the Tigris
Turkish Mesopotamia: 11 Days from Gaziantep to the Tigris

map_keywords: Cahit Sıtkı Tarancı House Museum Diyarbakır

visits.text: In 1994, vandals with pickaxes destroyed the statue of Cybele in the central niche of Aslankaya—it can only be seen in old photographs. What remains is nevertheless striking: a 15-meter free-standing tuff monolith, with a bas-relief lion on the right side and a carved triangular façade, dated to the mid-6th century BC. No guard on site, informal parking at the edge of the track.

days_plan.itin_title: Central Anatolia: Phrygia, Konya, and the Caravanserais

places.name: Dogubayaz T

visits.text: İshak Pasha Palace perches on a rocky ledge 5 km from Doğubayazıt, commanding the plain with Ararat as backdrop. This 366-room complex was built between 1685 and 1784 by three generations of the same pasha family. Its architecture is a deliberate blend of Ottoman, Persian, Georgian, and Armenian styles - a synthesis explained by the region's frontier position. The harem, ceremonial hall, and mosque are in good condition. The place is even more impressive from the viewpoint opposite, where you see the palace and Ararat together in the same frame.

Mount Ararat (Ağrı Dağı), 5,137 m, is Turkey's highest peak - an extinct volcano whose snow-capped slopes dominate the plains for hundreds of kilometers. It's considered a sacred mountain by Armenians (symbol of the nation) and associated worldwide with Noah's Ark. You can only circle it with your eyes from Doğubayazıt, unless you've booked a guided expedition months in advance (permit required). The view from the palace viewpoint is one of Turkey's most photographed.

The ruins of Old Bayazıt (Eski Bayazıt) occupy the foot of the castle hill, a few dozen meters behind İshak Pasha Palace. The town was destroyed by the Turkish army in 1930. House foundations, a restored mosque (Eski Bayezid Camii), and dressed stones from the Urartian fortress remain, some dating to the 9th century BC.

activities.text: The Durupınar site, 30 km south, is presented by some as the remains of Noah's Ark - a boat-shaped rock formation at 2,000 m altitude. Geologists are skeptical and consider it a natural volcanic structure. For those interested in unsolved mysteries, it's a possible excursion; for others, the view of Ararat's foothills is the reward.

days_plan.itin_title: Gaziantep to Trabzon: Mesopotamia, Nemrut & Historic Armenia - 14 Days

map_keywords: Ishak Pasha Palace Dogubayazit Ottoman palace

visits.text: Built over 99 years, Ishak Pasha Palace was begun in 1685 and completed in 1784 by its namesake, a Kurdish sanjak-bey. 7,600 m² perched on a rocky spur facing Ararat. Mosque, harem, salamlık, and 366 rooms according to the portal inscription. An Ottoman, Persian, and Georgian blend.

Doğubayazıt Fortress, more discreet, stands on a ridge east of the palace. Its foundations date back to the Urartian era, around 800 BC. Allow 30 minutes on foot from Ishak Pasha. You'll have the place to yourselves.

Kurds call the poet Ehmedê Xanî their spiritual father. His mausoleum, right next to the palace, draws Kurdish pilgrims in silence. Xanî wrote Mem û Zîn in the 17th century, an epic in Kurmanji. Free visit, allow 20 minutes.

activities.text: Park at dawn in front of the palace: morning light illuminates the east-facing façade over the plain. Tour buses arrive around 10 a.m. and the crowded site loses its atmosphere. The drive from downtown takes 15 minutes by taxi (around 100 TL round-trip with wait time).

In the evening, from the terrace of Tehran Boutique Hotel or any downtown rooftop, Ararat cuts a mauve silhouette. Doğubayazıt sits at 1,600 m altitude; the air is dry and the late-day light turns pink. Clouds often cling to the summit in the afternoon.

days_plan.itin_title: Eastern Anatolia in 13 days: from the Caucasus to Ararat

visits.text: At dawn on 25 April 1915, soldiers of the ANZAC corps stormed this pebble beach. They expected to land on flat terrain—they found cliffs instead. A simple stone memorial bears the words Atatürk addressed to Australian and New Zealand mothers in 1934: your sons now lie here, in friendly soil. Walk down to the beach: the water is clear, almost peaceful. It's precisely this disconnect that makes the place so haunting.

Among the 31 Commonwealth cemeteries maintained by the CWGC across the peninsula, Lone Pine is the most poignant on the ANZAC side. The memorial lists the names of Australian and New Zealand soldiers with no known grave. Take time to read a few inscriptions: many were under twenty. The pine tree that gives the site its name was replanted from seeds brought back to Australia—the original was destroyed during the fighting.

Climb here to understand the strategic stakes of the entire campaign. The Allies needed to seize this summit to control the strait. The New Zealanders held it for 36 hours in August 1915 before being driven off. From the top, the view takes in the Aegean on one side, the Dardanelles and the Asian coast on the other—geography explains everything.

At the southern tip of the peninsula, this 42-metre-high monument honours the approximately 253,000 Ottoman soldiers who died at Gallipoli. In front of it, rows of symbolic glass tombs arranged by Ottoman province. The most famous inscription is Colonel Mustafa Kemal's order to his ammunition-starved regiment on 25 April 1915: 'I am not ordering you to attack. I am ordering you to die.' This future founder of modern Turkey is omnipresent on this peninsula—it's here that his legend began.

This war museum preserves objects found on the battlefield: false teeth, belt buckles, sniper shields, photographs. Ordinary objects that make death tangible. Panels are in Turkish and English—allow a good hour and don't rush through: this is where the abstract numbers of war take on a human face.

activities.text: This marked 2-kilometre trail links the peninsula's 11 main ANZAC sites. Audio guides are available on-site. Walk early in the morning—before 9 a.m., you'll often have the cemeteries to yourself, and the low-angled light on the white crosses is striking. Closed-toe shoes required: the terrain is hilly.

The peninsula stretches roughly 30 km north to south and 25 km east to west. A car is essential to connect the ANZAC sites in the north with the Ottoman memorial in the south in a single day. Allow about 3 hours of driving for the full circuit, not including stops.

days_plan.itin_title: Turkish Aegean Coast: Gallipoli, Troy, Ephesus & Bodrum in 12 Days

visits.text: Sinan outdid himself in Edirne, and he knew it. The Selimiye Mosque, built between 1568 and 1574, boasts a dome 31 meters in diameter—slightly wider than Hagia Sophia's—and four 71-meter minarets. UNESCO-listed in 2011, set within a külliye complex with a museum of medicine. Free entry.

Built between 1402 and 1414, the Eski Cami ('old mosque') is Edirne's oldest surviving religious building, with grand Arabic calligraphy on the pillars. Contemplative atmosphere and rarely crowded. Open freely outside prayer times.

Light slants obliquely into the Bayezid II Külliye Museum, housed in the former Bayezid II mosque complex—Quranic school and medieval hospital converted. Dedicated to Ottoman medical history, it displays surgical instruments and reconstructed treatment rooms. Modest fee, few visitors.

activities.text: Wander the Ottoman arasta below the Selimiye: this 17th-century market gallery sells jewelry, textiles, and local olive oil. It's also the best angle to photograph the minarets in alignment.

As locals say: 'Edirne has to be earned.' A provincial Turkish town... that was once the capital of an empire: Edirne preceded Istanbul as the Ottoman seat from 1365 to 1453. Almost no international tourists know this, which explains the preserved atmosphere. In June, wrestlers compete at the Kırkpınar Oil Wrestling Festival, ongoing since the 14th century.

days_plan.itin_title: Istanbul to Sofia: Edirne, Plovdiv, Rila & the Rhodopes

visits.text: Eğirdir's Byzantine fortress is now just a wall and a gate, but it dominated the rocky bottleneck between lake and mountain for centuries. Built initially around 400 BC according to local guides (take the date with caution), rebuilt by the Seljuks. The view over the lake from the summit redeems the site's austerity.

Hızır Bey Mosque and Dündar Bey Medresesi face each other in the centre of Eğirdir, both from the early 1200s. The medrese, restored, sometimes hosts a craft market beneath its vaults. Pale stone, Seljuk portal with muqarnas. Free visit; headscarf for the mosque.

Yeşilada stretches at the end of a land bridge extending the town 1 km into the lake. It's a former Greek island linked to the mainland by an embankment, still dotted with stone houses from the 1900s. Walk along both shores: calm water on one side, ruffled by wind on the other—two microclimates across 30 metres.

activities.text: Locals call Eğirdir 'little Switzerland' for its Akpınar mountains ringing the lake. Rent a mountain bike at Anatoly Bisiklet Evi (around 200 TL per day) and loop the peninsula. The Saint Paul Trail passes through here; red-white blazes visible from the centre.

Sample Eğirdir apples in September-October: the region is one of Turkey's major orchards, with pyramids of apples sold roadside on the town's outskirts. Lake fish (pike-perch, pike) also feature on every menu. Arzava Restaurant serves an honest version for 300-400 TL.

days_plan.itin_title: 7-Day Taurus: Turquoise Lakes & Lycian Cities

activities.text: Continue 5 km to Lake Emre for a breather: this shallow lake attracts flamingos and herons during spring migration. A few local fishermen rent out boats for the day, and it's the only spot on the circuit where you sit without looking at a monument. No facilities; bring your own meal.

days_plan.itin_title: Central Anatolia: Phrygia, Konya, and the Caravanserais

places.name: Erzurum

visits.text: The Çifte Minareli Medrese (Twin Minaret Madrasa) is Erzurum's most recognizable monument. Built in the 13th century at the end of the Seljuk era, it's Anatolia's largest madrasa (35 x 48 m). Its two 26 m fluted brick minarets, adorned with blue faience details, rise above a portal covered in chisel carvings of a finesse few Anatolian buildings match. Tree of life, double-headed Seljuk eagle, geometric interlacing - every surface is inhabited. Today a museum with free courtyard access.

Erzurum's Ulu Cami (Grand Mosque), built in 1179 by Saltukid Emir Nasreddin Aslan Mehmed, is one of the most austere and oldest examples of Anatolian Islamic architecture. Its seven north-south running naves and sixty columns create an unusual stone forest, very different from grand Ottoman mosques with central plans. The interior wooden framework in 'kırlangıç' (swallow) style is an architectural rarity.

Erzurum's citadel occupies a hill dominating the city since the 5th century (restored by Byzantine Emperor Theodosius). Its Clock Tower (Tepsi Minaret), formerly a minaret repurposed, offers a panoramic view over the mountain-ringed city. The Üç Kümbetler (Three Tombs), 12th-century Seljuk mausoleums with conical roofs, stand 300 m south of the madrasa.

The Yakutiye Medresesi (1310), built by the Mongol Ilkhanids, now houses the Museum of Turkish and Islamic Arts. Its double-headed eagle portal and Central Asian-style faience minaret make it a perfect complement to the twin minaret madrasa visit.

activities.text: Erzurum is the regional capital at 1,750 m altitude, often wind-swept - jacket or warm clothing even in summer. The city offers highland cuisine: cağ kebabı (lamb roasted on a horizontal spit, local specialty served on lavash bread), ayran soup, and su böreği (boiled cheese pastry). It's also a dynamic university town, which shows in the center's liveliness.

days_plan.itin_title: Gaziantep to Trabzon: Mesopotamia, Nemrut & Historic Armenia - 14 Days
Eastern Anatolia in 13 days: from the Caucasus to Ararat

map_keywords: Çifte Minareli Medrese twin minaret Seljuk Erzurum

visits.text: Multicolored façades, overhanging balconies, cobbled lanes: the Odunpazarı district ('wood market') is the Ottoman soul of Eskişehir. The two-story wooden houses have been restored into workshops, cafés, and galleries without turning into a theme park. Ideal early in the morning before school buses arrive.

Kengo Kuma's Odunpazarı Modern Müze (OMM) would deserve its place in any major capital. Inaugurated in 2019, the stacking of laminated-wood volumes echoes the spirit of neighboring Ottoman houses. Inside, a collection of Turkish and international contemporary art, from Taner Ceylan to bamboo installations. Special mention for the 2021 European Museum of the Year Award.

Eskişehir's meerschaum, a soft white stone, is found almost exclusively in this region—the city has made it its artisanal identity. The Meerschaum Museum, integrated into the Kurşunlu Camii complex, displays pipes, objects, and lületaşı sculptures from the 15th century to today. In the shop: prices remain well below those in Istanbul.

activities.text: Walk along the Porsuk in the evening: terraces come alive along the channeled river, nicknamed the Turkish Venice with a few gondolas thrown in. The promenade of the Adalar islets is the busiest. For a cool drink, cafés on the university side offer the best value for money.

If you have two hours, a çini workshop is a must before leaving. Odunpazarı workshops offer tile-painting initiation sessions starting at 150-200 TL depending on the shop. Result dries in 30 minutes, transportable without risk. Avoid vendors around the OMM; their prices are doubled for tourists.

days_plan.itin_title: Central Anatolia: Phrygia, Konya, and the Caravanserais

visits.text: The tomb of Amyntas, son of Hermagios - that's what the Greek inscription on the lintel says - was carved into the cliff in 350 BC. Its Ionic columned facade is one of the best preserved in all of Lycia. To reach it, climb about a hundred steps cut into the rock: from the top, Fethiye Bay spreads before you with its wooded islands. Come preferably in late afternoon, when the raking light gilds the limestone.

The historic Paspatur quarter sits at the end of the bay, beneath the cliff tombs. Narrow lanes, overhanging houses, a few Lycian sarcophagi dropped between the cars - including a famous one sitting in the middle of a street, forcing traffic to go around it. The atmosphere is less touristy than the waterfront: this is where locals eat and drink.

Fethiye's fish market operates on an original principle: you choose your fresh fish from the stalls, then surrounding restaurants prepare it for you for a set cover-and-service charge. It's one of the most pleasant dinners on the Turquoise Coast. In the evening, the stalls are illuminated and the atmosphere festive.

The remains of Telmessos theater (2nd century BC) are visible near the harbor, recently restored. The restoration is controversial - many original stones were replaced - but it allows you to grasp the structure's scale. A good introduction before setting out to discover the better-preserved sites later in the journey.

Tlos's acropolis is a rocky spur dominating the plain. On the south cliff, look for the so-called Tomb of Bellerophon - the Lycian hero who, mounted on Pegasus, is said to have killed the Chimera (whose flames are still visible at Olympos, day 9). His tomb is a large columned burial chamber with a relief of Bellerophon riding Pegasus. On the summit, the foundations of the Lycian citadel bear the walls of an Ottoman fort built by the warlord Kanlı Ali Ağa in the 19th century. The climb is well worth the effort for the view over the Xanthos valley.

At the base of the acropolis, on the main road, you'll find the Roman theater (5,600 seats according to estimates) and two bath complexes - including the Great Bath with its seven arched windows opening onto the valley. The Hellenistic stadium with 2,500 seats is still legible, with its 72-meter-long water basin. Tlos received the honorary title of 'brilliant metropolis of the Lycian nation' in Roman times.

Saklıkent means 'hidden city' in Turkish. The gorge opens into the mountain for 18 km in length and reaches up to 300 m in depth with a width sometimes less than 2 m. Access is via wading through the torrent from a wooden boardwalk. The water is ice-cold even in midsummer - expect to get wet up to your waist to progress inside. The first section (1 to 2 km) is accessible to everyone. Further in, the canyon requires equipment. Restaurants on stilts above the river at the entrance offer a lunch break with your feet in the water.

activities.text: Butterfly Valley is hemmed between 350-meter cliffs falling straight into the sea. It takes its name from the more than 80 butterfly species that nest there, including the Jersey Tiger. A 50-meter waterfall descends into the valley before reaching the sea. Accessible only by boat from Ölüdeniz (regular departures from the beach in season): there's no other way to get there.

Fethiye's waterfront is one of the liveliest on the Turquoise Coast. Dozens of gulets are moored at the pier. In the evening, departures for multi-day cruises (Blue Cruise) are prepared in a contagiously festive atmosphere.

Progressing through Saklıkent canyon - on foot in the water - is an intense and memorable physical experience. The torrent is fed by snowmelt from Akdağ (2,000 m); even in August, the water doesn't exceed 12°C. Water shoes are rented at the park entrance if you don't have your own.

visits.text: Gaziantep wears its baklava capital nickname lightly: it's also the capital of pistachios, isot pepper, eggplant kebab, and one of the densest cuisines in the world. UNESCO designated it a Creative City of Gastronomy in 2015, alongside Lyon and Parma. Start with the historic center, restored across 5.5 km around the castle. Skip the crowded noon terraces—locals eat earlier than you'd think.

At Bakırcılar Çarşısı, the hammering of copper has set the rhythm of the day for four centuries. Workshops line the cobbled alleys below the castle. You'll watch trays, teapots, and kebab skewers forged by hand right in front of you. Haggle without hesitation—it's expected. Best prices are in the morning, before tour buses arrive.

Built in the Bronze Age, Gaziantep Castle has dominated the old town from its hilltop perch. The February 2023 earthquake damaged it severely, and restoration is ongoing. You may not be able to climb inside, but the circuit around the base of the ramparts remains one of the finest viewpoints over the city. Check access on the day with the tourist office.

One mosaic steals the show from all the rest: the Gypsy Girl at the Zeugma Museum. In a dark room, only her eyes are lit, and they follow you. The rest of the museum is vast: 2,448 m² of Roman mosaics rescued from the Birecik dam, across three floors. Budget two full hours.

Opened in 2011, the Zeugma Museum protects floor mosaics from the Roman villas of the ancient city of Belkis-Zeugma, founded around 300 BC. Don't miss the Ocean and Tethys mosaic, or the bronze Mars. The twelve panels recovered in 2018 from Bowling Green University have finally rejoined the original work. Avoid weekends—it's overrun with school groups.

The smell of hot sugar and clarified butter draws you in from the entrance of the Baklava Museum, housed in a restored Ottoman mansion. The displays show the 40 layers of phyllo pastry, thin as a page, and the role of Antep pistachios in baklava Gaziantep, which has been EU-protected since 2013. Small museum, big technical lesson.

activities.text: Locals start their day with beyran: a lamb broth simmered all night, served with rice and plenty of chili. Try it at the Coppersmiths' Bazaar from 8am, in a nameless diner. This isn't a dish for delicate stomachs, but it's the essence of Antep cuisine.

Push open the door of Zincirli Bedesten in late afternoon: this restored Ottoman covered market gathers spices, kutnu fabrics, and Antep pistachios under a timbered roof. It's less touristy than the copper bazaar, with a more mixed atmosphere. Look for isot, the local smoked pepper with its own protected geographical indication.

If you love pastry, book two hours at İmam Çağdaş, operating since 1887. Order the fıstıklı baklava and katmer with creamy pistachio. Service is quick, no fuss. It's the local reference, cited in every guide and always packed with Antep residents. Skip the copycat terraces downtown—the real deal is here.

Taste menengiç, the coffee made from roasted wild pistachios rather than coffee beans. Tahmis Kahvesi next to Zincirli Bedesten has served it since the 17th century. Paired with a bite of halva, it's sweet and earthy.

visits.text: Older than Stonehenge by several millennia, the Göbekli Tepe site has been dated between 9600 and 8200 BC by UNESCO. It was built by hunter-gatherers, before the invention of agriculture. This overturns our understanding of the Neolithic: the sacred preceded sedentism, not the other way around. Less than 5% of the site has been excavated.

Inscribed on UNESCO in 2018, the Göbekli Tepe site features 5.5-meter T-shaped limestone pillars, carved with bas-relief sculptures: wild boars, foxes, vultures, scorpions. A protective hall covers the main enclosures. You walk around them on walkways, without being able to descend. Klaus Schmidt, the German archaeologist who revealed it all, died on site in 2014.

activities.text: Arrive before 9am to avoid Turkish tour groups coming by bus from Şanlıurfa. The visitor center opens at 8am; a 10-minute film explains the context before climbing to the site. You can cover everything in 1.5 hours. The midday sun is merciless—no shade on the hill. The French audio guide is recommended.
