places.name: Goreme

visits.text: A volcanic canyon 14 kilometres long and 100 metres deep, ranked among Cappadocia's greatest natural and archaeological sites. Over a hundred dwellings and rock-cut churches are carved into the tuff cliffs. The best-preserved frescoes are found in the Ağaçaltı Kilisesi (Church under the Trees), Yılanlı Kilisesi (Serpent Church) and Kokar Kilisesi. The Melendiz River flows at the bottom, lined with poplars and vegetable gardens.

Carved into a volcanic formation at the northern end of Ihlara Valley, Selime is Cappadocia's largest rock-cut monastery. It comprises a cathedral with two naves, kitchens with chimneys cut into the rock, stables and monastic cells on several levels. The complex is integrated into a cliff that takes on the appearance of a natural castle when viewed from afar.

A rock-cut monastic complex inhabited from the 4th to 13th centuries, recognised as a UNESCO World Heritage site in 1985. The marked circuit passes through some thirty spaces: churches, chapels, refectories, cells, kitchens. Frescoes cover the vaults with gospel scenes - Nativity, Betrayal of Judas, Crucifixion - whose colours remain vivid despite the mutilation of eyes, performed superstitiously by villagers who feared the evil eye. Photography is forbidden inside all the churches.

The museum's most famous church. Its name comes from the fact that it originally had only one small window. This lack of light has preserved its 11th-century frescoes in a remarkable way: Christ Pantocrator, Nativity, Transfiguration, Betrayal by Judas and Crucifixion are still astonishingly vivid in colour. A 14-year restoration was needed to remove layers of pigeon droppings. Separate ticket in addition to the main ticket.

50 metres below the museum entrance (same ticket), this is Göreme's largest and most remarkable church for its frescoes. It results from the fusion of two successive churches: the old one (AD 910) with its complete narrative cycle of Christ's life, and the new one (960-1010 AD) decorated by a master from Constantinople, in deep red and blue tones.

One of the best-preserved on the main circuit, with frescoes on the theme of Christ's life and the apostles arranged around a central dome. The colours - ochres, blues, greens - have weathered the centuries well.

Cappadocia's highest point at 1,300 metres altitude. This volcanic pinnacle has been hollowed out with galleries, apartments, chapels and storerooms connected by a labyrinth of 20 levels, from the Hittite era (15th century BC) to the last Greek inhabitants in the early 20th century. From the summit, the view embraces Göreme and its fairy chimneys on one side, the Central Anatolian plain on the other.

The valley connecting Uçhisar to Göreme owes its name to the pigeon houses carved into the tuff cliffs, by the hundreds. These cavities were used to collect pigeon droppings, a precious fertiliser for Cappadocian vineyards and orchards. Today, some pigeon houses are still active. The 3.5-kilometre walk between Uçhisar and Göreme follows the tuff river, between vineyards and orchards.

On the south bank of the Kızılırmak (the Red River, Turkey's longest), Avanos has been Cappadocia's pottery centre since antiquity. The red clay extracted from the river banks is thrown on foot-powered wheels, using a technique handed down since Hittite times. Each workshop is family-run: you'll be shown the different stages (throwing, drying, firing, glazing) and can try for yourself.

Turkey's deepest accessible underground city: 8 open levels out of a total of 18, descending to 85 metres. It could shelter up to 20,000 people with their animals and food reserves. The Phrygians laid its foundations in the 8th-7th century BC; the Byzantines then considerably expanded it. Circular stone doors could be closed from the inside, blocking each level independently. A cruciform church occupies the 3rd and 4th levels. The city was connected by an 8 to 9-kilometre tunnel to the city of Kaymaklı.

Devrent Valley can be explored freely (no ticket required) and explored on foot in 30 to 45 minutes. The pink tuff formations here take unexpected shapes depending on viewing angle: a camel, a skull, a seal, a face. The rock is lighter and softer than elsewhere - the place is renowned for photography in slanting light.

Paşabağ is famous for its triple-headed fairy chimneys (a single chimney with three superimposed caps), Cappadocia's most photographed. Hermit monks from the 9th-10th centuries carved cells into the rock - some are still visible halfway up the columns. The valley's vegetation (vines, apricot trees) contrasts with the white formations.

Cappadocia is one of the world's best destinations for ballooning thanks to the reliability of morning thermal winds, the density of geological formations and atmospheric clarity. Hundreds of balloons rise simultaneously at dawn from different points across the region, creating a spectacle both from the air and from the ground. Flight duration is about 1 hour; the complete journey (transfer + inflation + flight + champagne) lasts 2.5 to 3 hours. Flights can be cancelled due to wind. Book at least a week in advance in high season (April-October).

An easy 2 to 4-kilometre walk through a valley carved with pink fairy chimneys and ancient troglodyte houses. The trail starts from the Çavuşin viewpoint and descends towards Göreme, revealing rock formations melting in the early morning hours. Several frescoed rock chapels are accessible along the way.

activities.text: Descend into the canyon via the main entrance at Ihlara village (360 steps). Walk back up at Belisırma after 3 to 4 kilometres walking along the river - that's where the best frescoes are concentrated, in the shade of poplars. Riverside restaurants in Belisırma serve local trout: perfect for lunch.

The site isn't comprehensively signposted - explore at your own pace, climb the stairs carved into the rock, enter the halls. The rock-cut cathedral is naturally lit through openings carved in the cliff. From the top of the rock, the view over the Cappadocian plain, the lava villages and the silhouette of Mount Hasan completes the day.

The site has explanatory panels in English but a local guide helps you understand Byzantine iconography, distinguish pre-iconoclastic from post-iconoclastic frescoes and identify scenes without exhausting yourself searching. Independent guides offer their services at the museum entrance. Allow 1.5 to 2 hours with a guide for the complete circuit.

In the evening, head down into the village itself. Göreme is organised around a bazaar and alleyways where troglodyte hotels pierce directly into the cliff. Find a rooftop terrace to watch the sunset over the surrounding fairy chimneys - the colours shift from yellow to fiery orange in a matter of minutes. The village also has several restaurants serving testi kebabı (stew cooked and served in sealed pottery).

This 3.5-kilometre walk (about 1 hour) starts from the bottom of Uçhisar and arrives in Göreme. The path descends gently between cliffs decorated with pigeon houses, passes abandoned vineyards and partially collapsed troglodyte houses. No signposting required - just follow the valley floor. Prefer early morning for light and coolness.

Most Avanos workshops offer free demonstrations then, for a modest sum, the chance to shape your own piece on the wheel. The red clay from the Kızılırmak is dense and responsive - harder to work than white clay. Pieces can be shipped home if you don't wish to carry them.

The visitor circuit follows red arrows (descent) and blue arrows (ascent). The corridors are narrow and sometimes low-ceilinged - bring clothes you don't mind getting dirty and closed shoes. The interior temperature is stable at around 13°C whatever the season. The lower levels are the most impressive: the circular stone doors, the descending ventilation shaft and the large barrel-vaulted hall on the 2nd level.

Rose Valley (Güllüdere Vadısı) catches fire at sunset: the ferruginous rock turns vivid orange-red for 20 to 30 minutes. From the viewpoint above Çavuşin, the view encompasses the fairy chimneys and glowing tuff terraces. The nearby Red Valley (Kızılçukur) offers another, wilder angle, with an easy 2-kilometre walk.

Kayseri Airport (ASR) is about 75 km from Göreme (1 hour drive). Nevşehir Airport (NAV) is closer (40 km, 40 min) but serves fewer domestic flights. Direct shuttles from Göreme serve both airports. If your flight is in late afternoon, you have time for a final market visit in Göreme village or a last pottery workshop in Avanos.

After ballooning and the morning walk, enjoy the village calm before the day's coaches arrive. A coffee on a troglodyte terrace, a market browse for local spices (saffron from Safranbolu, pistachios, tea), a final plate of mantı (Turkish ravioli): Göreme deserves an unhurried farewell.

visits.text: Fatih Sultan Mehmet had it built in 1464 for his teacher—Akşemseddin Türbesi honors the spiritual master who accompanied the conquest of Constantinople in 1453. The hexagonal ashlar stone building is in the courtyard of Gazi Süleyman Paşa Mosque. Free entry, open daily from 8:30am to 5:30pm.

Half-timbered façades rise in Göynük's green valley like pages from an Ottoman architecture manual. A Cittaslow like Mudurnu, the town has preserved a 14th-century urban texture. Gazi Süleyman Paşa Camii, built between 1331 and 1335 by Orhan Gazi's son, is one of the oldest Ottoman mosques in the region.

Slow city (Cittaslow) but history here isn't slow at all—Göynük is nicknamed 'Diyar-ı Akşemseddin'—land of Akşemseddin—and annual commemoration ceremonies in May draw pilgrims from the entire region. Houses from the Anatolian Beyliks period constitute rare testimony from that transition era.

activities.text: Climb to the Victory Tower (Zafer Kulesi), a three-story wooden tower erected in memory of the Battle of Sakarya (1923). Access is free and the view over the Ottoman houses and valley is the town's best. The climb is steep but short—allow 15 minutes round trip.

days_plan.itin_title: Black Sea & Safranbolu: 9 days village to village

visits.text: Built between 1229 and 1236 by Sultan Alaeddin Keykubad I, following plans by a Damascus architect. After a fire, it was restored and expanded in 1278 to reach 4,800 m² - Turkey's largest caravanserai. The structure comprises two parts: an open-air courtyard used in summer and a heated covered hall reserved for winter. The monumental entrance, 13 metres high in sculpted marble, is the finest surviving example of Seljuk portal art. A mosque raised on arches sits at the centre of the courtyard.

A 5.5-kilometre valley carved into volcanic tuff, sheltering 28 rock-cut churches dating from the 4th to 11th centuries. Saint Gregory of Nazianzus, one of the Cappadocian Fathers, chose his episcopal seat here in the 4th century to spread Christianity across Anatolia. The Büyük Kilise Cami, former St Gregory's Church built in 385, converted to a mosque in 1924, is the first monument past the entrance.

Accessible from the very entrance to Monastery Valley, this underground city is distinguished from the region's larger cities by its vertical connection system between levels (vertical shafts rather than horizontal galleries). It contains storage spaces, a rock-cut church, and rooms high enough to shelter camels, suggesting use as an underground caravanserai.

activities.text: The caravanserai is well enough preserved for you to roam freely through the courtyard, stalls, covered space and climb to observe the central mosque from its arches. Allow 45 to 60 minutes. The acoustics under the covered vault are particularly gripping: a rare chance to grasp what Silk Road hospitality meant.

The town of Güzelyurt preserves its houses carved into the rock and its Ottoman Greek facades, abandoned since the 1924 population exchange. Wander without a map: the alleyways climb towards a viewpoint over the valley with a view of Mount Hasan (3,268 m). In the village centre, the former Orthodox church converted to a mosque still serves Friday worshippers.

days_plan.itin_title: White cotton, whirling dervishes and fairy chimneys - 10 days from Antalya to Cappadocia

visits.text: Touristy but striking: only the minaret emerges from the waters of Halfeti since 2000, when the Birecik dam submerged 40% of the old village. The rest lies beneath the surface—mosque, stone houses, orchards. Halfeti joined the Cittaslow network in 2013. Skip the jet-skis that turn the experience into a theme park.

Grown only here, the black rose of Halfeti draws its color from a very particular microclimate along the Euphrates bank. The buds are almost black in spring, turning deep crimson as they mature. The gardens that still produce them can be visited near the old village. It's inspired several perfumes since the 2010s.

activities.text: Board a boat on the Euphrates for a 90-minute loop to Rumkale, an ancient Armenian fortress at the confluence with the Merzimen. Boats depart from the old village quay from 9am, without fixed schedule. Expect 100 to 150 TL per person. Choose a collective boat over a jet-ski: the silence of the river makes half the experience.

days_plan.itin_title: Turkish Mesopotamia: 11 Days from Gaziantep to the Tigris

visits.text: The dry silence of the Harran plain strikes you first. 44 km southeast of Şanlıurfa, a few kilometers from the Syrian border, the village remains one of the oldest known inhabited sites, mentioned in Genesis as Abraham's stop on the way to Canaan. The local language is still Arabic for many inhabitants, alongside Turkish and Kurdish.

Built between 744 and 750 by Marwan II, the Ulu Cami of Harran is considered the oldest mosque in Anatolia. Only the square minaret remains—atypical in Turkey—along with arches and foundations. The site also included the first Islamic university. All around, ruins of baths and cisterns. Access is free, ruined but open.

activities.text: Enter a beehive house: 4 to 5 meters high, in mud brick without a single wooden beam, with conical domes that insulate against heat. The Harran Kültür Evi tourist complex recreates the traditional way of life. Hot drink and flatbread offered, but plan for a tip. Allow 30 minutes; the visit is brief.

days_plan.itin_title: Turkish Mesopotamia: 11 Days from Gaziantep to the Tigris

places.name: Hasankeyf

visits.text: Hasankeyf is a difficult experience to classify. This town of 12,000 years of continuous occupation was partially drowned in 2020 by the Ilısu Dam on the Tigris. The old town - its cafés on stilts, medieval bridge, thousands of caves - now lies underwater. What remains visible: the citadel (Hasankeyf Kalesi), perched on its limestone spur 100 m above the new lake, and the New Hasankeyf Cultural Park, where eight major monuments were dismantled and rebuilt, including the Zeynel Bey Mausoleum (15th century, turquoise tiles of Iranian influence) and the El Rizk Mosque (1409). The citadel is reached by boat from the port, or by the mountain road circling the lake.

The New Hasankeyf Cultural Park groups the monuments saved from the flood and a museum documenting the town's history and the dam controversy. The north cliffs of the site reveal caves carved millennia ago, some still open to view. The silence reigning here today contrasts with what the town was - heartbreaking for anyone who knows its history.

activities.text: The boat crossing of the lake to reach the citadel is the only way to grasp the scale of the change. From the small ferry deck, you can see the tops of old houses emerging in places during low water, like urban ghosts. The local guide can explain what lies beneath the surface. A way of traveling in two eras at once.

visits.text: Helvadere isn't a destination per se, more the starting point for Mount Hasan and a photo stop on the steppe. The Seljuk village, modest, keeps a few stone mosques. 30 km south of Aksaray, it's the outpost for hikers aiming for the volcano's summit. A mountain lodge hosts trekkers.

Peaking at 3,268 m, Hasan Dağı is an imposing twin-peaked stratovolcano whose last eruption dates to about 6200 BC. This eruption would be depicted in a Neolithic fresco at Çatalhöyük, considered the first map or landscape of humanity.

activities.text: If you're an experienced hiker, count on 6 hours of walking one-way from the car-accessible refuge to reach the summit of Hasan Dağı. Trail marked in summer, but mountaineering gear required in winter. Aksaray's mountaineering festival runs every July. Otherwise, from Helvadere the view of the volcano is plenty.

days_plan.itin_title: Extended Cappadocia in 8 Days: Kayseri, Göreme, Konya

visits.text: Hopa marks the end of Turkey's coastal road, 18 km from the Sarp border crossing. A small port town with a mixed population of Turks, Laz, and border communities from the Caucasus. No major architectural interest, but strategically positioned for anyone continuing into Georgia. Two hours on-site, no more.

Hopa's harbor, built for trade with Georgia, livened up after the Sarp crossing opened on August 31, 1988. Russian and Georgian cargo ships still unload regularly. A short waterfront walk, working-class atmosphere, not touristy. Good tea terraces along the quay.

activities.text: Try anchovies in Hopa, the king dish of the eastern Black Sea coast. Hamsi pilavı (rice with anchovies), hamsi tava (pan-fried), anchovy bread: several lokantas along the waterfront serve these specialties at modest prices. Avoid high summer season—stocks collapse in summer.

days_plan.itin_title: Turkish Black Sea Road Trip: 8 Days from Istanbul to Hopa

visits.text: Declared a national park in 2007, İğneada Longoz Ormanları covers 3,155 ha of floodplain forests (longoz) – an ecosystem where trees grow with their feet in water part of the year. Rare in Turkey (only three longoz sites in the country), the park shelters seven forest lakes. Free entry, marked trails.

Mert Lake (Mert Gölü) reflects the alders with mirror-like precision when the wind drops. It's the most visited lake in İğneada national park, accessible 4 km from the town center. Bring water – no supply points inside the park.

activities.text: If you come in May or September, the Via Pontica – the great bird migration route between Northern Europe and Africa – passes directly over İğneada. Binoculars are essential: white storks, Dalmatian pelicans, and white-tailed eagles have been observed in the reserve.

days_plan.itin_title: Istanbul to Varna: Strandja and Black Sea Coast in 10 Days
Istanbul to Varna: Strandja and Black Sea Coast in 10 Days

visits.text: The shade of poplars drops the temperature several degrees as you descend the 400 steps into Ihlara Valley. The contrast with the steppe is striking: here water flows, orchards green, village women still wash their laundry in the Melendiz. The valley runs 14 km, count on 3 to 4 hours for the central section.

Stretching 14 km, Ihlara Valley shelters nearly sixty Byzantine rock churches from the 11th-13th centuries. About a dozen can be visited along the way. The finest frescoes are at Sümbüllü Kilise (Church of the Hyacinths) and Yılanlı Kilise (Church of the Serpents). Single entry ticket at the south entrance, 100 TL in 2026.

activities.text: Descend the 400 steps at the south entrance near Ihlara village, walk 3.5 km to Belisırma where floating restaurants serve trout and köfte on platforms above the river. You can go back by taxi (200 TL for 5 km) or continue 4 km to Selime Monastery. Count on 7-8h for the full route.

days_plan.itin_title: Extended Cappadocia in 8 Days: Kayseri, Göreme, Konya

visits.text: Isparta smells of roses the moment you step out of the car, especially in May-June. The province cultivates Rosa damascena for the global perfume trade and reportedly produces around 65% of the world's rose oil, according to official figures. Harvest is artisanal, by hand, at dawn when the flower is full of oil.

Isparta Museum houses carpets and kilims, testament to the town's textile heritage, along with Bronze Age artefacts. Also visit the 1561 Firdevs Paşa Mosque and the Atabey Ertokuş Medresesi, one of the oldest Seljuk structures in the region. Compact cluster; allow 2 hours on foot.

activities.text: If you're passing between mid-May and late June, head to Güneykent or Güneyköy 30 km southwest to join a dawn harvest. Several family distilleries offer visits with tastings. Count on 200 to 400 TL for a guided morning. It takes roughly 4 tonnes of petals to produce 1 kg of pure oil.

days_plan.itin_title: 7-Day Taurus: Turquoise Lakes & Lycian Cities

visits.text: Founded in 1891 by Osman Hamdi Bey to halt the dispersion of Ottoman antiquities, the Istanbul Archaeological Museum holds over one million objects. The centrepiece: the Alexander Sarcophagus (4th century BC) discovered at Sidon, carved with battle and hunting scenes with traces of original polychromy.

Cross the courtyard to enter the Tiled Kiosk (Çinili Köşk), built in 1472 by Mehmed II: it's the oldest surviving civilian Ottoman building in Istanbul. It exhibits Seljuk and Ottoman faience, including 16th-century floral panels from imperial mosques.

The Museum of the Ancient Orient, in the third building of the complex, displays cuneiform tablets including the Treaty of Kadesh (1259 BC)—the oldest known peace treaty, signed between Ramesses II and the Hittites—alongside a copy of the Code of Hammurabi.

activities.text: Step into the Museum of the History of Science and Technology in Islam, set in Gülhane Park: astronomical instruments, water clocks and mechanical devices from the 9th to 16th centuries are reconstructed at full scale.
Take a break in the archaeological museum's inner courtyard: the sarcophagi and ancient columns arranged in the open air form a free, peaceful sculpture garden.

days_plan.itin_title: Istanbul in 6 days: from sultans to the Bosphorus, neighbourhood by neighbourhood

visits.text: Descend the 52 steps and let your eyes adjust to the gloom: 336 columns, nine metres tall, support this subterranean space of 9,800 m². The Basilica Cistern (Yerebatan Sarnıcı), built by Justinian in 532, supplied drinking water to the Great Imperial Palace.

At the far end of the cistern, two columns rest on carved Medusa heads—one laid sideways, the other upside down. Their origin remains disputed. The cistern could hold 80,000 m³ of water piped from the Belgrade Forest via the Valens Aqueduct.

On your way up, note the Weeping Column (peacock-eye column), carved with teardrop motifs. It recalls a style similar to Trajan's Forum in Rome and may have been salvaged from a Roman monument during construction.

activities.text: Walk the wooden platforms between the columns bathed in shifting lights and reflections in the residual water—an excellent moment for tripod photography.

Back on the surface, walk up Divanyolu Caddesi, the ancient Roman triumphal route (Mese) linking the forum to the Great Palace. The Column of Constantine (Çemberlitaş), blackened by a 1779 fire, still stands midway, 35 metres tall.

days_plan.itin_title: Istanbul in 6 days: from sultans to the Bosphorus, neighbourhood by neighbourhood

visits.text: Built in 1871 by Nikoğos Balyan for Sultan Abdülaziz, Çırağan Palace stretches 300 metres along the Bosphorus. Gutted by fire in 1910, it was restored and converted into a hotel (Kempinski). Its white marble façade and gardens are accessible for a coffee with a view over the strait.

Walk up to Yıldız Park, Sultan Abdülhamid II's former private gardens, covering a wooded hill above the Bosphorus. The Sale Pavilion (Şale Köşkü), built to host Emperor Wilhelm II in 1889, contains the world's largest single-piece carpet (400 m²).

The Beşiktaş Naval Museum displays Ottoman imperial caïques—40-metre gilded ceremonial barges rowed by 144 oarsmen—alongside galley models and the sea charts of Admiral Piri Reis (16th century), the Ottoman cartographer whose 1513 world map shows the American coasts.

activities.text: Browse Beşiktaş Market, one of the rare authentic neighbourhood markets on the European shore, to sample cheeses, olives and Turkish pastries.
Finish the circuit from the Beşiktaş quays, çay in hand: on one side Dolmabahçe Palace, on the other the Bosphorus Bridge, and between them the strait where tankers and ferries pass.

days_plan.itin_title: Istanbul in 6 days: from sultans to the Bosphorus, neighbourhood by neighbourhood

visits.text: Six minarets pierce the sky above the Blue Mosque (Sultanahmet Camii), commissioned by Sultan Ahmed I in 1609 and completed in 1616. The count sparked controversy: only Mecca had as many, forcing the sultan to fund a seventh there to restore the hierarchy.

Enter through the north gate and let your eyes adjust: over 20,000 blue-and-white Iznik tiles line the walls, patterned with tulips, carnations and cypresses. The 260 windows bathe the prayer hall in soft light that makes the shades of blue shimmer—hence the nickname.

On the neighbouring Hippodrome, three ancient monuments still stand: the Obelisk of Theodosius (15th century BC, brought from Egypt in 390), the bronze Serpent Column (5th century BC, from Delphi) and the Walled Obelisk of Constantine VII. This 480-metre esplanade hosted chariot races before crowds of 100,000.

activities.text: Pause at the Fountain of Wilhelm II, gifted in 1901 by the German emperor: octagonal, crowned with a neo-Byzantine dome and adorned with golden mosaics inside.

Cross the square to enter the Museum of Turkish and Islamic Arts, housed in the former palace of Ibrahim Pasha (16th century). The collection of calligraphy and Anatolian carpets is one of the world's finest.

days_plan.itin_title: Istanbul in 6 days: from sultans to the Bosphorus, neighbourhood by neighbourhood

visits.text: Board the Şehir Hatları line at Eminönü and sail up the 32 kilometres of the Bosphorus to Anadolu Kavağı. The ferry passes the fortresses of Rumeli Hisarı (1452) and Anadolu Hisarı (1394), built by the Ottomans to choke Constantinople's supplies before the final assault.

Along the shores, yalıs (Ottoman wooden mansions) alternate with imperial palaces. The Yalı of Zarif Mustafa Pasha (18th century), painted red, and the Yalı of Amcazade Hüseyin Pasha (1699), the oldest survivor, testify to the art of living beside the strait.

At Anadolu Kavağı, the ferry's final stop, climb to the ruins of the Genoese Yoros Fortress for a view over the Bosphorus's outlet to the Black Sea. The fishing village at the foot of the hill serves grilled fish at prices well below downtown.

activities.text: On the return ferry, sit on the western side (left when facing forward) for the sunset behind the minarets of the European shore.
Lunch at Anadolu Kavağı in one of the harbour fish restaurants—grilled octopus mezze and whole turbot are local specialities.

days_plan.itin_title: Istanbul in 6 days: from sultans to the Bosphorus, neighbourhood by neighbourhood

visits.text: Inaugurated in 2019, the Grand Çamlıca Mosque (Büyük Çamlıca Camii) is Turkey's largest. Its main dome rises to 72 metres (symbolising Istanbul's 72 nations) with a diameter of 34 metres (the city's vehicle code). Designed by two female architects, Bahar Mızrak and Hayriye Gül Totu, it can accommodate 63,000 worshippers.

Çamlıca Hill (Büyük Çamlıca Tepesi), Istanbul's highest point at 267 metres, offers a panorama over the Bosphorus, both shores and the Sea of Marmara islands. The pine-wooded park surrounding it hosts tea gardens frequented by Istanbul families for generations.

Built in 1865 by Sarkis Balyan in Ottoman Neoclassical style, Beylerbeyi Palace served as the sultans' summer residence. Its interiors blend Bohemian chandeliers, Hereke carpets and European furniture. Empress Eugénie of France and the Duke of Windsor stayed here.

activities.text: Visit the Museum of Islamic Civilisations, opened in 2022 in the Çamlıca Mosque complex. Allow at least two hours to walk through its thematic galleries.
Settle into a Çamlıca tea garden at sunset: the Bosphorus turns gold between two continents, with the minarets of the European shore in silhouette.

days_plan.itin_title: Istanbul in 6 days: from sultans to the Bosphorus, neighbourhood by neighbourhood

visits.text: Completed in 1856 by architect Nikoğos Balyan, Dolmabahçe Palace stretches 600 metres along the Bosphorus. The last palace of the Ottoman sultans, it blends Baroque, Rococo and Neoclassical styles. Its Throne Room houses a 4.5-tonne Bohemian crystal chandelier gifted by Queen Victoria—the world's largest.

The Dolmabahçe Harem, separate from the main palace, comprises the imperial family's apartments furnished with European furniture and Hereke carpets. Atatürk spent his final days here and died on 10 November 1938 at 9:05—the time at which all the palace clocks remain stopped.

The neo-Baroque Clock Tower (27 metres), erected in 1895 facing the main entrance, served as a time marker for Bosphorus boatmen. Its four dials bear Roman and Arabic numerals, reflecting the dual European and Ottoman influences of the era.

activities.text: Walk the gardens between the palace and the Bosphorus to photograph the ornate grilles and the pleasure pavilions at the water's edge.
Continue to the Beşiktaş quarter for a çay by the water, facing the ferries criss-crossing the strait between two continents.

days_plan.itin_title: Istanbul in 6 days: from sultans to the Bosphorus, neighbourhood by neighbourhood

visits.text: Start with the Chora Mosque (Kariye Camii), a former 11th-century Byzantine church: its mosaics and frescoes from the 14th century rank among the finest achievements of late Byzantine art. The narrative cycles illustrate the lives of Christ and the Virgin with unprecedented realism for the period.

The Walls of Theodosius II, raised between 413 and 439, stretch 6.5 kilometres from the Sea of Marmara to the Golden Horn. This triple fortification system—moat, outer wall, 12-metre inner wall punctuated by 96 towers—defended Constantinople for a millennium.

Eyüp Sultan Mosque houses the tomb of Abu Ayyub al-Ansari, a companion of the Prophet who died during the first Arab siege of Constantinople in 674–678. It was here that Ottoman sultans girded the sword of Osman during their enthronement—the equivalent of European coronation.

activities.text: Take the cable car up to the Pierre Loti Café, perched above the Eyüp Ottoman cemetery. The view over the Golden Horn between the cypresses is one of the city's most iconic.
Walk down through the old Eyüp Ottoman cemetery, one of Istanbul's oldest. The turbaned headstones indicate the deceased's rank in the Ottoman hierarchy.

days_plan.itin_title: Istanbul in 6 days: from sultans to the Bosphorus, neighbourhood by neighbourhood

visits.text: Fener was the heart of the Greek Orthodox community after 1453 and still houses the Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople, spiritual seat of 300 million Orthodox worldwide. The small complex of modest buildings on the main street contrasts with the institution's importance.

Balat, the old Jewish quarter where Sephardim expelled from Spain settled in 1492, is best explored by climbing its steep lanes lined with colourful wooden houses—pink, yellow, blue—now one of Istanbul's most photographed backdrops.

The Bulgarian Church of St Stephen (Sveti Stefan), completed in 1898, is one of the rare churches built entirely from prefabricated cast iron in Vienna then assembled on site. Its iron components, totalling 500 tonnes, were transported via the Danube and the Black Sea.

activities.text: Climb to the Greek Lyceum of Fener (Megali Scholi), an imposing red-brick building from 1881 dominating the Golden Horn—the view from the steps is worth the effort.
Settle into one of Balat's café-galleries that have moved into renovated houses on Vodina Street. The neighbourhood has transformed in five years with the arrival of these creative studios.

days_plan.itin_title: Istanbul in 6 days: from sultans to the Bosphorus, neighbourhood by neighbourhood

visits.text: Built in 1348 by the Genoese as the Christea Turris, Galata Tower rises 67 metres. Its circular balcony offers a 360° panorama over Sultanahmet, the Golden Horn, the Bosphorus and the Asian shore. The Genoese quarter of Galata was an autonomous, prosperous merchant colony facing Constantinople.

The Galata Mevlevi Museum (Galata Mevlevihanesi), a former whirling dervish lodge founded in 1491, is Istanbul's oldest Mevlevi tekke. Sema ceremonies (whirling dance) are performed in an octagonal hall adorned with calligraphy.

Walk down the steep lanes towards Karaköy, the old quarter of bankers and merchants. The contemporary art space SALT Galata, housed in the former Ottoman Bank (1892), offers exhibitions and a research library on the Empire's history.

activities.text: Browse the designer boutiques and speciality cafés of Karaköy, now the European shore's trendiest neighbourhood.
Order a künefe (hot cheese-and-syrup dessert) at one of the Mumhane Street artisans—the cheese should stretch when you cut the first slice.

days_plan.itin_title: Istanbul in 6 days: from sultans to the Bosphorus, neighbourhood by neighbourhood

visits.text: Get lost in the 66 covered streets of the Grand Bazaar (Kapalıçarşı), founded in 1461 under Mehmed II. Over 4,000 shops line painted vaults and stone domes across 30,000 m²: it's the world's oldest and one of the largest covered markets.

Each street bears the name of its trade guild: Kalpakçılar for jewellers, Perdahçılar for coppersmiths, Halıcılar for carpet merchants. The Beyazıt and Nuruosmaniye gates are the main entrances to this labyrinth where haggling is a codified art.

Just beyond the west exit, the Book Market of Sahaflar (Sahaflar Çarşısı) occupies an open courtyard used by papermakers since Byzantine times. You can still find antiquarian books, Ottoman calligraphy and Persian miniatures.

activities.text: Step into Beyazıt Mosque (1506), the oldest imperial mosque still in use in Istanbul: its 20-column portico of granite and porphyry leads to a peaceful courtyard.
Scout by theme without purchase pressure: hammered copper, Iznik ceramics, mosaic lamps, leather, spices. Bargaining always begins with a çay offered by the seller.

days_plan.itin_title: Istanbul in 6 days: from sultans to the Bosphorus, neighbourhood by neighbourhood

visits.text: Push through the Imperial Gate of Hagia Sophia and look up: the dome, 31 metres in diameter, soars 56 metres above the floor, carried by four pendentives. Built between 532 and 537 under Justinian I by Anthemius of Tralles and Isidore of Miletus, this engineering masterpiece defined monumental architecture for a millennium.

In the upper gallery, Byzantine mosaics from the 9th to 12th centuries stand alongside Ottoman calligraphic medallions 7.5 metres in diameter. The 13th-century Deësis, depicting Christ flanked by the Virgin and St John the Baptist, remains one of the peaks of late Byzantine art.

Orthodox cathedral for 916 years, mosque for 482 years, museum from 1934 to 2020, then mosque again: the building encapsulates Istanbul's history in stone. Its 40 windows at the dome's base create a floating-light effect that impressed medieval visitors just as it does today.

activities.text: Slide your thumb into the copper-ringed hole of the Weeping Column and rotate your finger a full circle without pulling it out—tradition promises your wish will be granted.
On your way out, turn back to study the Ottoman ablutions fountain and the buttresses Sinan added in the 16th century to prevent the structure from collapsing.
Settle down with a çay on a bench in Sultanahmet Park, facing the façade: at sunset, the stone takes on a golden hue that transforms the building's appearance.

days_plan.itin_title: Istanbul in 6 days: from sultans to the Bosphorus, neighbourhood by neighbourhood

visits.text: Running 1.4 kilometres between Taksim Square and Galata Tower, Istiklal Avenue (Istiklal Caddesi) is traversed by the red nostalgic tram. Three million people pass through daily, among 19th-century embassies, Art Nouveau arcades and theatres.

Step into the Basilica of St Anthony of Padua (1912), Istanbul's largest Catholic church. Its neo-Gothic red-brick façade and vaulted interior contrast with the surrounding mosques, testifying to Beyoğlu's religious diversity.

The Çiçek Pasajı (Flower Passage) occupies the former Cité de Péra (1876). Its glass-roofed taverns (meyhane) are the traditional venue for rakı and mezze. The adjacent Fish Market (Balık Pazarı) brims with seafood stalls and lively little restaurants.

activities.text: Head up to Taksim Square to see the Republic Monument (1928), the work of Italian sculptor Pietro Canonica, commemorating the foundation of the Turkish Republic in 1923.

Explore the Atlas Passage and the independent cinemas of Istiklal: Beyoğlu remains the heart of Istanbul's alternative cultural scene, with screenings, galleries and bookshops in every covered arcade.

days_plan.itin_title: Istanbul in 6 days: from sultans to the Bosphorus, neighbourhood by neighbourhood

visits.text: Kadıköy is ancient Chalcedon, founded in 685 BC by the Megarians, seventeen years before Byzantium. It was nicknamed the 'city of the blind' because its founders supposedly failed to see the superior site of the Golden Horn opposite. Today it's the liveliest neighbourhood on the Asian shore.

Dive into the Fish Market (Kadıköy Balıkçılar Pazarı), a maze of alleys lined with stalls selling fish, olives, cheese, spices and dried fruit. This is where Istanbulites do their shopping, not in the tourist quarters. Güneşli Bahçe Street is home to Çiya Sofrası, a regional Anatolian restaurant open since the 1980s.

Built in 1927 by politician Süreyya İlmen Pasha in Art Deco style, the Süreyya Opera is the Asian shore's leading performance venue. Bahariye Street leading to it, traversed by the nostalgic tram, is the main shopping artery—often compared to Istiklal on the European side.

activities.text: Head down to Moda and walk the coastal promenade to Moda Park: the view stretches over the Sea of Marmara and the silhouette of Sultanahmet opposite.
Explore Kadife Sokak (Bar Street), the heart of Kadıköy's alternative nightlife, lined with live bars and independent clubs like Arkaoda, open since 1999.
Order a Turkish coffee at Fazıl Bey, a neighbourhood institution for decades, in Kadıköy market.

days_plan.itin_title: Istanbul in 6 days: from sultans to the Bosphorus, neighbourhood by neighbourhood

visits.text: Ortaköy Mosque (Büyük Mecidiye Camii), built in 1856 by the same architect as Dolmabahçe, is a Baroque Ottoman jewel set beside the Bosphorus. Its white façade topped by two slender minarets stands out against the 15 July Martyrs Bridge (former Bosphorus Bridge), creating Istanbul's most reproduced postcard view.

Ortaköy Square, buzzing with outdoor cafés and craft stalls, hosts a weekend market. The kumpir (giant stuffed baked potato with choice toppings) sold on the quays is a local speciality that has become a neighbourhood ritual.

Walk up the coast towards Kuruçeşme and Arnavutköy to admire the painted wooden Ottoman yalıs at the water's edge. The village of Arnavutköy, with its fishermen's houses lined up facing the strait, has kept a village feel despite the proximity of downtown.

activities.text: Try a kumpir on the Ortaköy quays: vendors line up dozens of toppings in trays—cheese, olives, sweetcorn, sausage, salad—and stuff the potato in front of you.
In the evening, the waterfront bars and clubs of Ortaköy come alive with a direct view of the illuminated bridge, which changes colour every hour.

days_plan.itin_title: Istanbul in 6 days: from sultans to the Bosphorus, neighbourhood by neighbourhood

visits.text: Büyükada (5.4 km²), the largest of the nine Princes' Islands (Adalar), is a car-free archipelago in the Sea of Marmara. The islands served as a place of exile for disgraced Byzantine princes and aristocrats. Trotsky lived here four years (1929–1933) in a 950 m² mansion now in ruins, where he wrote his History of the Russian Revolution.

Climb to the Greek Orthodox monastery of Aya Yorgi (St George), perched atop Büyükada's southern hill at 188 metres altitude. An active pilgrimage site since 1751, the path is dotted with ribbons tied to trees by pilgrims making a wish on the way up.

Heybeliada, the archipelago's second island, houses the Orthodox Halki Seminary (1844), closed since 1971 by Turkish authorities but still symbolically important to 300 million Orthodox Christians. Its pine forests cover the hills and run down to the coves on the southern coast.

activities.text: Rent a bike at the Büyükada jetty and cycle round the island (about 1h30) via the beaches, Victorian wooden villas and pine groves.
Lunch at a fish restaurant in Büyükada harbour, trying the stuffed mussels (midye dolma) and balık ekmek, island specialities.
Visit the Princes' Islands Museum (Adalar Müzesi), housed in a former warehouse east of Büyükada, to understand the archipelago's multicultural history—Byzantine, Ottoman, Greek, Jewish, Armenian.

days_plan.itin_title: Istanbul in 6 days: from sultans to the Bosphorus, neighbourhood by neighbourhood

visits.text: Built in 1660, the Spice Bazaar (Mısır Çarşısı) is Istanbul's second-largest covered market. Its L-shaped plan shelters 85 shops piled with spices, Turkish delight, teas, dried fruits and caviar. It owes its name (Egyptian Bazaar) to the taxes on Egyptian goods that funded the neighbouring mosque.

Completed in 1665 after 66 years of construction interrupted by wars and unrest, the New Mosque (Yeni Cami) dominates Eminönü Square. Its silhouette—two minarets and a cascade of semi-domes—has become the defining image of Istanbul's waterfront.

Climb the narrow staircase to the tiny Rüstem Pasha Mosque (1563), signed by Sinan. Every interior surface is covered with Istanbul's finest Iznik tiles: crimson tulips, carnations and geometric patterns on white-and-blue ground.

activities.text: Order a balık ekmek (grilled fish sandwich) on the Eminönü quays, facing the Galata Bridge where fishermen cast their lines from morning to night.
Cross the Galata Bridge on the lower level: fish restaurants with Golden Horn views occupy the entire length of the deck beneath the roadway.

days_plan.itin_title: Istanbul in 6 days: from sultans to the Bosphorus, neighbourhood by neighbourhood

visits.text: Completed in 1557 by Sinan for Suleiman the Magnificent, Süleymaniye Mosque crowns one of Istanbul's seven hills. Its four minarets with ten balconies encode the sultan's place in Ottoman history: fourth sultan of Istanbul, tenth of the dynasty.

The complex (külliye) extends far beyond the mosque: tombs of Suleiman and his wife Roxelana (Hürrem Sultan), four madrasas, a hospital, a caravanserai, a hamam and a public kitchen (imaret). Sinan himself is buried in a modest tomb in the north-east corner of the garden.

Approach Sinan's tomb (türbe), a small open-air stone enclosure, modest compared to the scale of his oeuvre: over 300 buildings across the Ottoman Empire, including 84 mosques. An inscription records that he lived almost a century (1489–1588).

activities.text: From the terrace of the funerary garden, the view plunges over the Golden Horn and the Galata shore—one of the city's best free viewpoints.
In the street below, try the white beans (kuru fasulye) and rice pilavı at the traditional restaurants backed against the mosque wall. These canteens haven't changed their recipes in decades.

days_plan.itin_title: Istanbul in 6 days: from sultans to the Bosphorus, neighbourhood by neighbourhood

visits.text: Pass through the Imperial Gate (Bab-ı Hümayun) and cross four successive courtyards: Topkapı Palace served as the residence of Ottoman sultans from 1465 to 1856. This 70-hectare complex housed up to 4,000 people—pages, eunuchs and women of the Imperial Harem.

In the Treasury, the 86-carat Spoonmaker's Diamond gleams behind its armoured case, beside the Topkapı Dagger encrusted with three massive emeralds and the golden throne of Murad III. The Sacred Relics chamber preserves the cloak and sword attributed to the Prophet Muhammad.

Take the corridor to the Harem: over 400 rooms decorated with Iznik tiles in floral patterns. The apartments of the Valide Sultan (the sultan's mother) and the domed Imperial Hall illustrate the power women wielded in Ottoman politics.

activities.text: Descend into Gülhane Park, the palace's former garden planted with centuries-old plane trees, for an unobstructed view over the Golden Horn and the Sea of Marmara.
Head to the terrace of the fourth courtyard to photograph the confluence of the Bosphorus, the Golden Horn and the Sea of Marmara—all three visible in a single panorama.

days_plan.itin_title: Istanbul in 6 days: from sultans to the Bosphorus, neighbourhood by neighbourhood

visits.text: Set on a raised terrace facing the jetty, Mihrimah Sultan Mosque in Üsküdar (completed 1548) is Sinan's first work to use the concept of the floating dome. Commissioned by Princess Mihrimah, daughter of Suleiman and Roxelana, its central dome flanked by three semi-domes once rose directly above the water, before the coastal road was built.

Take the boat from Salacak to reach the Maiden's Tower (Kız Kulesi), on its islet at the entrance to the Bosphorus. The first tower was erected in 408 BC by the Athenian general Alcibiades. Successively lighthouse, customs post, lazaretto and fortification, it now houses a museum and restaurant.

The Çinili Mosque (1640), commissioned by Kösem Sultan, one of the most powerful women in Ottoman history, is lined with blue-and-turquoise Iznik tiles in floral patterns. The adjacent Çinili Hamam still operates as a traditional Turkish bath.

activities.text: Stroll along the Salacak waterfront: the view over Sultanahmet, Hagia Sophia and the Blue Mosque from the Asian shore is Istanbul's most photographed.
Walk up to the garden of Fethipaşa Korusu, a wooded park overlooking the Bosphorus where Üsküdar families come to drink çay under the trees.
Explore Kuzguncuk, a former cosmopolitan village where synagogue, Armenian church, Orthodox church and mosque coexist on the same street—a microcosm of Istanbul's multicultural past.

days_plan.itin_title: Istanbul in 6 days: from sultans to the Bosphorus, neighbourhood by neighbourhood

visits.text: These ramparts have stood for 17 centuries—you won't forget them. İznik's Byzantine fortifications encircle the old town for about 5 km, with 114 towers and four major gates (Istanbul, Lefke, Izmit, Yalova). The inner wall, erected toward the end of the 3rd century under Claudius II, stands 9 meters high. Some sections are under restoration—notably the Istanbul Gate.

Orhan Cami was first a 4th-century church—tradition holds that the Seventh Ecumenical Council was held here. Converted to a mosque, then a museum, then back to a mosque in 2020 under the name İznik Hagia Sophia, it retains marble mosaics at the entrance and barely visible fresco fragments. Prayer is central; all around, visitors circulate freely.

The Green Mosque's minaret: dark green glazed tiles—the direct ancestors of the İznik ceramics that conquered the Ottoman Empire. Erected on commission by Çandarlı Halil Paşa, vizier to Sultan Murat, it's one of the earliest examples of Ottoman architecture. The elegant white marble porch contrasts with the nave's sobriety.

World-famous for its ceramics, but few travelers know the production had almost disappeared. İznik Vakfı workshops relaunched the tradition from the 1990s. The municipal museum displays authentic 16th-17th century Ottoman examples—the quality difference from current copies is immediately visible.

activities.text: If you're passing in summer, Lake İznik, the largest in the Marmara region, offers natural access from the shore for swimming. The water is fresh and clean. In 2014, a 4th-century Byzantine basilica was discovered just 20 meters from the shore, collapsed during the 740 earthquake.

Several workshops in town have perpetuated Ottoman İznik ceramics since the 1990s. İznik Vakfı shops are the most reliable for distinguishing real quartz-based ceramics from industrial imitations. An introductory tile-painting session is possible by reservation.

days_plan.itin_title: Black Sea & Safranbolu: 9 days village to village

visits.text: Local legend places Heracles' descent to the Underworld here to capture Cerberus. The Cehennemağzı caves—'the Mouth of Hell'—consist of three cavities, two carved by human hands and used as Christian sanctuaries in Roman times. Opened to the public in 2001; the geometric floor mosaics in the first cave are remarkable.

An industrial city at first glance, but Heraclea Pontica hides beneath its streets the remains of a Greek colony founded around 560 BC. The harbor ramparts and citadel are partially accessible. Some sections remain in military zones—check before approaching.

days_plan.itin_title: Black Sea & Safranbolu: 9 days village to village

visits.text: Less known than Göbekli Tepe, Karahantepe is still worth the archaeological detour: it's a twin site, also dated around 9400 BC, part of the Taş Tepeler (stone hills) network. Excavations began in 2019 and are advancing quickly. You can watch the workers at work from the walkways.

Under the protective hall, the eleven anthropomorphic pillars of Karahantepe's central sanctuary surround a relief figure: a human face carved directly into the rock, which doesn't exist at Göbekli Tepe. The elliptical building measures 28 meters wide. Less than 5% of the site has been cleared. Everything is still fresh; the effect is striking.

activities.text: If you've seen both sites, return to the Şanlıurfa Archaeology Museum: major pieces from Göbekli Tepe and Karahantepe are displayed there, including a replica of Sanctuary D and carved human heads. It's more complete than an on-site audio guide, especially for understanding the iconography of the reliefs.

days_plan.itin_title: Turkish Mesopotamia: 11 Days from Gaziantep to the Tigris

places.name: Kars

visits.text: Kars Citadel dominates the town from a black basalt promontory. Its origins likely date to the Armenians of the Bagratid era (9th century), before being successively rebuilt by the Seljuks (1153), destroyed by Tamerlane (1386), and restored by the Ottomans (1579). Its military history is dense: besieged by Russians, contested during World War I, finally integrated into the Turkish Republic in 1920. Access to the upper courtyard is free and the views over the city and river are frank and beautiful.

Kars's Church of the Holy Apostles (Kümbet Camii) was built in the 10th century by Bagratid King Abas I when Kars was still the Armenian capital. Its twelve bas-reliefs depicting the apostles are still visible on the dome's drum - a rarity for a building that has been successively converted into a mosque, a Russian Orthodox cathedral, a museum, and again a mosque in 1993. This building's history summarizes Kars's history in itself.

Kars's Russian quarter is a uniqueness in Turkey. From 1878 to 1918, the city was a town of the Russian Empire, and its Baltic-style neoclassical buildings - wide avenues, dressed-stone facades, steep roofs - differ radically from the rest of eastern Turkey. Kars is also one of the few eastern cities where you'll easily find beer and wine in normal shops.

Ani is a ghost city UNESCO-listed since 2016, former capital of the Armenian Bagratid kingdom. At its peak in the 11th century, it counted between 100,000 and 200,000 inhabitants and rivaled Constantinople and Baghdad. The Mongols (1236), a catastrophic earthquake (1319), and the shift of trade routes emptied it of substance. Today a windswept plateau, a few square kilometers of ruins on a promontory that the Akhurian gorge (Turkish-Armenian border) cuts knife-sharp. You can see Armenia 500 meters away. The site is visited on foot (5-6 km for the complete circuit). Allow at least 3 hours.

Ani Cathedral (1001) is the site's centerpiece - built by architect Trdat, the same who repaired the dome of Constantinople's Hagia Sophia. Its pointed arches and compound pillars appear in Europe only from the 12th century onward: some scholars think Gothic architecture drew inspiration from it. The dome collapsed during the 1319 earthquake and was never rebuilt. Entering, look for the star and cross markings in the stone.

The Church of St. Gregory of Tigran Honents (1215) is the best-preserved and most ornate on the site. Commissioned by a wealthy merchant, its interior walls are covered with frescoes floor to ceiling. The entrance is marked by bas-reliefs above the portal. It's the monument at Ani that offers the fullest vision of medieval Armenian artistic wealth.

activities.text: Kars is known for its kaşar cheese and mountain honey. The covered market sells aged cheese produced in the neighboring Caucasus pastures. If you're here December-March, Kars is the starting point of the Doğu Ekspresi (Eastern Express), the overnight train Istanbul-Kars that's become a tourist phenomenon in Turkey for its snowy landscapes.

From the cliffs overlooking the Akhurian, the view plunges onto the gorge and Armenia opposite. The remnants of the medieval bridge that once spanned the river are visible below - broken arches, symbol of a connection severed for centuries between two countries that still share no open border. It's one of the most emotionally charged viewpoints of the entire itinerary.

visits.text: Antiphellos's Hellenistic theater is carved into the hillside west of the center, 500 m from the main square. Built in the 1st century BC from local limestone, its 26 rows of tiers could accommodate about 4,000 spectators - and never had a permanent stage building, to preserve the view of the sea. Free access. Sunset from the upper tiers, with Kastellorizo on the horizon, is one of the most beautiful moments of the journey. Plan a good hour here.

The Lion Tomb (4th century BC) sits on a pedestal right in the middle of downtown, among restaurants and shops. Its lion heads sculpted at the roof corners and its Lycian language inscription (not yet translated) testify to its occupant's importance. Higher up on Uzun Çarşı street, the King's Tomb, also from the 4th century BC, displays a long poetic inscription in Lycian B (Milyan) - an older Lycian dialect, undeciphered. The rock-cut tombs in the cliffs northeast of town add another layer to this palimpsest.

Kaş has managed to remain a human-scale town despite its success. Cobbled lanes, bougainvillea spilling from terraces, small jewelry workshops, and dive shops give the place a particular atmosphere. The main square (Cumhuriyet Meydanı) stays lively until late in the evening. If you're here on a Friday, residents from Kastellorizo cross over to sell their products at the market behind the bus station.

activities.text: The Greek island of Kastellorizo (Meis in Turkish) is only 6 km from Kaş harbor: the crossing takes 20 minutes. It's Greece's easternmost inhabited island. It has about 500 permanent residents, a picturesque harbor with colorful neoclassical houses, and a blue cave accessible only from the sea. Visiting Meis from Kaş also means seeing the Turkish coast from Greece - a striking shift in perspective.

Kaş is considered one of Turkey's best diving destinations. Sites include a World War II plane wreck, ancient amphorae scattered on the seabed, coral formations, and underwater tunnels. Even snorkeling from a gulet, the water visibility is exceptional.

days_plan.itin_title: Turkey End to End: The Complete 25-Day Grand Tour

visits.text: The first mosque-madrasa-mausoleum complex built by a Seljuk sultanate in Anatolia, Kayseri's Hunat Hatun Complex marks the turning point toward the great urban külliye. The ensemble groups the mosque, madrasa, and mausoleum of Mahperi Hatun, built in the 13th century near the Byzantine castle. The madrasa today houses an ethnography museum. Allow 1 hour on-site, modest admission.

Small but undeniably perfect in its proportions, Kayseri's Döner Kümbet dates from 1276. Seljuk tomb with dodecagonal plan, conical dome, fine sculpture on the drum. Located on a roundabout, 15 minutes on foot from the center. Half an hour is enough, but don't miss it while passing through.

At the foot of Mount Erciyes, a dormant volcano reaching 3,916 meters, Kayseri spreads across the plain at 1,050 m altitude. The snowy summit dominates the city most of the year. The cable car at the Hisarcık ski area climbs to 2,200 m, open even off-season. Panoramic view over all of Central Anatolia.

activities.text: Taste Kayseri's mantı and pastırma, two local prides. Mantı: tiny beef ravioli, garlic yogurt sauce and spiced red butter. Pastırma: dried beef wrapped in spices (çemen). Several delicatessens near the covered bazaar sell vacuum-packed slices to take away.

The Museum of Seljuk Civilizations, the former 13th-century Gevher Nesibe Madrasa, recounts the history of a hospital that treated mental illness with music and the sound of water. Modern scenography, educational exhibition. Allow 1 hour. While there, the neighboring archaeological museum holds cuneiform tablets from Kültepe.

places.name: Kemer

visits.text: Phaselis's Roman aqueduct is the first thing you see arriving at the site. Its limestone arches rise among the pines, barely 50 meters past the entrance. Water came from a spring in the hill behind the north harbor and crossed the entire city to the agora. It's one of the best-preserved Roman hydraulic systems on the Mediterranean coast.

Phaselis's grand avenue is a 10-meter-wide paved street linking the north harbor to the south harbor. It was lined with columns, shops, and fountains. In 129 AD, Emperor Hadrian visited the city and residents erected a triumphal gate at the main harbor entrance - Hadrian's Gate. Ships of 100 tons traded cedar wood and rose oil here, commodities that made Phaselis wealthy.

Phaselis theater (2nd century AD, 1,500 to 2,000 seats) sits on a hillside above the central harbor. From its tiers, the view is striking: to the north, the summit of Tahtalı (2,365 m) dominates the entire landscape. It's the same mountain the Lycians called Olympos, which you can reach this afternoon. A secured wooden walkway provides access to the tiers.

The Olympos cable car (Olympos Teleferik) links the lower station at 596 m to the summit in 10 minutes via a 4.3 km cable - one of Europe's longest. The arrival station is at 2,365 m. The view from the summit is 360° panoramic: the Turquoise Coast, Greek islands, and on clear days as far as Cyprus. In summer, clouds often fill the intermediate levels that the cable car passes through. Bring a jacket even in midsummer: it can be 15°C colder than below.

activities.text: Phaselis's three coves are accessible from the archaeological site. The north harbor beach is the calmest; the south harbor beach (the former military cove) is the most beautiful - sandy bottom, translucent water. You swim among blocks of Roman quays visible under 1 to 2 meters of water. It's one of the most photogenic swims on the Turquoise Coast.

visits.text: Not spectacular, but these Quaternary-era cliffs dropping into the sea at Kerpe Kayalıkları have something honest about them. Kocaeli municipality has developed the waterfront with a row of cafés before the rocks. Young people dive from heights of 15 to 20 meters—guaranteed spectacle from the promenade.

activities.text: If you arrive on a weekday, the bay is nearly deserted—Istanbul and Kocaeli residents only invade the beaches on weekends. The water is calmer here than at Şile. The fine sand beach runs about 200 meters. Good transition stop before the wilder coast that begins at Akçakoca.

days_plan.itin_title: Black Sea & Safranbolu: 9 days village to village

visits.text: The Archaeological and Ethnographic Museum of Kırklareli displays discoveries from the Aşağı Pınar tumulus, one of Europe's earliest organized settlements dating to prehistory. The first-floor ethnographic section offers a glimpse into Eastern Thracian traditions. Closed Mondays.

The Hızırbey Complex (Hızırbey Külliyesi) – mosque-bazaar-hammam – dates from 1383 and was erected by Köse Mihalzade Hızırbey. The ensemble on the central square is one of the rare preserved examples of early Ottoman architecture in Turkish Thrace.

activities.text: Take Mustafa Kemal boulevard toward the covered market – Kırklareli is renowned for its hardaliye, a non-alcoholic fermented drink made from red grapes and crushed mustard seeds. Sample it in market groceries, take home in bottles.

days_plan.itin_title: Istanbul to Varna: Strandja and Black Sea Coast in 10 Days

visits.text: Morning mist clings to the wooden houses of Kıyıköy (ancient Medea) above the Black Sea. The village retains its medieval ramparts and a few sloped lanes not yet disfigured. Population around 2,500 lives on fishing and summer tourism.

Discreet, almost ignored by guidebooks, the rock-cut Saint Nicholas Monastery (Aya Nikola) is carved into the hillside cliff, a Byzantine-era legacy. Accessible on foot from the village via a forest trail of about 20 min. Wear closed shoes – the path can be muddy.

activities.text: Head to the harbor before dawn to watch the boats come in. The river emptying into the Black Sea just north of the port offers rowboat rentals in season. Peaceful atmosphere, cheap grilled fish on return.

days_plan.itin_title: Istanbul to Varna: Strandja and Black Sea Coast in 10 Days

visits.text: They call it 'the Ephesus of the Black Sea'—and the theater of Prusias ad Hypium, 95% excavated, once held some 10,000 spectators. Dating from the 3rd century BC, its semicircular 100-meter tiers still bear relief sculptures of Apollo and Actaeon. Ongoing excavations regularly yield new finds.

Founded in 1994, Konuralp Museum houses 1,788 archaeological pieces from Prusias ad Hypium excavations—including a mosaic of Orpheus, one of Achilles and Thetis, and a 1st-century sarcophagus. Small but dense. Closed Mondays; allow 30 to 45 minutes for a complete visit.

days_plan.itin_title: Black Sea & Safranbolu: 9 days village to village

visits.text: Identified and excavated in 2010, this three-aisled 4th-century basilica is the seventh of the seven churches of Asia mentioned in the Book of Revelation. Its north and south aisles are paved with mosaics. The baptistry is in opus sectile. It's now one of the main Christian pilgrimage sites in Turkey.

Built in the Hellenistic period and recently restored (2022), this theatre held 8,000 spectators. It was used until the 7th century AD. From the cavea, you can glimpse the white terraces of Pamukkale in the distance, a view that was already worth the journey for ancient inhabitants.

The western agora covers 35,000 m². Its back wall, 100 metres long and 11 metres high, was covered with frescoes - a rare case for a commercial agora. The colonnaded street bordering it was the city's main north-south artery, 1,500 metres long.

The former tekke (monastery) of the Mevlevi dervishes houses the tomb of Jalaluddin Rumi, covered with an enormous turban symbolising his spiritual authority. The turquoise dome dates from 1274. The museum preserves 13th-century manuscripts, ancient carpets, musical instruments used during sema ceremonies, and Rumi's personal belongings. The Semahane - the hall where the ritual dance was performed - is also visible. Remove your shoes and cover your head (women) before entering the mausoleum.

Built in 1221 under Sultan Alaeddin Keykubad, this mosque is one of Turkey's oldest Seljuk mosques. It crowns Alaeddin Hill, a Neolithic tell inhabited for 7,000 years, which later became the citadel of the Seljuk kings. In the neighbouring enclosure lie the tombs of eight Seljuk sultans as well as the remains of the 12th-century royal palace.

This Seljuk theological school built in 1251 by vizier Celaleddin Karatay now houses the Museum of Ceramic Tiles. The highlight of the visit is the ceiling of the central hall: a dome entirely covered with blue and white star-patterned tiles from the 13th century. The entrance portal is a classic example of Seljuk carved stone ornamentation.

This other 13th-century Seljuk medrese owes its name to its slender minaret decorated with glazed bricks. Now a lapidary museum, it displays stone and wood sculptures from the Seljuk period, including animal reliefs recovered from the city walls.

activities.text: Laodicea is a living archaeological site: some areas are being restored, others have just reopened. Allow 1.5 to 2 hours to see the essentials. The audio guide available on site helps distinguish what's ancient from what's been reconstructed - the difference isn't always obvious at first glance.

The route via Isparta and Lake Eğirdir lengthens the journey slightly but crosses one of Turkey's most beautiful mountain lakes, ringed by pines and orchards. Lake Eğirdir sits at about 1,000 metres altitude - a chance to take a lakeside lunch break before resuming the drive to Konya.

Sema ceremonies - the ritual meditation dance of the Mevlevi dervishes, listed as UNESCO intangible heritage - take place on Saturday evenings at the Mevlana Cultural Centre (Mevlana Kültür Merkezi), near the museum. In summer (June-September), free ceremonies are held on Thursday evenings in the Mevlana Museum garden. Arrive 30 minutes early to find a seat.

Konya is famous for two specialties: etli ekmek, a thin flatbread topped with minced meat and baked in a wood-fired oven (eat with your fingers, that's the local rule), and fırın kebabı, lamb slowly braised in the oven. Restaurants around the central square also serve tantuni (cumin-spiced sautéed meat in thin bread) and various honey pastries.

visits.text: Once a supplier of Ottoman galleons, Kurucaşile today shelters eight active shipyards in the villages of Ovatekkeönü and Kapısuyu. Under tarp shelters, master carpenters build yachts and fishing boats in chestnut, pine, and oak—without drawn plans, from memory. In 2021, a yard produced for the first time in 50 years a single-masted Ottoman-style boat. Come in the morning to see the teams at work.

The village of Kurucaşile itself deserves a walk: modest fishing harbor, fishermen's houses facing an often-agitated sea, and the rocky coast extending eastward. It's one of the few stretches of the western Black Sea coast still preserved from tourist concrete. The scenic road between Amasra and Kurucaşile (55 km) is among the wildest of the route.

days_plan.itin_title: Black Sea & Safranbolu: 9 days village to village

visits.text: Tiles of azure and red invade the sidewalks, stations, and fountains of Kütahya: the city is recognized as a UNESCO Creative City for the tradition of çini. The Ceramic Museum, housed in an Ottoman soup kitchen from 1411 (the Vacidiye Medresesi), displays pieces from the 14th century to today, from Iznik to Kütahya. Modest entry fee, panels in Turkish only.

Climb up to Kütahya Kalesi for a view over the city and the ceramic workshops dotting the slopes. The Byzantine citadel preserves its walls and a few towers; access is free. The panorama is worth the 15-minute ascent.

activities.text: If you're leaving with a piece of çini, choose a workshop on the old town side rather than the souvenir shops in the center. The Sıtkı Olçar workshop—UNESCO prize awarded during his lifetime, now run by his daughters—remains a reference for pigment quality. Prices start at 200-300 TL for a small hand-painted piece.

days_plan.itin_title: Central Anatolia: Phrygia, Konya, and the Caravanserais

visits.text: Lake Salda unfurls its unreal turquoise at the foot of Eşeler Dağı. Alkaline waters (pH around 9), depths to 196 m, white hydromagnesite beaches. One of Turkey's deepest crater lakes. Protected area since 1989, designated a special environmental protection zone in 2019.

NASA studies Salda as a terrestrial analogue of Jezero Crater on Mars, where the Perseverance rover has been searching for traces of ancient life since 2021. The hydromagnesite microbialites resemble the carbonates detected at Jezero. The space agency has published comparative images on its official site.

activities.text: Only swim in Lake Salda's designated areas now. Since 2021, several stretches of shore have been off-limits to preserve the microbialites and white sand. Doğanbaba beach remains open, easy access from Yeşilova. Sandals essential; the sand is compact but sharp in places. Don't remove sand.

days_plan.itin_title: 7-Day Taurus: Turquoise Lakes & Lycian Cities

visits.text: The honey-colored stone of Mardin heats in the sun and gives the old town its unique glow. Perched at 1,100 meters on a rocky spur, it dominates the Mesopotamian plain that unfolds toward Syria. The alleys climb and descend steeply, cobbled, sometimes in steps. Sandals not recommended; closed shoes essential.

Dating from 1385, the Zinciriye Medresesi (or Sultan Isa Medrese) remains one of the finest examples of Artuqid architecture in Mardin. Its carved portal, two ribbed domes, and inner courtyard deserve your time. The terrace roof overlooks the Mesopotamian plain, accessible via a discreet staircase on the west side.

The Church of the Forty Martyrs is worth the detour: it honors the Forty Martyrs of Sebaste, Roman soldiers executed in the 4th century for their Christian faith. Also known as Kırklar Kilisesi, it dates back to the 6th century and still hosts a weekly mass in Aramaic, the language spoken by Jesus. Inside, Byzantine icons and an ancient baptismal font. The local Syriac Orthodox community is a minority but vibrant.

A Byzantine crypt beneath a Syriac monastery: the basement of Deyrulzafaran houses an ancient temple dedicated to the Mesopotamian sun god Shamash, older than the Christian building. The transition between the two cults is visible in the stone, without rupture. That's what makes the place so dense with history.

Founded in the 5th century, Deyrulzafaran stands about 6 km east of Mardin, on a site once devoted to sun worship. It served as the seat of the Syriac Orthodox patriarch from 1160 to 1932. The visit is guided, the tour lasts 30 minutes, departures every half hour. Visits are in groups, sometimes bilingual English.

activities.text: Have breakfast in a konak in the old town: local cheeses, black olives from Hatay, pistachio honey, flatbread with molasses, and dibek coffee pounded in a mortar. Several mansion-restaurants line up their tables on terraces overlooking the plain. Expect around 200 TL per person; better to book on weekends.

If you like terraces, reach the Kasimiye Medresesi on foot from the lower old town, about 25 minutes downhill. The central basin represents the Sirat bridge of Islamic tradition. The accessible roof offers the view featured on all postcards. Go at sunset—it's free but monitored.

Allow 30 km to reach Dara, the ancient Byzantine city southeast of Mardin. Its vaulted underground cisterns, rock-cut necropolis carved into the limestone, and 6th-century fortifications are worth half a day. The site is free access, poorly signposted, few visitors. Bring a flashlight for the cisterns.
