Turkish Mesopotamia: 11 Days from Gaziantep to the Tigris
Turkish Mesopotamia: 11 Days from Gaziantep to the Tigris

visits.text: Perched on the hill above the harbor, this castle was completely rebuilt in 1522 by Suleiman the Magnificent - he used it as a staging base for his campaign against the island of Rhodes. Arrive at opening time (8:30am) to enjoy the cool morning air: from the ramparts, you'll have an unobstructed view over the bay and wooded hills. Inside, the museum displays amphorae, coins, glassware, and ceramics from the Hellenistic, Roman, and Byzantine periods, including finds from the Knidos site. Allow an hour and a half to explore everything at a relaxed pace.

Coming down from the castle, let yourself wander through the narrow alleys of the old town. Traditional houses with blue shutters stand alongside small artisan galleries and cafés serving black tea in small tulip glasses. This is where you'll find craftsmen's shops (ceramics, blown-glass lamps, spices) at noticeably better prices than in the covered bazaar.

Adjacent to the old town, this covered market gathers dozens of stalls selling souvenirs, spices, jewelry, and textiles. If bargaining intimidates you, know that it's perfectly expected here. Bring cash and don't hesitate to refuse the first offer.

Just beside the bazaar, this small two-story caravanserai was commissioned in 1545 by Hafsa Sultan, mother of Suleiman the Magnificent. The building is one of the few 16th-century Ottoman structures still standing in Marmaris. The austere stone facade is worth a quick look before heading to the waterfront.

The Kordon promenade runs along the marina for several kilometers: a succession of cruise boats, wooden gulets, sidewalk cafés, and fish restaurants. It was from this waterfront that in 1798, Admiral Nelson assembled his entire fleet before departing to face Napoleon at the Battle of the Nile. In the evening, settle into a terrace table to watch the boats come in.

activities.text: Numerous boats offer afternoon outings into Marmaris Bay and to nearby coves. It's a good way to grasp the scale of the coast before setting out by car. The bay is sheltered by wooded hills: the water remains calm even when the sea is rough elsewhere.
Marmaris's main beach stretches about 4 kilometers southwest of the center. It's well-equipped (sun loungers, showers, wheelchair access) and faces due west - ideal in late afternoon.

days_plan.itin_title: From Caria to Pamphylia: 12 Days on the Turquoise Coast, Marmaris to Antalya

visits.text: Midyat should be savored slowly, alley after alley. The old town of Eski Midyat features medieval Syriac mansions in golden stone, hidden inner courtyards, bell towers of still-active churches. The Tur Abdin region was inscribed on UNESCO's tentative list in 2021 for its medieval churches and monasteries.

Founded in 397, the Mor Gabriel Monastery is the world's oldest still-active Syriac Orthodox monastery. 20 km east of Midyat, it's home to around twenty monks and nuns. The liturgy is in Syriac, a language derived from Aramaic. Organized visits all day; plan for a donation at the exit.

At the Konukevi, the smell of old wood and cardamom mingles in the vaulted rooms of this large 19th-century Syriac house, now the official guesthouse. You can visit even if you're not staying overnight; the hall and courtyard are accessible. It's one of the symbols of Midyat's Syriac heritage, restored by the state.

activities.text: Artisans call their art telkari: silver filigree worked into tiny lace patterns, passed from master to apprentice for centuries in Midyat. Several workshops can be visited in the old town. Prices seem high, but the work is measured in hours, not grams. Look for the Turkish 925 hallmark to authenticate the silver.

Taste Syriac wine in a stone cellar: Midyat's Christian community has cultivated vines for centuries, and several cellars serve local vintages in 19th-century stone houses. The reds are full-bodied, the whites rarer. Expect 200 to 300 TL for a tasting with a platter.

days_plan.itin_title: Gaziantep to Trabzon: Mesopotamia, Nemrut & Historic Armenia - 14 Days
Turkish Mesopotamia: 11 Days from Gaziantep to the Tigris

visits.text: Mount Ararat, 5,137 m, is Turkey's highest peak. It's a dormant stratovolcano whose last eruption dates to July 2, 1840, and destroyed the Armenian monastery of St. James. According to Genesis, Noah's Ark came to rest here. The massif is about 35 km wide at the base.

Little Ararat, a regular cone of 3,896 m, accompanies Greater Ararat 11 km away. Both peaks emerge from the same volcanic base. The Iranian border passes 16 km to the south, the Armenian border 32 km to the north. You'll see both summits from the road between Doğubayazıt and Iğdır.

activities.text: If you want to summit, count on 4 to 5 days with an approved local guide, from Doğubayazıt. Permit required (military zone) to request at least a month in advance. Camp 1 at 3,200 m, Camp 2 at 4,200 m, summit push at 2 a.m., crampons and ice axe essential above 4,900 m.

days_plan.itin_title: Eastern Anatolia in 13 days: from the Caucasus to Ararat

visits.text: Evliya Çelebi described it as fertile and famous for its vineyards, wooden spoons, and combs. Mudurnu is a Cittaslow (slow city) on the UNESCO tentative list. Hundreds of preserved Ottoman houses rise following the natural slope of the terrain—builders worked with topography, not against it.

Yıldırım Bayezid Mosque, built in 1382 by Prince Bayezid (future Bayezid I), is part of a complex with hammam and madrasha from the same year—one of the region's oldest Ottoman ensembles. The hammam is still in service. Free entry to the mosque.

The Saturday bazaar is truer than many coastal tourist markets. Local products, wooden crafts, regional cheeses. Ahi culture—a religious and mystical corporation practiced here for centuries—still manifests in a bread-throwing ritual on Fridays.

days_plan.itin_title: Black Sea & Safranbolu: 9 days village to village

visits.text: Locals talk about the Virgin's smile at Eski Gümüşler, a Byzantine rock monastery 10 km from Niğde. The fresco depicts a Theotokos (Mother of God) with a half-smile, which would make it one of the rare examples in the world. Rediscovered in 1962, restored by a British team. Entry about 100 TL, open daily except Monday.

Built in 1223 by Governor Beşare for Seljuk Sultan Keykubad I, Niğde's Alaaddin Mosque stands on the citadel hill. Three domes, massive minaret, carved door depicting at certain sun hours the shadow of a crowned woman's head. Free visit, headscarf provided at entrance.

activities.text: Eat a kayseri mantısı in downtown Niğde before hitting the road again. The town is mainly agricultural, not touristic, the lokantas serve simple and hearty cuisine. Thursday's market runs around the clock tower, good chance to catch local life. Count on 100 to 150 TL for a full meal.

days_plan.itin_title: Extended Cappadocia in 8 Days: Kayseri, Göreme, Konya

visits.text: Ölüdeniz means 'dead sea' in Turkish - a reference to the absolute calm of this water protected by a tombolo. The lagoon itself is closed to swimming (protected zone) but the large Belcekız beach right next to it offers direct access to that turquoise water. Plan to arrive early in the morning before the influx of tour buses. Looking north, you'll spot paragliders descending from Mount Babadağ (1,900 m) - one of the world's most renowned paragliding sites.

Kayaköy is one of the most striking testimonies to the 1923 Greco-Turkish population exchange. This village - which its inhabitants called Levissi - had up to 10,000 Orthodox Greeks living there peacefully for generations with their Muslim neighbors. After the Greek defeat in the 1919-1922 war, the Treaty of Lausanne imposed a massive exchange: Greeks left for Greece, and Turks from Greece arrived in Anatolia. No one settled in Levissi, and the village remained empty. Since the 1957 earthquake, roofs have collapsed, but walls still stand. You can visit the two large churches (Panayia Pirgiotissa and Taxiarhis), the 14 chapels, and the remains of the school. From the heights, the view over the Mediterranean is striking.

The two large churches are the village's best-preserved buildings. The lower church (Panayia Pirgiotissa) even served as a mosque until the 1960s. Fragments of mosaics and painted decorations survive on the interior walls. The cemetery behind the upper church offers a silent panorama over the entire ruins.

activities.text: Ölüdeniz is one of the world's top destinations for tandem paragliding. From the launch platform at 1,900 m, the flight descends 20 to 30 minutes toward the beach, soaring over the lagoon, Butterfly Valley, and the jagged coast. Thermal updrafts are stable from May to October. No experience needed: instructors handle everything.

A marked trail connects Kayaköy to Ölüdeniz beach in 1h30 to 2h of walking. The path crosses pine forest, offers viewpoints over the coves, and ends directly at the beach. It's a section of the Lycian Way (Likya Yolu). Wear hiking shoes: the terrain is rocky.

days_plan.itin_title: From Caria to Pamphylia: 12 Days on the Turquoise Coast, Marmaris to Antalya

visits.text: Olympos is a Lycian city that, founded in the 2nd century BC, was one of the six cities with three votes in the Lycian League - the highest distinction. The ruins are scattered in dense vegetation on either side of the Akçay stream, which you cross on foot through the water to access the northern section. There's an ionic pronaos entrance gate dedicated to Marcus Aurelius (172-173 AD), a Lycian sarcophagus with a ship bas-relief (Captain Eudomus), a Byzantine basilica, and acropolis remains. The atmosphere is totally different from the concreted and signposted sites: here, you forge a path among ferns and carobs.

Yanartaş ('burning rock' in Turkish) is the name of the site identified as ancient Mount Chimera. About twenty fissures in the limestone rock have been releasing non-biological methane for at least 2,500 years - ancient sailors used them as a natural lighthouse. Immediately beneath the flames, the ruins of a temple of Hephaestus (god of fire) dating from Roman times. Visit preferably at sunset or after dark: the flames, barely visible in full sun, glow strangely in the darkness. The climb from the parking lot takes 30 minutes on foot.

activities.text: Olympos beach - technically Çıralı beach, 2 km away - is a long strip of fine pebbles at the stream's mouth. A few bungalows under orange trees border one side. The beach is also a nesting site for loggerhead turtles: sections are marked off in season. The water is clean and swimming pleasant.

The nighttime visit is very different from the daytime one. The flames stand out sharply in the dark, with an oscillating light that constantly changes in intensity. Groups from Çıralı and Olympos organize evening excursions (departure around 9pm) with flashlights. It's the perfect time to brew tea on one of the flames - locals still do it today.

days_plan.itin_title: Turkey End to End: The Complete 25-Day Grand Tour
From Caria to Pamphylia: 12 Days on the Turquoise Coast, Marmaris to Antalya

visits.text: From the Boztepe cable car, Ordu's coastline unfolds across 30 km. The cabin climbs 450 meters in six minutes; round-trip ticket around 50 TL. At the summit: panoramic café, paragliding for enthusiasts, and noticeably cooler air than below. Skip foggy days—common in spring.

Paşaoğlu Mansion houses a small ethnographic museum in a properly restored 19th-century timber house. Period furniture, Pontic costumes, and an antique kitchen give a clear picture of merchant life before 1923. Allow 45 minutes; right in the center.

activities.text: Here, people live on hazelnuts, and Ordu is one of Turkey's main producers. Stop at a downtown roaster to taste freshly roasted hazelnuts. September is harvest season—you'll see sacks piling up everywhere on the roads.

days_plan.itin_title: Turkish Black Sea Road Trip: 8 Days from Istanbul to Hopa

visits.text: Terraced limestone formations created by 17 thermal springs, some reaching 45°C. As the water cools, it deposits calcium carbonate, forming white stepped pools cascading down a 200-metre hillside. Listed as a UNESCO World Heritage site since 1988. Walk barefoot on the authorised sections - shoes must be carried in a bag.

Built during the reign of Septimius Severus (2nd-3rd century), this theatre could seat 15,000 spectators. The stage is adorned with bas-reliefs depicting a ritual procession and a sacrifice to Artemis of Ephesus. From the upper tiers, the view over the white terraces of Pamukkale is breathtaking.

One of the largest ancient necropolises in Anatolia, with over 1,200 tombs spreading north of the city. Marble sarcophagi, tumuli, monumental family tombs: each type reflects the social status of its occupants. The Greek inscriptions on the sarcophagi are a precious source of information about daily life in the ancient city.

This thermal pool filled with toppled ancient columns is open for swimming. The 36°C mineral water floats between submerged marble drums and capitals, remnants of the Roman portico that collapsed during an earthquake. Access to swim requires a separate ticket beyond the general entrance fee.

262 metres long and 50 metres wide, this stadium with two curved ends could accommodate 30,000 spectators - while the ancient city's population barely reached 10,000, testimony to its regional importance. It's the best-preserved ancient stadium in the world. It hosted Greek games, festivals and public votes.

A monumental gateway with four rows of Corinthian columns, built in the mid-2nd century. It marked the entrance to the sanctuary of Aphrodite for pilgrims' processions. Restored to 85% from original pieces between 1982 and 1990, it's now the site's most photographed image.

Built in the 3rd century BC, this white marble Ionic temple was transformed into a Christian basilica around AD 500. A unique architectural operation: the temple was literally turned inside out, with its columns reoriented. The 14 columns still standing allow you to read both historical phases simultaneously.

Housed within the site itself, this museum presents an exceptional collection of marble sculptures: imperial portraits, reliefs from the Sebasteion (a complex dedicated to the cult of Julio-Claudian emperors), statues from the local school. It's here you understand why sculptors from Aphrodisias were sought after throughout the Empire.

A cult complex dedicated to Augustus and the Julio-Claudian emperors, built in the 1st century. Its two colonnaded galleries on three superimposed levels were decorated with sculpted reliefs depicting mythological scenes and imperial conquests. It's one of the best-documented sets of narrative reliefs from the Roman world.

activities.text: Climb the basins from the south entrance (South Gate) all the way to the top of the plateau. The water is cold in the morning, warmer in the afternoon. Bring a waterproof bag for your shoes. The surface can be slippery: walk carefully, especially on areas that are still wet.

Stay until evening: when the sun drops over the Denizli plain, the travertines turn gold and orange. The Byzantine ramparts on the eastern side offer an unobstructed viewpoint, away from the tour groups who leave by bus from 5pm onwards.

Arrive at opening time to enjoy the site before the rare tour groups that turn up in early afternoon. Morning light reveals the sculpted details of the Tetrapylon, particularly on the western facade. The site covers several hectares: allow 3 to 4 hours to see everything without rushing.

A few kilometres northeast of the site lie the quarries that fed all of Aphrodisias's sculptural production. White rocks exposed in the open air, on the mountainside. A short detour that gives a concrete sense of the complete chain, from raw stone to masterpiece.

days_plan.itin_title: White cotton, whirling dervishes and fairy chimneys - 10 days from Antalya to Cappadocia
White cotton, whirling dervishes and fairy chimneys - 10 days from Antalya to Cappadocia
Turkey End to End: The Complete 25-Day Grand Tour

visits.text: A Polish village 25 km from Istanbul—the incongruity is total. Polonezköy (Polish: Adampol) was founded in 1846 by Polish refugees fleeing the partition of their country. Turkey was, at the time, the only European state that refused to recognize the partition. Declared a nature park in 1996, the village retains its wooden houses in the forest.

Founded in 1846 by Polish settlers, Polonezköy hosted Franz Liszt in 1847 and Gustave Flaubert in 1850—then Atatürk in 1937 and the future Pope John XXIII in 1941 for a confirmation. The Church of Our Lady of Częstochowa, built in 1914 after a fire, is still active. Its restorers bear distinctly Polish names: Ohotski, Dohoda, Novicki.

Life-sized wood sculptures among the oaks—Polonezköy's open-air exhibition brings together works by Turkish and Polish artists, installed in the nature park. Beekeeper Kemal Sayal's honey museum is small but well-documented. Look for it on your way back from the forest walk.

activities.text: Have the village breakfast (köy kahvaltısı) before tackling the 5 km marked forest trail. That's the main activity here—what Istanbul residents come seeking on weekends. Arrive early to avoid Saturday crowds; on weekdays, the paths are almost deserted.

days_plan.itin_title: Black Sea & Safranbolu: 9 days village to village

visits.text: The tea plantations of Rize cascade in green terraces from the seashore up to 700 meters altitude - it's the landscape that defines the entire province. Turkey is the 6th largest tea producer worldwide, and Rize is the capital: more than 200,000 families cultivate Camellia sinensis here. The Ziraat Bahçesi botanical garden, in the city center, offers a panorama over the terraced plantations and the Black Sea. Free entry, free parking.

The Fırtına ("storm") valley, 30 km from Rize in the Çamlıhemşin district, is classified among the world's 200 priority ecoregions by WWF. The Fırtına river, fast-flowing and jade-colored, is crossed by several Ottoman stone arch bridges from the 17th and 18th centuries still in use. The most famous is the Taş Köprü bridge at Çamlıhemşin. The road then climbs toward the Ayder plateau - the ultimate destination for hikers tackling Kaçkar.

The fortress of Rize sits atop a rocky spur in the city center. It dates from the Byzantine era and was modified under the Ottomans. From the ramparts, views over the harbor, the tiered plantations, and the Kaçkar range in the background. Climb on foot from the center: 15 minutes.

activities.text: At any shop in Rize, ask to see how Turkish çay is prepared: double teapot (çaydanlık), boiling water in the large one, dry leaves steeping in the small. The result - an amber tea served in a tulip-shaped glass - is drunk sweet and without milk. It's a social ritual as much as a beverage.

Rafting on the Fırtına is offered by several operators in Çamlıhemşin from April to September. Level accessible to beginners on downstream sections, more technical upstream. The river is icy cold even in summer - allow one hour of descent and mandatory change of clothes.

days_plan.itin_title: Trabzon to Istanbul: Black Sea, Hittites, and Safranbolu in 10 Days
Turkish Black Sea Road Trip: 8 Days from Istanbul to Hopa

places.name: Safranbolu

visits.text: Safranbolu's old town is one of two Turkish cities inscribed on UNESCO's World Heritage list as early as 1994, along with Istanbul. Of the 2,000 Ottoman houses that compose the urban fabric, 800 are officially protected by the state. These three-level residences - stone basement, upper floors in timber frame with overhangs - were built primarily in the 18th century, when Safranbolu was a major stopover on the caravan route between Istanbul and the Black Sea coast. Begin the visit in the Çarşı (market) quarter to see the town at its usual rhythm.

The Cinci Han is the most imposing caravanserai east of Istanbul, built in 1645 by Cinci Hoca, an influential figure at the Ottoman court. This two-level ashlar stone building housed merchants, horses, and goods on the Silk Road. Today converted into a hotel and café, it also houses a coffee museum on the second floor, with Ottoman preparation tools and archives on coffee's role in Safranbolu's urban life.

The Cinci Hamam (Cinci Hamamı), built in the 17th century at the same time as the neighboring caravanserai, is one of the rare historic baths still functional in the region. The two entrances - men and women - open onto the same cobbled streets. The domes pierced with frosted glass pentagons let in filtered light characteristic of classical Ottoman hammams.

The Köprülü Mehmet Paşa Mosque, built in the second half of the 17th century, is the other central monument of the Çarşı. Its painted three-dimensional coffered ceiling and colorful wall details contrast with the usual external sobriety of provincial mosques. Right across from the Cinci Hamam, the two buildings form a cohesive ensemble on the same square.

activities.text: The arasta of the shoemakers (Yemeniciler Arastası) is Safranbolu's historic artisan market - a covered gallery where craftsmen still work making yemeni, heelless leather sandals using Ottoman technique. Only one active manufacturer remains in the entire city. The arasta also houses groceries selling local saffron and artisanal lokum.

Safranbolu is explored on foot - the Çarşı's cobbled streets are not drivable. Park the vehicle at the old town entrance parking and set out on foot for the day. The climb toward the Bağlar quarter (summer houses with gardens and vines) offers a view over the entire valley.

map_keywords: Safranbolu UNESCO Ottoman houses historic district

visits.text: Virtually no one in the Sagalassos ruins on a Tuesday morning in September—one of the site's great pleasures. This Pisidian city perched on Mount Akdağ around 1,500 m was abandoned around the mid-7th century after several earthquakes. Belgian excavations since 1989 (Marc Waelkens); inscribed on UNESCO's Tentative List.

The Antonine Fountain, restored in 2010, flows again for the first time in roughly 1,500 years. Built under Marcus Aurelius around 160-180 AD, it lines the full length of the upper agora. Statues replaced by copies (originals in Burdur Museum). The theatre, at 1,500 m, is one of the world's highest from antiquity.

activities.text: Park at the Sagalassos lot after 7 km of switchbacks from Ağlasun; allow 3 hours on site. The site is terraced, the fountain stands at the top. Water and cap essential; little shade. Goats graze among the columns—don't approach too closely. Picnicking permitted outside the excavation zone.

days_plan.itin_title: 7-Day Taurus: Turquoise Lakes & Lycian Cities

visits.text: This is where Atatürk landed on May 19, 1919, the official starting point of Turkey's War of Independence. The Bandırma ferry, reconstructed at full scale, now serves as a museum. Small, informative, free. Allow 45 minutes if you want to read the panels.

Samsun's waterfront lines up 5 km of paved promenade, popular late afternoon. The Onur Monument, a massive 1932 equestrian statue of Atatürk, marks the center. A simple stop, with little old heritage; the city works mainly as a staging point.

activities.text: Stroll along the corniche west to Atakum Park. Black volcanic-sand beaches, popular in summer. You'll find pide salonu in the Atakum neighborhood—quieter than downtown. Half a day at most.

days_plan.itin_title: Turkish Black Sea Road Trip: 8 Days from Istanbul to Hopa

places.name: Sanl Urfa

visits.text: Göbekli Tepe challenges everything we thought we knew about the origins of civilization. This UNESCO site dating to roughly 9600 BC - 6,000 years before Stonehenge and 7,500 years before Egypt's pyramids - was built by hunter-gatherers who erected T-shaped limestone pillars reaching 5.5 m in height, carved with foxes, vultures, snakes, and scorpions. More than 200 pillars have been detected by geophysics, but less than 10% of the site has been excavated. The visitor center does an excellent job explaining the context. Come in the morning, before the tour groups.

In Şanlıurfa, the City of Prophets, the Pool of Abraham (Balıklıgöl) is one of the region's most story-laden sites. Legend holds that Abraham was thrown into a fire here by King Nimrod, and the flames turned to water, the wood to carp. These sacred fish now swim by the thousands in a marble-lined pool surrounded by Ottoman gardens and the Halil-ur Rahman Mosque. No one touches them. The mosque, built in the 12th century on the site of a Byzantine church, deserves a quiet visit.

Şanlıurfa's covered bazaar, with its aisles of spices and fabric merchants, is one of eastern Turkey's most authentic. Less touristy than Istanbul's or Gaziantep's, it serves locals first. Regional spices - isot (smoked black pepper unique to the region), sumac, cumin - are sold by weight. The neighboring Gümrük Han, a 16th-century Ottoman caravanserai, houses craft shops and tea salons in its arcades.

The village of Harran is known worldwide for its beehive houses - conical mud-brick domes, built without wood, designed to stay cool in summer and warm in winter. This type of architecture dates back at least 3,000 years in Mesopotamia; the current versions date from the 19th century and use bricks salvaged from ancient ruins. Harran is mentioned in Genesis as the city where Abraham stayed before departing for Canaan. Wander the lanes and imagine that the town has been continuously inhabited since the Bronze Age.

The Harran mound (Tell Harran) rises 35.5 m above the ancient city center. Excavations have revealed a main street, baths, fountains, and a covered market dating from the Zengid and Ayyubid periods. On the north flank stand the ruins of Harran's Grand Mosque - one of the first built in modern-day Turkey, erected around 744 AD by Umayyad caliph Marwan II. The square minaret, 33 m tall, still stands.

Harran University, founded in the 8th century, was one of the first centers of Islamic learning, specializing in astronomy, mathematics, medicine, and philosophy. It played a crucial role in transmitting classical Greek knowledge by translating it first into Syriac, then into Arabic. The ruins, now partially inaccessible due to works, include remnants of the prayer hall and courtyard.

Harran's medieval walls form a 4 km perimeter, pierced by several gates of which only the Aleppo Gate (west side) remains. Built from the Assyrian period and remodeled by successive dynasties, they enclosed a city that at its peak was one of the most important in Upper Mesopotamia.

activities.text: The Şanlıurfa Archaeology and Mosaic Museum is essential for contextualizing Göbekli Tepe. It displays casts of the pillars, anthropomorphic figurines discovered on-site, and a complete collection of Neolithic art from the Taş Tepeler region. The 'Urfa Man' statue, dated to 9000 BC, is one of the oldest known human representations.

Dinner with a local specialty: Şanlıurfa's lahmacun differs from everywhere else - a crisp, thin flatbread topped with spiced minced meat, herbs, and isot. Eaten rolled with parsley and a squeeze of lemon. Lahmacun ovens open at 6am and close when the dough runs out.

The Harran plain is part of the GAP (Southeastern Anatolia Project) that transformed these lands into irrigated agricultural zones. From the town, the view stretches over kilometers of cotton, corn, and wheat - a striking contrast with the ruins behind you. A few camels still graze in the surroundings if you're lucky.

days_plan.itin_title: Turkey End to End: The Complete 25-Day Grand Tour
Gaziantep to Trabzon: Mesopotamia, Nemrut & Historic Armenia - 14 Days
Gaziantep to Trabzon: Mesopotamia, Nemrut & Historic Armenia - 14 Days

map_keywords: Harran ancient ruins city walls Mesopotamia
Harran Grand Mosque Ulu Cami ruins

visits.text: Locals simply call it Balıklıgöl, the pool of fish. Islamic legend places Abraham's miracle here: Nemrod threw him into a fire, which turned into water, and the wood into carp. The carp are protected, never fished. The site is free, very busy in late afternoon with local families. Avoid midday and the scorching sun.

Built in 1211 by the Ayyubids, the Halil-ur-Rahman Mosque borders directly onto the sacred pool. Its shaded courtyard is one of the few places in Şanlıurfa where you can breathe in summer. The square minaret recalls Syrian models. Cover your shoulders and legs before entering; scarves provided at the entrance for women.

The air smells of isot and rubbed copper in Kazaz Bedesten, a covered market built in 1562. It's the oldest bazaar in Şanlıurfa, dedicated to fabrics and traditional outfits. All around: Sipahi for Kurdish carpets, the coppersmiths' bazaar, the pepper market. Haggle—it's expected.

The Şanlıurfa Archaeology Museum is by far the most important in Anatolia for the Neolithic. Opened in its current building in 2015, it displays nearly 10,000 pieces: original T-pillars from Göbekli Tepe, Karahantepe sculptures, Amazons mosaics from Haleplibahçe. Budget two hours minimum. Closed Tuesday mornings.

Standing 1.80 meters tall, the Urfa Man dates from around 9000 BC and is believed to be the world's oldest life-size statue. Found in 1993 during construction work in Yeni Mahalle, it's limestone, with obsidian eyes. No mouth, which intrigues archaeologists. It has its own display case at the museum—don't miss it on arrival.

The Mevlid-i Halil cave smells of dampness and incense. Muslim tradition places Abraham's birth here. Now part of a mosque complex adjoining Balıklıgöl, the cave can be visited for free. Men and women enter separately. Cover your hair and shoulders. The atmosphere is that of an active sanctuary, not a museum.

activities.text: If you climb to Urfa Castle in late afternoon, the view over the pool and old town is worth the effort. Legend has it that Nemrod catapulted Abraham from those two Corinthian columns you still see at the top. Count on 20 minutes' walk from Balıklıgöl. The stairs are steep, closed at 7pm in summer.

Buy a packet of pellets to feed the sacred carp of Balıklıgöl, about 5 TL from vendors around the pool. The fish rush in a compact shoal. According to locals, spotting a white carp brings luck. Don't bring your own food—it's forbidden to preserve the carp population.

Dine at Cevahir Konukevi, a restored 19th-century caravanserai serving Urfa cuisine: hand-prepared çiğ köfte, thin lahmacun, ali nazik with smoked eggplant. The inner courtyard is cooled with misters in summer. Moderate prices, and it's one of the few places still serving mırra, strong Arabic coffee.

The Urfalis call their city the city of prophets: tradition holds that Job, Abraham, and Elijah all left their mark here. Stroll through the Eyyübiye district at sunrise, when worshippers arrive at Balıklıgöl and the heat hasn't yet crushed you. The golden light on the stone walls is worth the early wake-up.

days_plan.itin_title: Turkish Mesopotamia: 11 Days from Gaziantep to the Tigris
Turkish Mesopotamia: 11 Days from Gaziantep to the Tigris

visits.text: Lake Sapanca: a vast green and blue expanse between Sakarya's hills. It's also one of the Kocaeli region's main drinking water reserves. About 80 bird species have been recorded here. Along its northern shore, the road to Maşukiye is one of the route's greenest.

Maşukiye comes from the Arabic word for 'love'—which explains its reputation as a honeymoon destination. The mountain village is wedged between forests and waterfalls, 10 km from Sapanca. Terrace restaurants above streams serve extended Turkish breakfast among the trees.

activities.text: Lunch facing Mahmudiye waterfalls and breathe—this is the decompression stop before İznik. The waterfalls are accessible from a small parking lot at the hamlet entrance; the path is short but steep. Several overlooking restaurants offer river fish menus.

days_plan.itin_title: Black Sea & Safranbolu: 9 days village to village

places.name: Selcuk

visits.text: Enter from the top (south entrance) and descend towards the north exit—all gently downhill, your legs will thank you. In the 2nd century AD, Ephesus was one of the four largest cities in the Roman Empire with an estimated population of 250,000. Allow 3 to 4 hours for the main highlights and, if budget permits, add 45 minutes for the Terrace Houses (separate ticket): their preserved mosaics and frescoes are among the finest in the Roman East. Arrive before 9 a.m.—groups of cruise passengers from Kuşadası invade the site mid-morning.

Built between 110 and 135 AD by consul Tiberius Julius Aquila in honour of his father—whose sarcophagus rests in the building's basement—the Library of Celsus is the third-largest library of antiquity, with approximately 12,000 scrolls. Its two-level façade, with Corinthian columns and four statues symbolising Sophia, Arete, Ennoia, and Episteme, is Turkey's most photographed image. The visible statues are copies—the originals were transferred to Vienna's Ephesos Museum in 1910.

This open-air theatre could seat 25,000 spectators—considered the largest in antiquity. From the upper tiers, the view plunges down the ancient Arcadian Way (which led to the ancient harbour, now silted up) is breathtaking. This is where the Apostle Paul sparked a riot by preaching against the cult of Artemis, according to the Acts of the Apostles.

Eight kilometres from Selçuk, Şirince is a former Greek Orthodox village founded in the 2nd century AD. It was once called Çirkince—'ugly'—a name chosen to discourage outsiders from settling there. White houses with red-tiled roofs climb hills surrounded by fruit vineyards. After the 1924 population exchange, Greeks were replaced by Turks from Greece who perpetuated the fruit wine tradition—apple, peach, mulberry—still sold in village shops.

activities.text: This Christian and Muslim pilgrimage site is located 9 kilometres from Ephesus, in an oak forest. The house was located in the 19th century thanks to descriptions from a vision by German nun Anne Catherine Emmerich, confirmed by the Lazarist Fathers. Pope Paul VI visited in 1967, John Paul II in 1979. The fountain at the entrance is reputed for its beneficial virtues.

Şirince shops offer locally produced fruit wines—apple, peach, mulberry, pomegranate. These aren't grape wines but fruit fermentations inherited from the village's Greek tradition. Taste before buying: quality varies. The best producers are often those who cultivate their own orchards on the surrounding hills.

days_plan.itin_title: Turkish Aegean Coast: Gallipoli, Troy, Ephesus & Bodrum in 12 Days
Turkey End to End: The Complete 25-Day Grand Tour

map_keywords: House of Virgin Mary Ephesus Turkey

visits.text: Salt crunches underfoot, like snow, on the southeast shore of Lake Tuz near Şereflikoçhisar. The surface forms a perfect mirror early in the season, a blinding white crust in July-August. Sunglasses mandatory, extra sunscreen. Access is from a free parking lot then 200 m on foot across the salt.

Turkey's second-largest lake, Tuz Gölü is also one of the world's saltiest, average depth about 50 cm. It hosts a major breeding colony of flamingos, one of the most active in the region. The lake also supplies a large share of Turkey's salt.

activities.text: Go to the south shore between April and June for flamingos, May-June for chicks, July-August for salt crust and algae that pink the water. The south shore near Şereflikoçhisar concentrates the best photo spots and a café-restaurant. Avoid the lake edge after rain, the salt turns muddy.

days_plan.itin_title: Extended Cappadocia in 8 Days: Kayseri, Göreme, Konya

visits.text: Built on a hill where Phrygians already prayed, the Seyyid Battal Gazi Külliyesi is one of those multilayered places: a Christian sanctuary that became a Seljuk complex in 1207, expanded by Suleiman the Magnificent in 1511. The türbe houses a 5.5-meter sarcophagus—legend says the hero was a giant. Entry is free, the adjacent cemetery mixes Ottoman tombs with older structures.

days_plan.itin_title: Central Anatolia: Phrygia, Konya, and the Caravanserais

visits.text: Painted black and white to be visible from afar, Şile Lighthouse (Şile Feneri) was built on 60-meter-high rocks during the reign of Sultan Abdülmecid I. Its 19-meter stone tower houses a small maritime museum. Originally built to guide ships coming from the Black Sea during the Crimean War.

Inaccessible, Şile Castle fascinates more from the cliff than if you could enter it. This 10th-century Byzantine fortress occupies an islet separated from the shore—you look at it, imagine it. Take in the view from the promontory facing the lighthouse to grasp the entire coastline.

activities.text: If the sea is calm, Şile Beach and Kilimli Bay (Kilimli Sahil), a few minutes east, offer less crowded coves. The Black Sea is cool even in summer—expect 18-22°C in July-August. Avoid summer weekends when Istanbul residents flood the beaches.

days_plan.itin_title: Black Sea & Safranbolu: 9 days village to village

visits.text: Konya is 8 km away, but Sille seems centuries distant. This Christian-Orthodox village, inhabited until the 1923 population exchange, preserves Aya Eleni Kilisesi (St. Helen's Church), whose foundation supposedly dates back to the 4th century according to tradition. The recently restored frescoes and baptismal crypt are worth the detour. The adjacent mixed Islamic-Christian cemetery is rare to see in Anatolia.

days_plan.itin_title: Central Anatolia: Phrygia, Konya, and the Caravanserais

visits.text: Sinop's residents claim Diogenes, the Cynic philosopher born here around 412 BC. His 5.5-meter statue, erected in 2006, shows him with his lantern and dog, standing on his famous barrel. The spot, at the peninsula's entrance, also makes the best landmark to start your visit.

Sinop Fortress Prison operated from 1887 to 1997. Nicknamed the Alcatraz of Anatolia, it once held writers like Sabahattin Ali and Nazım Hikmet. The windowless solitary cells set the tone. Allow 1.5 hours to cover all 28 wings. Signage in English and Turkish.

Sinop Archaeological Museum displays Neolithic pottery, Hellenistic amphorae, and statuary from the Kingdom of Pontus. Small, well done, located near the harbor. Perfect for understanding how this peninsula spent 2,500 years changing hands.

activities.text: If you're arriving by sea, Sinop's Byzantine fortress still commands the northern tip. Walk the ramparts from Lonca Gate to the cape, about 3 km, with views of the Black Sea on both sides. The path runs above the prison.

The fish market is worth the stop around 10 a.m. Hamsi (anchovy), barbun (red mullet), kalkan (turbot). Several small restaurants around the small harbor will grill what you choose. Expect 200–300 TL per person in 2026, drink included.

days_plan.itin_title: Turkish Black Sea Road Trip: 8 Days from Istanbul to Hopa

visits.text: Three great madrasas within a few minutes' walk, all built the same year. Sivas saw the completion in 1271 of the Gökmedrese, Buruciye Medrese, and Çifte Minareli Medrese. A Seljuk-Ilkhanid administrative coincidence. Allow half a day to see them all, separate tickets.

Built in 1271 by the vizier Sahip Ata, Sivas's Gökmedrese bears the work of Kaluyan al-Konavi, master mason from Konya. Carved marble portal, two brick minarets decorated with blue and black tiles. The symmetry of the façade is broken by a fountain, one of the first integrated into a Seljuk building.

At Buruciye Medrese, the air smells of cold stone and old wood. Built under Gıyaseddin Keyhüsrev III, classic four-iwan plan around a courtyard. Restored between 1960 and 1968, then again in 2005. Now serves as a cultural center with a small cafeteria in the courtyard. Very quiet on weekdays.

activities.text: Sit down at the Şifaiye, a former Seljuk hospital from 1217, built by Sultan Kaykaus I. Open courtyard, iwans converted into craft shops and a café. It's one of the oldest preserved hospitals in Anatolia. The room housing the sultan's tomb remains accessible, entrance at the rear.

The people of Sivas take pride in their kebab köfte, a local version shaped into balls and grilled on a spit. Try it in one of the canteens near the Çifte Minareli Medrese, preferably at midday for freshness. Sivas köfte is denser and spicier than the Ankara version, systematically accompanied by bulgur pilavı.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Central Turkey: 7 Days Ankara to Kayseri

visits.text: Sixty-seven columns of yew and pine support the roof of Sivrihisar Ulu Camii, built in 1232 under the Seljuks. The carved walnut minbar, dated 1245, is one of the best-preserved pieces of medieval woodwork in Anatolia. The building was just UNESCO-listed in 2023 as part of the medieval Anatolian wooden-column mosques. Free entry, cover your shoulders.

days_plan.itin_title: Central Anatolia: Phrygia, Konya, and the Caravanserais

visits.text: Caravaneers called Sultanhanı the 'palace of the poor': for the first three days, food, lodging, and medical care were offered free by the sultan. Built in 1229 by Aladin Keykubat I, this kervansaray is Turkey's largest. The Taç Kapı, a gate crowned with sculpted stalactites, sets you up well for the interior courtyard. Recent café installed in the former stables.

days_plan.itin_title: Central Anatolia: Phrygia, Konya, and the Caravanserais

visits.text: Clinging at 1,200 meters to the cliff face of Mount Mela, Sumela Monastery was founded around 386 by two Athenian monks, Barnabas and Sophronios. Most of today's buildings date from the 13th–14th centuries, the Empire of Trebizond period. Reopened in 2019 after four years of rockfall prevention work.

Inside Sumela Monastery, Byzantine frescoes layer over each other across centuries, sometimes covered with 19th-century graffiti. The troglodyte chapel, the historical starting point, holds the oldest layers. Allow an hour on-site after the shuttle arrives.

activities.text: If you come in winter, the road is often closed for snow and the monastery itself can suspend visits. Safe period: May to October. Parking is 4 km away; mandatory shuttles since 2019, combined ticket. Expect a 30-minute uphill walk.

days_plan.itin_title: Turkish Black Sea Road Trip: 8 Days from Istanbul to Hopa

visits.text: If you're looking for Safranbolu without the tourists, you've found Taraklı. Over 100 registered cultural properties, Cittaslow since 2011, European Destinations of Excellence Award 2013—and almost no one in the cobbled alleys. Traveler Evliya Çelebi in the 17th century already described it as 'fertile, famous for its vineyards, spoons, and combs.'

Yunus Paşa Mosque, built in 1517 by Grand Vizier Yunus Paşa and designed by Mimar Sinan, is Taraklı's largest building. Locals call it 'Kurşunlu Camii' (Lead Mosque) because of the lead used in its construction. It had a heating system powered by the adjacent hammam—a rarity for the era.

activities.text: The colorful bazaar still sells wooden combs—the very name Taraklı comes from the word 'tarakçı' (comb seller). It's one of the few craft specialties to have survived in its city of origin. Timber houses restored as guesthouses offer the route's most authentic overnight stays.

days_plan.itin_title: Black Sea & Safranbolu: 9 days village to village

visits.text: Nemrut's crater lake, at 2,247 m altitude, occupies a volcanic caldera of more than 12 km² north of Tatvan. It holds two distinct lakes, one cold and one hot (thermal springs at the edge). Blue water, sheer rock walls. Access only from mid-May to late October—under snow the rest of the year.

Mount Nemrut sealed the Lake Van basin with its lava flows, transforming an open basin into a salt lake with no outlet. Eruptions date back to roughly 90,000 to 30,000 years ago. You'll spot obsidian and tuff at several crater points; collect sparingly (officially prohibited).

Mount Süphan, 4,058 m, intimidates less than Ararat but remains a major summit. Visible from the north rim of Lake Van, its snow-capped cone dominates the Adilcevaz plain. It's one of Turkey's highest volcanoes, dormant. You can gaze at it from the Tatvan pier at dawn.

activities.text: If the crater road isn't iced over, drive up to the rim: 13 km from the main road, paved but potholed. Round-trip taxi with wait around 600 TL from Tatvan. Better to take your own car if you want to stay for sunset.

Pitch a tent near Nemrut's hot springs if you're camping: one side of the crater has water above 60°C, the other freezes in winter. The site received the European Destination of Excellence label in 2010. Birch forest surrounds the shores; spectacular colors in September–October.

days_plan.itin_title: Eastern Anatolia in 13 days: from the Caucasus to Ararat

visits.text: Termessos sent Alexander the Great packing in 333 BC. The conqueror dubbed the city an 'eagle's nest' before abandoning the siege. The Solymi, a Pisidian people, occupied this mountain stronghold at 1,050 m altitude on Mount Güllük (ancient Solymos, 1,665 m at the summit). The national park now encompasses the entire massif.

Termessos's theatre clings to the mountainside at 1,050 m, facing the Taurus peaks. 4,200 seats carved from limestone, a raw panorama over the range and the Pamphylian plain. Go early; fog often rolls in around 11 am. Sarcophagi lie scattered through the surrounding pine woods, some prised open by treasure hunters.

activities.text: Allow 4 hours and proper footwear at Termessos. The trail from the car park climbs 1 km with roughly 200 m elevation gain. No refreshments inside; water and a hat are mandatory in summer. National park entry is modest, separate site ticket (free with Museum Pass). Snakes possible; don't lift stones.

days_plan.itin_title: 7-Day Taurus: Turquoise Lakes & Lycian Cities

visits.text: Built around 1275 by Mu'in al-Din Süleyman, Seljuk vizier of the Sultanate of Rum, Tokat's Gök Medrese displays its blue and black tile mosaics on a bicolor stone portal. It's one of the earliest known examples of pure mo'araq mosaic. It long housed the local museum before the 2012 move.

The Latifoğlu Mansion, a restored 19th-century Turkish house, gives a precise idea of late Ottoman bourgeois housing. Painted woodwork, coffered ceilings, private hammam. Allow 45 minutes. Located in the old fabric of the town, 10 minutes on foot from the new Tokat Museum installed in the bedesten.

activities.text: Stop by the Ottoman bedesten to see the new Tokat Museum, opened in 2012. Seljuk pieces, ceramics, and especially a manuscript Quran dated 1191. Two rooms address local crafts: textile printing (yazma) and copper work, two skills still practiced in town.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Central Turkey: 7 Days Ankara to Kayseri

visits.text: Tortum Waterfall, 48 meters high: magnificent from May to June, nearly dry the rest of the year. An 18th-century landslide blocked the valley and created both the falls and the lake. Allow 30 minutes on the viewing terraces.

Lake Tortum stretches its turquoise waters 8 km, wedged between red cliffs. The D950 hugs its eastern shore and offers several picnic areas with views. Make the most of it: you're crossing one of northeastern Turkey's most beautiful road stretches.

activities.text: Pack a picnic by the lake: bread, Kars cheese, tomatoes, and cucumbers are available in Uzundere grocery stores. The waterfall runs dry outside May–June, so the lake is the better bet. A vendor often sells fresh cucumbers at the main parking area.

days_plan.itin_title: Eastern Anatolia in 13 days: from the Caucasus to Ararat

visits.text: The Panagia Sumela Monastery is built directly into a vertical cliff face at 1,200 meters altitude in Altındere National Park, 45 km from Trabzon. According to tradition, it was founded in 386 AD by two Athenian monks, Barnabas and Sophronios, who followed a vision of the Virgin Mary, and served as an Orthodox spiritual center for over fifteen centuries. The frescoes in the rock-cut church - scenes from the New Testament, Last Judgment, Pantocrator - date mostly from the 18th century. Wear sturdy shoes: the path from the parking lot climbs through forest for 600 meters of elevation gain.

The Hagia Sophia of Trabzon is one of the few major Byzantine churches still standing in Anatolia - built between 1238 and 1263 under Manuel I Komnenos, sovereign of the Empire of Trebizond. The frescoes in the narthex, uncovered during a restoration led by the University of Edinburgh in the 1960s, notably depict a Last Judgment on the west wall and a Dormition of the Virgin in the apse. The building is now an active mosque: the frescoes are partially veiled during prayers. Check prayer times to plan your visit.

Trabzon's citadel occupies a rocky spur above the old center. The ramparts date partly from the Byzantine era and were modified by the Ottomans after the conquest of 1461. The view from the walls sweeps across the harbor and the Black Sea. The interior houses a small mosque and traces of a Komnenian palace.

activities.text: Arriving in Trabzon early in the day (flight from Istanbul or continuation of the Mesopotamia itinerary), start with Sumela in the morning when crowds are lighter. Shuttles depart from Trabzon bus station to Maçka, then dolmuş connect to the park entrance. By car, allow 1 hour from downtown.

In the evening, head down to the harbor and look for a restaurant serving hamsi - the Black Sea anchovy. It's the quintessential local fish, fried in a cornmeal crust. Bakeries also offer Trabzon ekmek, a dense wood-oven bread.

visits.text: Pure white in summer, soft pink in spring thanks to halophilic algae: Tuz Gölü is Turkey's second-largest lake by area and supplies a significant share of national salt production. In July-August, crystals cover the bottom like snow and you can walk on it—bring sturdy shoes, the salt cuts into light soles. Morning for the mirror effect, evening for the flamingos.

days_plan.itin_title: Central Anatolia: Phrygia, Konya, and the Caravanserais

visits.text: Rising 60 m high, Uçhisar Castle is actually a giant fairy chimney hollowed with tunnels, stairs and rooms. It's Cappadocia's highest point, used as a watchtower since the Hittites. The steep climb is on foot. The summit opens a 360° view over all the valleys. Ticket 150 TL in 2026.

Uçhisar beats Göreme for spending the night: quieter, higher, with the region's best cave hotels. The village climbs around the castle, its cobbled lanes preserved. Several café-terraces overlook Pigeon Valley. It's the right spot for sunset over the tethered balloons and the valley below.

activities.text: Climb in late afternoon to the summit of Uçhisar Castle for sunset. The stone heats then turns pink-violet, panoramic view of Erciyes in the background. Avoid weekends, the summit fills fast after 5 PM. Cap and closed shoes essential, lots of steep stairs inside.

days_plan.itin_title: Extended Cappadocia in 8 Days: Kayseri, Göreme, Konya

visits.text: Uzungöl - literally "the long lake" - is a mountain lake formed by a landslide that dammed the Haldizen Stream valley, at 1,090 meters altitude. The village bordering it, populated by Laz and Pontic communities, long served as a summer yayla before transforming into a tourist destination. Around the lake: forests of spruce and beech, morning fog, and wooden hotels built on the shores - some directly on stilts over the water. The trail that circles the lake (about 5 km) is walkable in two hours.

Above Uzungöl village, the yayla of Demirkapi and Şekersu open onto high-altitude pastures wrapped in mist. In summer, Laz families still bring their herds up to these plateaus. From the ascending road, the views over the steep-sided valley are among the most beautiful in Pontus.

activities.text: The Trabzon → Uzungöl road crosses the Haldizen valley and climbs in switchbacks through humid forests characteristic of eastern Pontus. Stop at the viewpoint at the village entrance for the classic view of the lake and mosque reflected in the water.

The horon dance - a traditional Laz and Pontic circle dance performed to the sound of the kemençe (local bowed fiddle) - is still practiced at weddings and festivals. If your visit falls on a July or August weekend, the chances of witnessing a ceremony in the villages around Uzungöl are real.

days_plan.itin_title: Trabzon to Istanbul: Black Sea, Hittites, and Safranbolu in 10 Days

places.name: Van

visits.text: Lake Van is Turkey's largest (3,755 km²) and the world's largest soda lake - its water is alkaline and slightly soapy, which explains why you float more easily than in the sea. Akdamar Island lies 3 km offshore from the Gevaş landing. Crossing by boat (15 min) to reach the Armenian Church of the Holy Cross, built between 915 and 921 by King Gagik I of Ardzrouni, is one of the itinerary's highlights. The exterior bas-reliefs in pink volcanic tuff depict biblical scenes - Adam and Eve, Jonah and the whale, David and Goliath - an iconographic program unique in Armenian art. Inside, partially preserved frescoes.

Van Castle (Van Kalesi) dominates the lake's east shore from a 100 m rocky promontory. Erected by the Kingdom of Urartu in the 9th century BC, it guarded Tuşpa, the ancient capital. Cuneiform inscriptions in Urartian are still readable on the rock faces. Below lie the ruins of the old city of Van (Eski Van), destroyed in 1915 and never rebuilt - only three minarets and some foundations emerge from a grassy field. It's a heavy place.

The Van Archaeological Museum displays the most important objects from Urartian civilization: bronzes, ceramics, jewelry, and cuneiform inscriptions. The section dedicated to the lake presents Van's unique fauna, notably Van cats, a naturally bicolored breed often with eyes of two different colors.

activities.text: Van breakfast (Van kahvaltısı) is a national institution: a dozen cheeses including otlu peynir (wild herb cheese), mountain honey, kaymak, tomatoes, olives, eggs prepared several ways. Cafés serving this breakfast open from 7am and are packed on weekends. It's the best in Turkey, Van locals will say - and they're probably not wrong.

visits.text: Viranşehir means "ruined city" in Turkish, a name that sums up its eventful history. It was Constantia under the Romans and Byzantines, Tella d'Mawzlat for the Syriacs. Rebuilt by Constantine I in the 4th century after destruction by the Persians, it was a strategic lock on the Byzantine frontier. Today it's a large Kurdish cotton town, more useful as a stopover than a destination.

The Han El-Burur, a medieval caravanserai noted near Viranşehir, testifies to the town's role on the old caravan routes. The remains are modest, accessible at the eastern exit of the town. Allow 20 minutes for a photo stop, no more. It's mainly an opportunity to break the long stretch between Şanlıurfa and Mardin without forcing the stage.

activities.text: Take a meal break in Viranşehir before tackling the stretch to Mardin. Several lokantas lined up on the main street serve pistachio kebab and local çiğ köfte. Expect around 150 TL for a full meal with ayran. Avoid prayer times on Friday; many shops close for 45 minutes.

days_plan.itin_title: Turkish Mesopotamia: 11 Days from Gaziantep to the Tigris

visits.text: According to a 2nd-century AD inscription preserved in Edirne museum, Vize Castle (Vize Kalesi) dates to 72–76 BCE. The fortress served as acropolis for the Thracian city of Bizye. The still-standing south face rises over 5 m high. The site is freely accessible but unsigned – ask locals for Kale Caddesi.

Locals simply call it Küçük Ayasofya – the Little Hagia Sophia of Vize (Gazi Süleyman Paşa Camii). A former Byzantine church converted to a mosque in the 14th century, it retains its original proportions. The spare interior contrasts with its illustrious Istanbul namesake.

activities.text: Walk up to the castle via Kale Caddesi from the town center – about 700 m. Vize is designated Cittaslow (slow city label); the old wooden houses in the castle quarter merit the stroll.

days_plan.itin_title: Istanbul to Varna: Strandja and Black Sea Coast in 10 Days

visits.text: Called the Midas Tumulus, it actually shelters the king's father—the dating of the 1957 excavations places the construction around 740 BC, too early for Midas himself. What is certain: the MM tumulus rises to 53 meters, its burial chamber in pine and juniper is the world's oldest standing wooden building, and the site has been UNESCO-listed since 2023. A 70-meter tunnel descends to it, well lit.

Cross the road to reach the Gordion Museum, a small building that reconstructs the tomb and displays the Phrygian funerary furnishings recovered in situ. The archaeological site behind the museum reveals the citadel foundations and the Phrygian-Assyrian megalithic gate, still standing at over 10 meters. A few signs in English, bring a water bottle.

days_plan.itin_title: Central Anatolia: Phrygia, Konya, and the Caravanserais

visits.text: The façade of the Midas Monument emerges from the volcanic tuff cliff at 17 meters high: geometric panels, Phrygian inscription, and central niche where an effigy of Cybele was displayed during festivals. This 8th-century BC monument is the largest of its kind known. You have to tilt your head back to read the inscription—and even that takes five minutes.

Go behind the monument to climb up to the acropolis of the city: the stepped altar, traces of streets, and rock-cut tombs follow one another on the same stone. The Kırkgöz Kayalıkları formation, opposite, was carved in the Byzantine period into a network of galleries and rooms. Free access, marked trail.

activities.text: If you like walking, the Phrygian Way starts here and links the rock-cut sites of the region. A 2-hour round-trip section to Gerdekkaya is enough to see the fairy chimneys and two additional small Phrygian monuments. Hiking shoes recommended, irregular limestone terrain.

days_plan.itin_title: Central Anatolia: Phrygia, Konya, and the Caravanserais

visits.text: Seven lakes linked by wooden bridges—Yedigöller National Park is perhaps the most photogenic park in the western Black Sea. Declared a national park in 1965, it owes its name to seven basins—Büyükgöl, Deringöl, Séringöl, Nazlıgöl, Sazlıgöl, İncegöl, and Küçükgöl—formed by ancient landslides. Autumn (October-November) is peak season: beech and oak trees turn red and orange.

activities.text: Follow the main trail that passes each lake and returns in a 2 to 3-hour loop. Artisan bridges span the small waterfalls and fountains between basins. Camping is possible on developed areas (Nazlıgöl)—but without electricity. In winter, the road from Bolu is closed by snow.
